- Duration: 2 weeks active evaluation (Mon 2026-...

1) Team & kickoff: We can start on Mon 2026-03-08. I'll assign two engineers: Peter (cc'd) and Nina Park (nina.park@brightai.com). Can you add Nina to future threads?

Hi Samantha & Peter,

Thanks again for the call today — good alignment on the evaluation goals. Per our discussion, I put together a compact POC plan so BrightAI's engineering and product teams can validate whether Redwood meets your production latency and cost goals. Please review and flag anything missing.

Top-level proposal (attached: poc-success-criteria.xlsx):
- Duration: 2 weeks active evaluation (Mon 2026-03-08 to Fri 2026-03-19) with light steady-state week following.
- Scope: 1 critical flow (customer-facing chat), dataset subset (approx. 10k prompts), model candidate: redwood-7B-quant (we can swap to 13B if you want quality-first).
- Success criteria (quantified):
 - 95th pct t...

messages.subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline
messages.body:
Hi Aditya — thanks for the clear write-up. This looks good overall. A few quick items from our side: 

1) Team & kickoff: We can start on Mon 2026-03-08. I'll assign two engineers: Peter (cc'd) and Nina Park (nina.park@brightai.com). Can you add Nina to future threads?

2) Security: Our security team requires SOC2 evidence and the answers to a short questionnaire before we allow any VPC peering or connection. I'll send the questionnaire to Marcus today.

3) Blocking issue observed: when we attempted to spin up the sample harness in our staging account yesterday we hit an immediate dependency failure in your runtime image (glibc version mismatch). That prevented us from running the quick s...

cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Peter Holt <p.holt@brightai.com>
messages.subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline
messages.body:
Hi Samantha & team — appreciate the fast turnaround and the added regression requirement (we agree on 0 critical failures).

On the dependency failure: Laura — can you confirm the root cause and quick mitigation? If glibc mismatch is accurate we can push a patched runtime image to a sandbox repo within 24 hours. Marcus — I'll need a quick confirmation on whether BrightAI wants the Hosted fallback (we can run the POC on Redwood Hosted to avoid the peering step) or if you prefer us to remediate in your staging account.

Immediate plan:
- Laura to reproduce dependency failure and publish a patched image (target: 2026-03-03 18:00 PST).
- If patch requires longer work, Redwood will provide a H...

to: Samantha Cole <samantha.cole@brightai.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline
messages.body:
Thanks all — I reproduced the staging failure this morning. Root cause: base runtime image we documented still references glibc 2.28; BrightAI staging uses a glibc 2.34-based host. The binary we ship was linked against the older ABI.

Mitigation plan (short/medium term):
- Short term (24 hours): build and publish a compatibility image (tag: rinf-compat-glibc-2.34) that bundles the correct runtime libs. I'll push to our sandbox registry and share pull URL. I expect this to be usable by your engineers within ~6 hours after I push.
- Medium term (by 2026-03-12): produce multi-arch / multi-glibc images and add integration tests in our CI to avoid regressions.

Escalation path:
- If the compat...

to: Samantha Cole <samantha.cole@brightai.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: POC kickoff — proposed success criteria & 2-week timeline
messages.body:
Hi Samantha — quick update on procurement & SOC2: 

1) SOC2 artifacts: I uploaded the SOC2 summary and evidence bundle to the HubSpot record and shared the Drive link here: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1a2b3c. Samantha, please confirm your security team can accept the bundle for the trial onboarding.

2) Procurement / billing: For a short 2-week POC we can run on Hosted with a temporary trial billing profile (no contract required). If you want Dedicated reserved capacity for the test, we will need a short order form (estimate 2–3 business days). For speed, I recommend Hosted sandbox to avoid procurement friction — we'll apply a credit cap and monthly invoicing terms if you convert afte...

body:
Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>: Hi Priya,

Thanks again for taking the time to talk yesterday. As discussed, we'd like to run a 75–90 minute session that covers:
- High-level architecture for Redwood Dedicated (VPC + network flow)
- SE-led walkthrough: integration steps, sample infra diagrams, and ramp timeline
- Security & compliance: SOC 2 artifacts, data segregation, audit logging
- Q&A with your engineering & procurement teams and next steps...

Priya Singh <priya.singh@lumendata.com>: Jordan —

Thanks for the clear agenda and materials. Unfortunately we've got two blockers: our staging cluster had a partial networking outage this morning (we're still stabilizing) and our lead integration engineer, Carlos, is travelling through Friday and will be largely offline. Between those two things we won't be able to get productive value from the demo on Wed/Thu.

Could we push to ...

Best,
Priya

- Typical 95th and 99th percentile request rate (rps) for inference and embeddings by hour (weekday vs weekend). If you can attach a 7-day export, that’s great.

messages.subject: Dedicated commissioning window & initial data request
messages.body:
Hi Tara —

Thanks again for the helpful demo last week. To align our Dedicated onboarding timeline with your SRE window, can you confirm the following by EOD Tuesday so we can lock the commissioning slot: 

- Typical 95th and 99th percentile request rate (rps) for inference and embeddings by hour (weekday vs weekend). If you can attach a 7-day export, that’s great.
- Expected peak concurrent sessions and average prompt length (tokens).
- Any large-batch workload windows (e.g., nightly indexing or bulk embed jobs).

We expect the safest commissioning window to be a four-hour maintenance period where we run acceptance traffic, failover tests, and an automated rollback rehearsal. Proposed da...

messages.subject: Re: Dedicated commissioning window & initial data request
messages.body:
Hi Jordan —

Thanks — attached is a quick export of last 7 days (odyssey_traffic_profile.csv) and our internal procurement timeline (procurement_timeline.pdf). Highlights: weekday peak 95th ~ 85 rps, 99th ~ 130 rps; average prompt length ~ 110 tokens. We do a nightly bulk embed job at 02:00 local (2–3 hrs) that can be deferred during commissioning.

May 20 evening looks best for us (after 18:00 PDT) — we can reserve a maintenance window 18:30–22:30. We do need a clear rollback SLA and a brief runbook in advance; can you share Redwood’s rollback criteria and the cutover play sequence?

Looping in procurement for any contract timing questions.

—
Tara Singh
SRE Lead, Odyssey Labs
tara@odyss...

to: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Dedicated commissioning window & initial data request
messages.body:
Jordan / team —

Thanks for the export, Tara — that’s helpful. Quick notes from validation perspective: 

- Acceptance profile: we should run a 1.5x headroom soak at 120 rps with the same prompt distribution to exercise batching and KV cache warmup.
- Regression checks: run 50 synthetic prompts that exercise the top three templates and compare token-level latency and correctness to hosted baseline.
- Rollback criteria (suggested): if p99 latency increases > 40% vs baseline for longer than 10 minutes AND error rate > 1% over a sustained 5-minute window, trigger automated rollback.

I’ll draft the commissioning checklist and a short runbook (will name commissioning-runbook-draft.md). Stepha...

cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Dedicated commissioning window & initial data request
messages.body:
Tara / team —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the proposed window. Based on what Kimberly outlined, here’s a concise MAP and timeline for May 20 (attached MAP_Odyssey_v1.xlsx):

- 18:00: Alerting and on-call staff assemble (Redwood + Odyssey SRE).
- 18:30: Switch 20% production traffic to Dedicated pool (smoke tests).
- 19:00: Expand to 60% and run 30-minute soak at scaled concurrency.
- 20:00: Full cutover (100% traffic) for 60 minutes of monitoring.
- 21:15: Rollback rehearsal (perform automated rollback to Hosted in <5 minutes).
- 21:45: Post-cutover stabilization and cost reconciliation handoff.

Runbook & rollback: Kimberly’s rollback thresholds look good. Our automated rollback...

messages.subject: Re: Dedicated commissioning window & initial data request
messages.body:
Jordan / team —

Confirming May 20 18:30–22:30 PDT works for Odyssey. Procurement will sign the MAP — please send DocuSign to procurement@odysseylabs.com and cc me. We do want SOC2 evidence prior to the window — procurement will review the pack early next week.

Two small asks before commissioning: 
1) Can you add a concrete rollback checklist step for data retention/kv-cache invalidation so our compliance lead can sign off?
2) Can Redwood run the 1.5x soak as a dry run in staging on May 13 to verify orchestration timing?

If you can confirm both, we’ll proceed with the DocuSign routing.

Thanks —
Tara
SRE Lead, Odyssey Labs

Attachments: none

messages.subject: Trial planning — acceptance criteria & MAP outline
messages.body:
Hi Priya,

Thanks again for the demo last week. The team is aligned that we want to run a time-boxed trial (4 weeks) to validate integration with our Triage pipeline and confirm latency/recall thresholds before committing to Dedicated. Before we lock dates, procurement asked for a concise mutual action plan (MAP) that lists success criteria, stopgates, and who owns each acceptance test.

High level asks:
- Clear pass/fail criteria for the trial (technical & business).
- A short roster of acceptance tests (what we will run, inputs, expected outputs).
- Cutover triggers and rollback signals if the trial fails.

Can you share a suggested MAP + roster we can attach to the SOW? Ideally by EOD ...

messages.subject: Re: Trial planning — acceptance criteria & MAP outline
messages.body:
Thanks Alice — attaching a first-pass MAP and an acceptance-test roster (attached: trial-acceptance-roster.xlsx, redwood-map-draft.pdf). Below is the short-form we propose so procurement/security can review now.

Summary of trial (4 weeks):
- Scope: Integration of Redwood hosted inference with Mediq Triage (inbound API, prefix caching enabled).
- Primary objective: Validate clinical-response recall and production latency under representative traffic.

Proposed success criteria (pass = ALL must be met):
1) Functional correctness: >= 92% recall on the curated evaluation set (50K clinical prompts) measured by Mediq evaluation scripts.
2) Latency: 95th percentile end-to-end for 512-token requ...

messages.subject: Re: Trial planning — acceptance criteria & MAP outline
messages.body:
Priya — quick confirmation on tech items from my side so the acceptance roster is actionable:

- Latency test details: we'll use a replay harness that mirrors 1:1 request distribution for 72 hours and a synthetic stress run (90th/95th/99th). For the trial we should record both server-side inference latency and end-to-end (client->Redwood->client) numbers.
- KV/prefix cache: we recommend enabling prefix caching for the trial; note this changes throughput/latency profiles — documented in the attached XLSX.
- Telemetry needed from Mediq: sample traces (redaction applied) for 1000 representative requests and their expected outputs to seed the regression probes.
- Failure modes: define what co...

cc: Daniel Cho <daniel.cho@mediq.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Mediq Procurement <procurement@mediq.com>
messages.subject: Re: Trial planning — acceptance criteria & MAP outline
messages.body:
Thanks Priya & Marcus — this is exactly the level of detail procurement and security needed. A couple small asks from our side before we finalize the MAP appendix: 

1) Can we tighten the latency acceptance to 220ms 95p for the replay harness evidence? We can tolerate 240ms as an end-to-end production target but procurement wants a stricter replay metric.
2) For the functional threshold, Mediq will provide the evaluation script but would like to see the baseline scores on the first run so we can agree on pass/fail during the kickoff.

If those two items are marked as "pending evidence" in the MAP (with owners and timelines), procurement is comfortable moving forward. I'll have procurement...

title: HelioMed security discovery — next steps & containment/DR evidence
body:
Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>: Hi Sarah — thanks again for taking the demo earlier today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned on the compliance items you raised.

Recap (from our call)
- SOC 2: Redwood completed a SOC 2 Type II assessment in 2025; scope includes serving runtime, control plane, and console. Full assertion available on request.
- Data protection: Redwood supports GDPR controls; for HIPAA we offer Private deploy...

Sarah White <sarah.white@heliomed.com>: Rachel — thanks for the summary and the materials. A few clarifying items from our side before we kick off the questionnaire:

1) SOC 2: can you confirm whether the 2025 Type II report is a full report that we can receive under an NDA or if you provide a SOC 2 assertion only? We typically request the full report for our risk review.
2) HIPAA / BAA: HelioMed will need a BAA for any Protected Heal...

normalized.subject: HelioMed security discovery — next steps & containment/DR evidence

cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
messages.subject: HelioMed security discovery — next steps & containment/DR evidence
messages.body:
Hi Sarah — thanks again for taking the demo earlier today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned on the compliance items you raised.

Recap (from our call)
- SOC 2: Redwood completed a SOC 2 Type II assessment in 2025; scope includes serving runtime, control plane, and console. Full assertion available on request.
- Data protection: Redwood supports GDPR controls; for HIPAA we offer Private deployments and a BAA for customers that require it.
- IR / breach notif: we operate an IR playbook and can produce redacted incident timelines and notification templates.

What we can provide now (attached / links)

Thanks again for taking the demo today. Quick recap of the performance items we discussed so they’re captured for your decision team:

- Latency targets: p50 <= 80ms, p95 <= 350ms for short-chat queries (<= 256 tokens).
- Streaming/long-form: real-time token stream target p50 <= 30ms per token, p95 <= 120ms per token (we’ll validate with your 1k token use-case).
- Throughput & concurrency: steady-state ...

Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>: Tara —

Adding a few technical clarifications and the perf-harness link:

- The p95 numbers Laura listed assume a production-optimized shard (A100-class) with continuous batching disabled for low-latency routes. If you want lower median latency with fewer costly resources, we can enable aggressive KV caching and prefix caching at the cost of slightly higher p95 under burst.
- For streaming sema...

messages.subject: Recap & SLO checklist (post-demo)
messages.body:
Hi Tara —

Thanks again for taking the demo today. Quick recap of the performance items we discussed so they’re captured for your decision team:

- Latency targets: p50 <= 80ms, p95 <= 350ms for short-chat queries (<= 256 tokens).
- Streaming/long-form: real-time token stream target p50 <= 30ms per token, p95 <= 120ms per token (we’ll validate with your 1k token use-case).
- Throughput & concurrency: steady-state target 450 concurrent streams / 1800 r/s aggregate for baseline; burst capacity to 3k r/s for short spikes.
- Concurrency behavior: session affinity required for KV cache warmness; we recommended pinned workers for 200+ long-lived sessions.
- Fallback policy: automatic fallback t...

messages.subject: Re: Recap & SLO checklist (post-demo)
messages.body:
Tara —

Adding a few technical clarifications and the perf-harness link:

- The p95 numbers Laura listed assume a production-optimized shard (A100-class) with continuous batching disabled for low-latency routes. If you want lower median latency with fewer costly resources, we can enable aggressive KV caching and prefix caching at the cost of slightly higher p95 under burst.
- For streaming semantics: ensure the client supports incremental chunk-ACKs; the harness will surface per-chunk latencies.
- Concurrency: the 450 concurrent streams target is conservative based on your stated user concurrency pattern (2000 monthly active, 10% peak). If you expect higher tail concurrency, we recommend ...

messages.subject: Re: Recap & SLO checklist (post-demo)
messages.body:
Laura / Marcus —

Thanks for the clear write-up and the harness link. A couple of clarifying points from our side before we loop in procurement:

1) Decision criteria: our engineering lead asked us to confirm three pass/fail items —
 a) Sustained 95th latency <= 400ms under our medium workload (sample attached).
 b) No more than 2% error/abort rate during a 30-minute full-scale run.
 c) Stream fidelity: incremental tokens must arrive in order with no reordering artifacts for >= 95% of events.

2) PO timing: procurement indicated they need a workshop + short run from Redwood to sign — can you support a two-hour on-site (remote) run in week of 6/8?

We will upload sanitized prompt traces by...

messages.body:
Hi Irene,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Novum is evaluating Redwood's Optimize toolkit to reduce inference spend on our conversational assistants. Before we get legal involved, can you help clarify a couple of items so we can model economics on our side?

High-level asks:
- A baseline commercial example: commit levels, credits, and typical first-year discount bands for customers our size (~$12k/mo expected consumption pre-optimization).
- Quantization: which model families and precisions do you recommend for 8-bit/4-bit flows that still meet our quality bar? Any known tradeoffs for long-context dialogs (>4k tokens).
- Batching & caching: expected latency/throughput impact and a co...

messages.subject: Re: Optimize toolkit — pricing & quantization questions
messages.body:
Elena — thanks for the clear list. Quick answers below and I attached a short provisional SOW that maps the commercial examples to service levels. Happy to hop on a quick sync if you prefer.

1) Commercial example (ballpark):
- Trial: $10k in Optimize credits (apply to token units & Tooling advisor reports) for 30 days to validate with your prompt set.
- Entry reserved (monthly): $6k/mo commit --> equates to ~15% list discount + 500k toolkit credits/month.
- Mid commit: $18k/mo --> ~20% list discount + 2M credits/month + 24/7 support.
- Enterprise (annual): custom pricing, deeper discounts with multi-region private deployment.

2) Quantization guidance (short):
- Recommend starting with 8...

messages.subject: Re: Optimize toolkit — pricing & quantization questions
messages.body:
Elena — looping in technical notes and a tiny benchmark sample (attached). A few concrete items you can use for modeling: 

- Quantization: in our internal tests with Llama2-13B derivatives:
 * 8-bit (int8): token throughput improves ~1.8x vs FP16 with negligible BLEU/ROUGE changes on typical dialog prompts.
 * 4-bit (nf4/aq): throughput improves ~2.6x but you should validate on multi-turn coherence — we saw hallucination risk uptick on loosely constrained prompts.

- Long context (>4k): keep hot KV for the last ~1k tokens in-memory; compress older turns into embeddings and fetch as needed. That pattern preserves persona and reduces end-to-end compute ~20–35%.

- Batching: continuous batc...

messages.subject: Re: Optimize toolkit — pricing & quantization questions
messages.body:
Thanks Irene & Marcus — this is very helpful. A few follow-ups from our infra and finance teams: 

- Can you pin the proposed discount bands to list prices (we need a % relative to list for CFO review). The mid-commit example at 20% is promising — what triggers escalation to enterprise pricing?
- The $10k trial credits: will those cover the sandbox runs + the test batch Marcus mentioned, or do we need to provision additional infra?
- For the benchmark CSV: can Marcus run the same tests with our dialogue prompt set (I will share a 200 prompt sample via Drive) and return a delta table we can include in our evaluation?
- Legal asks: standard MSA + a 60‑day pilot SOW is fine. We will need a c...

 * Option B (Growth): $18k/month commitment (12-month contract) → 20% off list + 2M toolkit credits/month + standard support.
 * Option C (Enterprise): Annual commit (>$200k ARR) → custom discounting and private deployment options. Trigger to move to Enterp...

I've attached a short summary of requirements and our procurement checklist. Please confirm next steps and an estimated timeline for a redline.

Hi Samir — thanks for the details and the list.

Quick summary / next steps from our side:
- I'll draft an amendment to the MSA/Order Form that captures the added capacity, the adjusted monthly commitment, and a revised price table.
- Marcus will scope the SOW Appendix B edits with your engineering notes and propose SLA language.
- We'll route the draft to legal for redlines and then to your procurement for signature.

Target timeline: draft to you by EOD Tue 6/15, legal review Wed, aim for signatures by end of next week if there are no major open items. If the price delta requires finance approval, there may be a slight delay — we'll flag early.

Attached: Proposed_Amendment_OMN-98742_v1...

messages.subject: Internal: Draft amendment & SOW notes (OMN-98742)
messages.body:
Laura / Mina — tagging you both. I attached my redline of the amendment focusing on Appendix B (SOW) changes and a suggested updated pricing table. Key technical asks from OmniTech:
- 6x additional A100-equivalent GPUs, dedicated pool, start 2026-07-01.
- Throughput SLA: 99th pct < 250ms for sub-512 token requests; 95th pct for > 2048 tokens to be described as throughput-tested.
- KV cache: preserve cold-start latency behavior; allow prefix caching for deterministic workloads.
- Data retention: 90 days for ephemeral logs; PII to be customer-managed or hardened with KMS.

Commercial items to confirm: monthly committed tokens vs burst, whether we keep them on the same billing cadence, and w...

messages.subject: RE: Internal: Draft amendment & SOW notes (OMN-98742)
messages.body:
Thanks for the context. I reviewed the proposed amendment and have the following high-level legal items before we send externally: 

1) Indemnity and liability cap: the current MSA has a liability cap tied to annual spend. Increasing committed capacity materially may require recalibrating the cap or adding a carve-out for SOC/data breaches — propose we keep the cap but add a carve-out to avoid open exposure.
2) Data retention / PII: we should add explicit language that customer-managed PII is outside Redwood's control and require a shared responsibility paragraph; for logs, 90 days is OK but add an audit log retention clause and access SLA for compliance requests.
3) Price-tier reset: com...

Attached is Amendment v2 which incorporates the SOW Appendix B language (throughput SLA and KV cache notes), the 90-day log retention language, and the commercial table we discussed. Legal suggested a small carve-out to the liability cap for breach/forensic costs; we've called that out in section 7.2.

Summary of next steps:
- Please review the attached and let us know any redlines by EOD Thu 6/17.
- If there are no substantive legal changes, we will issue a DocuSign envelope for signature (links below).
- Expected effective date if signed within the week: 2026-07-01.

to: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, David Kim <david_kim@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya_rao@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Provisional lane request + priority clauses — Vega Health pilot
messages.body:
Thanks — adding technical input and mitigations.

Summary: we can support a provisional 4 GPU carve with the following constraints and recommended mitigations to meet low-latency expectations:
1) Model variant: recommend serving a quantized 16-bit variant (Q4_0) for pilot runs — reduces memory footprint and speeds cold starts. We'll need model onboarding work (1 dev, ~3 days).
2) Prewarm & KV cache: allocate prewarmed containers with persistent KV cache priming for top 3 prompt flows Vega provided (we should ask Jay for representative traces).
3) Scheduler flag: we can mark the slice as "pilot-priority" which prevents opportunistic preemption but does not block emergency system-level evic...

messages.subject: Re: Provisional lane request + priority clauses — Vega Health pilot
messages.body:
Appreciate the fast turnaround. Decision summary and next steps: 

1) Conditional approval: Ops will provision an ephemeral dedicated slice with a 4 GPU min for an initial 14-day pilot window starting Sep 1.
2) Scaling and priority: We accept the staged scale (4 -> 8 -> 12) with manual Ops-triggered bumps and a 72-hour automated rollback unless procurement confirms extension. The slice will be marked "pilot-priority" (per Marcus) — not absolute non-preemptible.
3) Billing & gating: Sales (Aditya) to include a pilot MOU with a partial prepayment (50% of estimated two-week infra run rate) and a DocuSign clause that invoice triggers the full carve. Aditya, please coordinate with Procurement ...

Proposed exec decision options (please pick):

- Egress/CIDR: ACCEPT as long as it does not conflict with infra HA requirements; infra to confi...

body:
Jamie Ross <jamie.ross@nutrifyhealth.com>: Hi Lauren,

We're starting to lock down our QBR/renewal week and want to minimize calendar churn for our execs. We have a mix of US West, Central EU, and APAC stakeholders — the ask is to compress the roadmap + tech demo into two contiguous, shorter blocks instead of three separate sessions.

Top priorities for us:
- Show the migration path and rollback story for private deployments
- See the cost/latency tradeoff...

lauren.bishop@redwood.com <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>: Hi Jamie — great summary, thanks.

We can do the compressed format and I agree it's the lowest-friction for execs. Proposed options (all PT):
- Option A: Wed 10:00–11:15 (PT) + Thu 14:00–15:15 (PT) — gives an APAC overlap in the Thu block
- Option B: Thu 09:00–10:15 (PT) + Thu 15:00–16:15 (PT) — single-day back-to-back with an ops buffer

I'll attach a quick timezone matrix ...

- We can support both Option A and Option B. Option A gives us a little breathing room between blocks to collect logs and refresh any live demo state.

- Backup: If APAC attendance drops, we can record the Thu block and schedule a 30-min follow-up with APAC-only stakeholders next week.

One operational ask: please confirm whether Nutrify wants a recorded canary of the private deploy migration (we typically run it in a staging slice, takes ~20 minutes). If yes, ...

messages.subject: Re: Nutrify renewal QBR — consolidating demo windows

Lauren — Option A works best for us. Wed 10:00–11:15 PT and Thu 14:00–15:15 PT.

Couple of follow-ups:
- Yes, please include the side-by-side cost snapshot for our token tiers (we'll share expected Q3 volumes in a separate note).
- We want the private deployment canary recorded — please schedule the dry run 48 hours before Wed's block. Priya, can you confirm procurement will be ready to e-sign if everything looks good?
- For the SRE drop-in, Thu 14:45–15:00 PT during the second block is fine.

Thanks — looking forward to the session. I'll share the finalized attendee list by EOD Monday.

Best,
Jamie
VP Product, Nutrify Health

messages.subject: Post-demo: model fit & JSON output reliability — next steps
messages.body:
Hi Sofia + Acme platform team,

Thanks again for your time this morning — really appreciate the thoughtful questions about structured output and reliability. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 

What we heard
- Primary goal: replace brittle rule-based extraction with a function-calling flow that returns strict JSON matching your schema, with automatic retries and an E2E monitoring signal for \"schema compliance rate.\"
- Non-goal for the eval: full canonical integration into production. We want a runbook and objective metrics to decide next steps (Dedicated capacity vs hosted).

Recommended models for the eval (initial):
- rw-infer-small-v2: lower latency, good for 1–...

messages.subject: Re: Post-demo: model fit & JSON output reliability — next steps
messages.body:
Marissa, thanks — this is great and aligns with where we want to go. A few clarifications from our side before we kick off the formal window: 

Critical requirements
- Strict JSON schema compliance for the \"order_event\" payload. Fields are validated downstream; malformed JSON causes a 1–2 hour manual rollback. We need to measure both syntactic compliance and semantic adherence (enums, types).
- Retries must be idempotent. If the model returns a malformed payload, the retry should return a corrected payload without creating duplicate downstream side effects.
- We need an automated classification for \"repairable\" vs \"unrepairable\" outputs so we can route to human review.

Acceptance t...

messages.subject: Fwd: Post-demo: model fit & JSON output reliability — next steps
messages.body:
Jordan / Ethan — forwarding Acme's clarifications. TL;DR: they need strict JSON compliance for the \"order_event\" payload, idempotent retries, and telemetry hooks. They want the run Mar 9–20 and the pass thresholds are in the original plan (schema compliance >=98%, retry success >=90%).

Attachments mentioned by Acme are included in the thread. My proposed kickoff: 30 minutes on Wed to align on runbook and sampling size. Please review and propose:
- Sampling size (Jordan): how many calls do we need to get statistically meaningful schema compliance numbers?
- Implementation approach (Ethan): what model variants + runtime knobs to exercise (temperature, top_p, sampling vs deterministic).

...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Post-demo: model fit & JSON output reliability — next steps
messages.body:
Thanks for the context — listing a pragmatic plan and a few technical notes below. I can own the eval automation and reporting.

Quick plan
1) Sampling: target 2,000 calls per model across the three prompt families (happy path, partial data, adversarial). That gives ~6k total calls; should be enough to estimate compliance at the percent level.
2) Function-calling enforcement: use the runtime function signature feature + a strict schema validator (jsonschema v4) in the post-processor. Any parse failure goes to a deterministic repair pass (see below).
3) Retry strategy: three-stage approach
 - Stage A: deterministic re-call with system prompt instructing \"output only JSON matching schema\"...

messages.subject: Re: Post-demo: model fit & JSON output reliability — next steps
messages.body:
Jordan / Marissa, this looks good — thanks for the clear plan. We confirm:

1) Eval window: Mar 9–20 (we'll allocate a small runner team).
2) Kickoff: please schedule the 30-minute alignment meeting next Tue (Mar 3) so we can go over sampling and telemetry. Time zones: PST works.
3) Acceptance criteria approved as written (schema comply >=98%, retry first-pass >=90%, latency target 95p <=450ms).

Security contact for SOC2 follow-up: Ana Gomez (ana.gomez@acmeretail.com). We'll share the security questionnaire after kickoff.

Looking forward to the run.

Regards,
Sofia

Hi Marissa,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. We're kicking off the private deployment prep for the control plane that will run in our VPC/on-prem colo. Before the security review next Thursday, the identity team asked for a concise list of integration expectations so we can align our IdP (Okta) and provisioning flow:

- SAML metadata (SP metadata or ACS/Entity ID you expect)
- Required SAML attributes/claims (email, sub, role, groups, mfa) and expected claim names
- SCIM provisioning endpoint + bearer header format; exact user schema we will push (displayName, givenName, familyName, emails, groups)
- Guidance on IAM role binding for the control plane control-plane service accounts a...

messages.subject: Re: Private control plane - IdP/SCIM integration details
messages.body:
Hi Beth — thanks for the context and the attachment.

High level: we support SP-initiated and IdP-initiated SSO, SCIM v2 provisioning (bearer token auth), and a role-mapping layer that can map SAML claims or SCIM group membership into control-plane RBAC roles. We've sketched a runbook and a checklist (attached) to get you to a working end-to-end test. Key bullets:

messages.body:
Soojin / team,

Attach: svc-logs-20260613.txt (sampling), and an aggregated error snapshot (finly_error_snapshot.zip).

Impact: our production inference is degraded (affecting live billing flows). We're throttling client traffic but still see ~10% request failures and high tail latency. Can Redwood immediately:
- confirm whether this is headroom exhaustion on the reserved GPU po...

messages.subject: Re: Sudden spike: 5xx and latency on our dedicated pool (Finly Prod)
messages.body:
Marissa / David / Olga,

On it — forwarding Finly's logs. Initial read: token queue depth jumped and KV cache miss rate spiked on their reserved pool (p4-dedicated-42). That pattern points to a headroom/throughput shortfall rather than a model-level regression. I need ops to confirm GPU utilization, queue lengths, and whether any noisy-tenant migration changed allocation windows in the last hour.

Immediate asks:
- Olga: please run a live check on p4-dedicated-42 (utilization, kernel stalls, evicted KV cache size) and post findings to SUP-9123.
- Marcus: can you pre-authorize a short (12-hour) routing of Finly traffic to hosted-inference fallback with burst billing? We'll also ask for a t...

messages.subject: Re: Sudden spike: 5xx and latency on our dedicated pool (Finly Prod)
messages.body:
Soji / team,

Runbook snapshot (live checks):
- p4-dedicated-42: GPU utilization steady at 98-100% across the last 30m; queue depth increased ~6x; KV cache eviction rate up 420% vs baseline; continuous batching windows widened -> longer tail latency.
- No kernel OOMs or driver faults observed. No scheduled maintenance.
- Canary routing for non-critical traffic shows hosted variant reduces median latency to 150ms.

Immediate mitigation executed:
- Enabled fast-fallback route policy for Finly: their critical route /v1/generate now routes to hosted-fallback pool for the next 2 hours (config id: route-finly-0623).
- Began a controlled throttling policy on low-priority batch jobs in the same a...

messages.subject: Re: Sudden spike: Finly headroom exhaustion — errors/latency and request for fallback
messages.body:
Liam / Finly team,

Thanks for the quick flag — and apologies for the impact. We have temporarily routing Finly's critical traffic to our hosted fallback for the last 20 minutes while we stabilize the reserved pool. That reduces tail latency (we'll keep monitoring).

To unblock runtime capacity while engineering scales the dedicated pool, Redwood can offer the following immediate options (please confirm which you prefer):
- Option A (fastest): Apply a 12-hour burst authorization (adds 2 p4 nodes to the reserved pool). We can put this into effect now; we will bill at burst rates and provide a one-time goodwill credit for the customer-facing impact. Requires Procurement confirmation (signat...

messages.subject: Re: Finly headroom exhaustion — exec summary and asks
messages.body:
Liam / Finly / team,

Executive summary and actions (post-triage):
- Root cause: sustained traffic burst + KV cache churn exhausted provisioned headroom on p4-dedicated-42; no software regressions identified.
- Mitigation taken: hosted fallback routing enabled (reduced median latency to ~160ms); low-priority batches throttled; additional telemetry added to SUP-9123.

Asks / approvals:
1) Finly: Please confirm whether you approve Option A (12-hour burst, 2 p4 nodes) or Option B (continue hosted fallback + KV cache tuning). If Option A, please sign the attached burst-authorization-form.pdf and we'll provision immediately.
2) Redwood execs: request approval for a courtesy credit equal to one...

messages.subject: Re: MarketPulse — follow-up: performance targets, streaming, and token limits
messages.body:
Thanks Sergio — great walkthrough. Short answers to your open questions: 

1) Prompt sizes: your guessed buckets are right for the majority. Rough split: 70% search-assist (user prompts ~40–160 tokens, outputs 80–200), 20% document summarization (inputs 2k–6k, outputs 400–900), 10% misc (short chat).

2) Streaming: search-assist must stream (typeahead / progressive answer). Summarization can be non-streaming; incremental progress display is nice-to-have but not required for v1.

3) Data residency: US-only for now; we operate out of AWS us-west-2.

We’re happy to proceed with the two POC profiles. For sizing: peak concurrent active users equates to ~120 concurrent streaming sessions; avera...

messages.subject: Re: MarketPulse — follow-up: performance targets, streaming, and token limits
messages.body:
Hi Kai / team — looping in some technical detail so expectations are concrete. High level tradeoffs and realistic estimates based on our runtime: 

TL;DR recommended profiles:
- Interactive streaming profile (for search-assist): optimize for low tail latency; recommend model variants quantized int8, persistent KV cache for short prompts, 95% target <= 250ms for first token, 95% token-step latency <= 40ms for subsequent tokens at 512-token context. Estimated GPU sizing: 2x A100 equivalent nodes per 60 concurrent streaming sessions (w/ moderate batching).
- Summarization batch profile (long context): prefer chunk+rerank or sliding-window context; latent throughput per request grows roughly ...

messages.subject: Re: MarketPulse — follow-up: performance targets, streaming, and token limits
messages.body:
Thanks Lauren — this is exactly the detail we need. A couple items before we kick off the POC: 

- We will prioritize streaming for search-assist. I confirmed with product: 65–75% of user-facing calls must be streamed.
- Attached in-line is our internal SLO doc (short) — we need p95 first-token <= 300ms for streaming interactions, and p95 end-to-end <= 4s for long-context summarization. That aligns pretty closely to your estimates; please flag if any of those are infeasible.
- We require US-only deployment and standard SOC-type controls (we’ll send a vendor security questionnaire soon).

If you can send the two POC configs and a recommended schedule (we prefer starting next Tuesday), we’l...

messages.subject: Re: Implementation timeline request — internal notes
messages.body:
Internal note (reply-all suppressed):

Couple of technical clarifications we should fold into the roadmap PDF before sending final:
- SAML: we need Helios staging IdP metadata + at least two test accounts in staging to validate role mapping and SCIM (if they plan to use SCIM for provisioning)
- Embedding migration: recommend incremental load + on-the-fly rehydration. Warm-up: dependent on corpus size — example: 10M docs will need ~36–48 hours of prioritized cache fill if we traffic 1% to canary; propose a short pre-seed job on Week 3 night window to accelerate
- Network: confirm whether Helios requires egress via specific NAT IPs — otherwise our hosted endpoints should be sufficient

I'll...

messages.subject: Re: Implementation timeline request — success metrics & canary ops
messages.body:
Hi Alyssa — adding CS input on success criteria and runbook ops. Thanks for the clear ask.

Success metrics (what we'll monitor during canary and ramp):
- Latency: mean and 95th/99th percentiles per model and per route (alert at 99th > 2x baseline)
- Cost: tokens per successful request and estimated monthly delta at projected traffic 
- Accuracy/regression: set of 50 smoke prompts from your product team for regression monitoring
- Error rates and model fallback frequency

Canary ops & support model:
- Canary sample size: start 1% for 72 hours; success -> 10% for 5 business days -> 50% for 2 business days -> full cutover
- Rollback: automated routing fallback to the previous model version ...

messages.subject: Re: Implementation timeline request — confirmation and next steps
messages.body:
Jordan / Marissa / Rishi — thanks for the thorough response and the attachments. Procurement reviewed the high-level timeline and asked for two things before sign-off: a brief SOC 2 evidence summary (redacted as needed) and a finalized cutover runbook with the escalation tree. We can commit to the June 12–13 weekend if you can provide the runbook by Monday EOD (US-PST).

Also attaching our internal maintenance blackout calendar and initial procurement PO template — procurement@heliosfin.com will push the next steps once the runbook and SOC summary are in.

I'll add the staging IdP metadata this afternoon and send test accounts. Thanks again; looking forward to the runbook.

Best,
Alyssa

messages.subject: Onboard Atlas-12B-Alpha — compatibility checks and pinning policy
messages.body:
Team,

We received a heads-up from OpenSourceModels about a new release (Atlas-12B-Alpha). They believe this variant will be a good fit for a number of latency-sensitive customers if we can get a compatible quantized variant and pin strategy in place. Can we run a quick feasibility sweep and produce a recommended pinning + fallback policy?

Goals:
- Confirm runtime compatibility (kernels, attention optimizations) for the -Alpha variant.
- Produce a suggested pin (model name + version string) and a fallback policy (e.g., fp16 fallback, conservative token limit).
- Outline perf targets and expected memory footprint for our Dedicated pools.
- Propose a canary rollout plan and estimated ETA f...

to: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Onboard Atlas-12B-Alpha — compatibility checks and pinning policy
messages.body:
Thanks Karthik — I'm happy to coordinate with Sophia on artifact delivery and licensing. A couple quick clarifying questions before I ping them:

1) Target SLOs? Do we have a latency or throughput budget for this customer segment (p90 token latency target)?
2) Cost envelope — are we aiming for a competitive per-token cost (meaning we should prioritize int8/4-bit quantized variants) or is correctness the priority (keep fp16)?

I'll get the artifacts and the model checksums from Sophia and drop them into the Drive link Karthik provided.

I attached an initial compat-check-matrix.csv where I started listing the test rows I think we should run (inference with kv-cache, long-context stability,...

to: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Onboard Atlas-12B-Alpha — compatibility checks and pinning policy
messages.body:
Quick tech note from infra:

- Initial look: Atlas-12B-Alpha uses grouped attention with rotary embeddings and an unusual kv layout compared to our existing Atlas-12B-v1. We may need a small kernel tweak to avoid an extra copy for long-context workloads.
- Memory: fp16 baseline looks to be ~24GB live for a 2048 context on a single A10G-like device; int8/4-bit quantization could reduce that to ~9-12GB depending on choice of quant method and act-order.
- Suggested temporary pin name for internal testing: atlas-12b-alpha@v1.0-quant-candidate (we can publish a canonical pin later).
- Fallback policy suggestion: try atlas-12b-alpha@v1.0-quant-candidate; if OOM or latency > p95 threshold, fallb...

messages.subject: Re: Onboard Atlas-12B-Alpha — compatibility checks and pinning policy
messages.body:
Hi Marissa / Karthik,

Thanks for the heads up. I've uploaded the artifacts to the Drive link and included a checksum file. Notes:
- Release name: Atlas-12B-Alpha (repo tag: alpha-2026-02-10)
- Variants we are shipping:
 * atlas-12b-alpha_fp16
 * atlas-12b-alpha_int8_q4
 * atlas-12b-alpha_int4_q2
- Known issues: int4 variant has shorter safe-context stability at >3k tokens (we call this out in the README).

Attached: atlas-12b-alpha-checksum.txt (text, 1KB). You can reach me for a short sync if you want to walk the kernel/kv layout — we can also provide a small sample binary for integration tests.

Best,
Sophia
OpenSourceModels — Ecosystem Eng

to: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Onboard Atlas-12B-Alpha — compatibility checks and pinning policy
messages.body:
Summary and next steps:

1) Marissa: confirm license/TOU and pull the final artifact links into Drive (target: Mon 02/23 EOB).
2) Dev: run the compatibility matrix tests (use the attached compat-check-template.xlsx and populate results). If you hit kernel copy overheads for long contexts, file an infra ticket (SUP-4321) and flag whether we need a kernel patch vs routing to a different instance type. Deliverable: test results and recommended pin string by Tue 02/24.
3) Lauren: prepare a canary runbook — include canary sizing, percentage rollout, auto-fallback policy, and rollback criteria. Target Tue 02/24 for runbook draft.

Proposed initial internal pin for testing: atlas-12b-alpha@v1.0-...

title: Hosted evaluation follow-up — capacity, burst windows, and quickstart plan
body:
Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.ai>: Hi Evan — thanks again for the demo earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we can size the Hosted evaluation correctly.

Recap from our call:
- Primary use case: checkout personalization + fraud signal enrichment (server-side calls from checkout microservice).
- Typical request: 150–400 tokens; peak promotional bursts last ~10–20 minutes.
- Current constraint: budget-conscious but need predictable latency...

Evan Ross <evan@nimblecart.com>: Alex — thanks for the clear summary. Answers inline below.

1) Burst sizing: the 450 RPS is aggregate observed at the ingress LB during Black Friday style promos. Concurrency to downstream microservice usually peaks ~120 concurrent connections; retries add up to the aggregate number.

2) Token lengths: roughly p50=120 tokens, p95=380 tokens, p99=820 tokens (checkout text + cart context + session features)....

messages.subject: Hosted evaluation follow-up — capacity, burst windows, and quickstart plan
messages.body:
Hi Evan — thanks again for the demo earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we can size the Hosted evaluation correctly.

Recap from our call:
- Primary use case: checkout personalization + fraud signal enrichment (server-side calls from checkout microservice).
- Typical request: 150–400 tokens; peak promotional bursts last ~10–20 minutes.
- Current constraint: budget-conscious but need predictable latency for checkout (<120ms p99 streaming where possible).

Key questions we flagged for Hosted vs Dedicated/Private qualification:
1) Peak burst sizing: you mentioned spikes to ~450 requests/sec for 8–12 minutes during promotions; can you confirm if that’s concurrent HTTP requests or ...

messages.subject: Re: Hosted evaluation follow-up — capacity, burst windows, and quickstart plan
messages.body:
Alex — thanks for the clear summary. Answers inline below.

1) Burst sizing: the 450 RPS is aggregate observed at the ingress LB during Black Friday style promos. Concurrency to downstream microservice usually peaks ~120 concurrent connections; retries add up to the aggregate number.

2) Token lengths: roughly p50=120 tokens, p95=380 tokens, p99=820 tokens (checkout text + cart context + session features). I can share an anonymized percentile export — attaching anonymized sample now (see nimblecart-traffic-sample.csv).

3) Data residency: for this evaluation we can use Hosted but logs that include PII must be redacted. For production we prefer VPC (Private) if long-term storage of any cus...

messages.subject: Re: Hosted evaluation follow-up — capacity, burst windows, and quickstart plan
messages.body:
Evan — perfect, thanks for the fast replies and the sample. I reviewed the percentiles you sent; a couple of notes and a concrete pilot proposal below.

Quick notes:
- With p99 around 820 tokens, batching behavior will vary; we’ll recommend small continuous batching on the streaming endpoint to avoid latency spikes.
- Given aggregate bursts ~450 RPS but concurrency ~120, Hosted sandbox with a burst policy is a good starting point. Dedicated tends to be better if sustained concurrency stays >200 for long stretches (cost predictability and reserved GPUs).
- For compliance: Hosted with explicit redaction rules + customer-managed KMS should satisfy pilot requirements; for production we can pr...

messages.subject: internal: NimbleCart Hosted pilot — request for pilot credits and KV support
messages.body:
Team — quick internal note following the NimbleCart thread. Evan confirmed they can run the Hosted pilot if we redacted PII and provide a burst window as discussed. Key asks:

- Pilot credits: can we approve up to $2k in pilot credits to cover token costs for the 2-week run? This helps their cost evaluation and speeds decisioning.
- KV/prefix support: can Solutions allocate 1 engineer for a 2-day config to tune prefix caching and streaming batching (target p99 <120ms)?
- Success criteria: p99 streaming latency <120ms, sustained baseline 200 RPS with burst to 500 RPS for 10m windows, and a cost/per-token report at end of pilot.

I’ve attached the quickstart checklist to the Drive folder an...

messages.subject: Hosted API latency SLO - measurement window question
messages.body:
Hi Priyom,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the Dedicated offer looks promising. Before we move to procurement, our infra team needs clarity on how Redwood measures p50 and p95 for the hosted API. Specifically:

- Do you measure p50/p95 on a rolling 1-minute, 5-minute, or longer window?
- Are there carveouts for known maintenance windows or large regional bursts?
- If we request a temporary exception during rollout, what evidence do you accept?

Attached is a short list of the evidence our compliance team prefers (latency histograms, sample traces, request-timestamps). Can you confirm what you can provide and whether we need a contract amendment for a temporary measurement carveout?...

messages.subject: Re: Hosted API latency SLO - measurement window question
messages.body:
Elena — thanks for the clear list, and really glad the demo resonated. Short answers up front: we can provide the evidence you listed, and temporary carveouts are supported but should be scoped in the deal (we normally attach an addendum). More detail below.

What we measure
- p50: measured as a 1-minute rolling quantile computed across successful token-response latencies (edge-to-edge for streamed requests).
- p95: measured as a 60-minute rolling quantile, with hourly aggregation used for contracted SLO enforcement.

Carveouts and exceptions
- We have three standard carveouts: scheduled maintenance (customer-notified windows), regional cloud provider incidents (transparent incident ids),...

messages.subject: Re: Hosted API latency SLO - measurement window question
messages.body:
Elena — quick commercial note from our side and a concrete proposal to unblock procurement.

Proposal (commercially scoped)
- Contracted SLOs: p50 <= 40ms (1-min rolling), p95 <= 420ms (60-min rolling), measured on successful token-response latencies for the hosted API in the primary region.
- Temporary onboarding exception: up to 48 hours per major version rollout or customer cutover, once per quarter, per region. Exception must be requested 72 hours in advance; we will require the 48-hour traffic/evidence package you mentioned.
- If you need a longer temporary exception (up to 7 days), we can approve that with a commercial concession (one-time service credit) and enhanced monitoring.

O...

messages.subject: Re: Hosted API latency SLO - measurement window question
messages.body:
Thanks, Marissa & Priyom — that structure is helpful. A couple quick clarifications from compliance/procurement: 

- For p95 measurement: will the 60-minute rolling quantile window include retries and 5xx responses, or do you exclude failed requests? Our regulator requires that retries be counted against the consumer-facing SLA.
- For the fallback model variant: we need the fallback behaviour enumerated in the addendum (which variant, expected quality delta, and how rollback is executed).

We can provide the 48-hour traffic sample by EOD tomorrow. We also need the evidence package in a redacted format (traces + histograms) — please confirm whether you can deliver that within 72 hours of r...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Hosted API latency SLO - measurement window question
messages.body:
Elena — appreciate the quick turnaround. Confirming next steps and timelines so procurement has something to attach to the PO.

What Redwood will deliver (within 72 hours of receiving your 48-hour sample):
- Redacted 1-min histograms and p50/p95 rolling-curves for the sample window.
- A CSV of sample request timestamps and status codes (we will include retry markers).
- Two representative redacted traces illustrating normal and fallback routing flows.
- Draft addendum language that enumerates the fallback model ("redwood-medium-latency-variant-v2"), expected quality delta (high-level note: higher perplexity on long-form, acceptable for transactional use), and rollback triggers.

Answers t...

messages.body:
Kimberly —

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. Legal at AsterCloud asked us to confirm a couple of public-facing bullets in the hosted API and Console copy before we sign the SOW. High level asks:

1) We need unambiguous language on subprocessors — can you confirm the list that will be published and how often it updates?
2) For data transfers, is the phrase "we may transfer" sufficient or do you use SCCs/adequacy language in the DPA?
3) They want a short snippet we can put in our vendor inventory that explains retention and whether Redwood uses any human reviewers for hosted API content.

I've attached AsterCloud's vendor intake template and a draft phrasing we think would work — would ...

to: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Michael Grant <michael_grant@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Fwd: Clarify hosted API privacy language before public release
messages.body:
Team — forwarding AsterCloud's request. Quick asks below, can each of you take a pass by EOD Tuesday?

- Priya: legal wording and confirmation around SCCs / transfers and whether the DPA redline we proposed covers their intake checklist.
- Michael: product / docs: two short public-facing snippets (one-sentence, two-sentence) that are conservative and accurate for Hosted API + Console about retention, subprocessors and human review.
- Marcus: infra/engineering: confirm subprocessor cadence (how often we publish updates) and whether any subprocessors could change between notices (i.e., we need a "may change" clause).

Attachments: I've uploaded our redline of the DPA and a draft privacy sni...

messages.subject: Re: High latency + throttles observed — affecting card-check endpoint
messages.body:
Quick diagnostic from infra:
- Confirmed elevated queue_depth on PayLane's dedicated pool node group (pool-id: dp-784).
- Observed increased KV-cache eviction rates starting 08:40 PT after a minor kernel rollout at 08:20. Evictions correlate with tokenized-session rehydration spikes.
- Routing engine attempted fallback to compatible variants but hit slot contention; reserved throughput floor requests were queued rather than prioritized.

Initial hypothesis: kernel micro-optimization changed cache eviction heuristics -> higher cache churn -> workload gets more cold-starts -> request-per-token increases -> queueing -> 429 cascade.

Immediate actions taken:
1) Rolled back the 08:20 kernel tw...

messages.subject: Re: High latency + throttles observed — affecting card-check endpoint
messages.body:
Elena — update: Irene pushed rollback and cache adjustment at ~09:28. We're seeing latency p95 fall from 1.9s -> 720ms and 429s drop sharply. That said, reserved throughput still occasionally queues during short spikes when KV rehydration bursts.

Given PayLane's transaction sensitivity and the contract reserved floor, I'm asking for two actions (internal and customer-visible):
- Internal (redwood): authorize a temporary burst authorization for PayLane for the next 6 hours (to be consumed if the pool hits headroom again) and schedule a targeted routing hotfix to ensure reserved flows bypass transient queue windows. 
- Customer (PayLane): propose a short maintenance window (20 min) to cuto...

messages.subject: Re: High latency + throttles observed — affecting card-check endpoint
messages.body:
Jordan / Irene — thanks for the rapid investigation and rollback. We approve the 20 minute maintenance cutover to the sibling pool — please proceed ASAP and let us know the precise start time so we can hold windows on our side.

Regarding burst credits: we accept temporary consumption as a remediation step provided we get a retrospective and transparent billing ledger for the burst usage. We'll need a post-incident report (root cause, fix timeline, regression tests) within 72 hours.

Please start the cutover and keep us updated on the ops bridge.

—Elena
Elena Ramos
Head of Engineering, PayLane

messages.subject: Clarify retention for streaming sessions and prompt storage
messages.body:
Hi Marcus,

Quick question from our security team about how Redwood treats streaming interactions. We have two specific asks: 1) while a streaming response is being generated, are intermediate fragments (partial token buffers) ever persisted to disk or logs by default? 2) do the original user prompts (the raw user input) get stored by default along with final outputs?

We're evaluating default retention windows and need concrete examples to include in our Data Processing Assessment. Can you outline typical default behavior for hosted Redwood API (non-dedicated) and note any exceptions (debug logs, forced-profile traces, or legal hold scenarios)?

Thanks,
Diane
Diane Schaefer
Head of Platf...

cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Clarify retention for streaming sessions and prompt storage
messages.body:
Hi Diane — thanks for the clear questions. High-level answers first, then a short set of examples below. Attaching a small capture-flow JSON and our retention matrix for reference.

Short answers:
- Intermediate streaming fragments: ephemeral by default. The serving runtime keeps an in-memory buffer for partial tokens and a short-lived write-ahead buffer for crash safety, but we do not persist streaming fragments to customer-visible logs unless crash diagnostics are enabled for a specific session (opt-in or on-demand via support ticket).
- Raw user prompts: final request payloads (the user-provided prompt) are stored by default in the request store for hosted API customers and are retaine...

messages.subject: Re: Clarify retention for streaming sessions and prompt storage
messages.body:
Diane — adding a short formal summary you can drop into your assessment.

Formal summary (recommended language):
"By default, Redwood's hosted API retains request payloads (user prompts) and final model outputs for a 30-day window to support customer debugging and analytics. Transient artifacts generated during streaming (intermediate token fragments and in-memory KV caches) are not persisted to long-term storage and are considered ephemeral. Exception paths (verbose tracing, crash post-mortem buffers, or contractual legal-hold extensions) are documented and available on request; these require explicit opt-in, support coordination, or contractual terms. Evidence of deletion and retention ...

messages.subject: Re: Clarify retention for streaming sessions and prompt storage
messages.body:
Kimberly / Marcus — this is perfect, thanks. Two quick follow-ups: 1) can you provide an example API call and the exact fields we should expect in an export for proof-of-deletion (we want to show auditors how to verify a purge), and 2) can you confirm the default 30-day window applies to embedded content (images or files attached to prompts) or are those treated differently?

If easier, a short export example and a statement that attachments follow output retention rules would be ideal.

Best,
Diane

[Attachment stubs referenced above: example_capture_flow.json, redwood_retention_matrix_v2.xlsx]

to: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: [URGENT] NovaHealth prod - model 3a p95 latency spike
messages.body:
Lauren / Vivek —

We observed a sustained p95 increase for NovaHealth on model_3a starting 09:03 PDT. Current p95 is 820ms (baseline ~320ms) across their primary US region for requests >512 tokens. Error rates are not elevated; token throughput and GPU utilization look normal, but tail latencies tripled after last model push (model_3a:v2026-03-20-19).

Attached: novahealth_model3a_p95_report_20260321.pdf and a trimmed log file novahealth_p95_spike-20260321.log. Quick findings:
- KV cache hit rates unchanged

messages.subject: Re: [URGENT] NovaHealth prod - model 3a p95 latency spike
messages.body:
Alex —

Thanks for flagging this. Quick note to acknowledge and apologise for the disruption. We’ve identified a regression associated with the v2026-03-20 model push and are proposing two immediate mitigations:

1) Short-term: authorize rollback to v2026-03-17 for NovaHealth traffic (expected resolution: p95 back to baseline within 3–7 min).
2) Mid-term: route NovaHealth to model_3a-quantized for long-context traffic while we investigate kernel selection and sequence-length handling.

Please confirm if you want us to proceed with the rollback now. If you approve, we’ll schedule the rollback immediately and follow with a post-mortem and proposed credits for the window of degraded SLO.

Ap...

messages.subject: Re: [URGENT] NovaHealth prod - model 3a p95 latency spike
messages.body:
Lauren —

Please do the rollback now. This is affecting customer-facing pages; we’re seeing timeouts on multi-step forms. ETA is important — can you share expected duration and whether there will be brief errors during the rollback window? Also want confirmation that no model weights will be lost and that historic logs remain available for our compliance team.

Thanks,
Alex Mercer
Head of Core Platforms, NovaHealth
ops@novahealth.com

Alex replied inline to the SRE note above.

messages.subject: RE: [URGENT] NovaHealth prod - model 3a p95 latency spike
messages.body:
We can roll back to v2026-03-17 for NovaHealth only (traffic-splitting by customer ID). Steps (short):
1) Pause autoscale for the customer slice (1 min).
2) Drain new requests for that slice and flip router to v2026-03-17 (expected <2 min); monitor p95 and 5xx.
3) Re-enable autoscale.

Risks: temporary increased latency for in-flight requests during drain (~30–90s) but overall p95 should drop to baseline. No model weights deleted; this is a routing change. We will keep logs and snapshots; compliance artifacts are preserved.

I have the runbook open (runbook-model3a-rollback.md) and can execute as soon as Lauren gives authorization. I will coordinate with Hannah to watch the nodes and with...

Marissa/Rishi — please coordinate the messaging and log a HubSpot ticket noting the SLO window. I’m authorizing a one-time SLO exception for the degraded window and will follow up with a credit proposal.

Lauren Bishop
Senior Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference

Forwarded for records: runbook-model3a-rollback.md (attached in SRE note above).

Next steps:
- We will run a joint post-mortem (30–45 min) with NovaHealth tomorrow 11AM PDT to review root cause and mitigation plan.

Thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from my side so we have a shared baseline and clear next steps:

Summary of what I heard on the call:
- Primary SLO: 95th percentile end-to-end response latency for streaming responses \u003c= 250ms (soft), 99.9p \u003c= 750ms (hard).
- Expected steady-state throughput: ~1,200 req/min (~20 rps) across a handful of front-end services.
- Typical con...

Samantha Li <samantha@finflow.io>: Hi Sergio —

Appreciate the recap — that looks accurate. A few clarifications from our side:

- Burst requirement: We need a best-effort guarantee to handle up to 500 concurrent streams for at least 5 minutes during a product push. If that can't be guaranteed, we need a documented fallback (e.g., reduced quality model variant or regional failover).
- Queueing: prefer short queueing (up to 8s) to avoid...

messages.subject: Autoscaling follow-up: call recap and a couple of clarifying questions
messages.body:
Hi Samantha / Devon —

Thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from my side so we have a shared baseline and clear next steps:

Summary of what I heard on the call:
- Primary SLO: 95th percentile end-to-end response latency for streaming responses \u003c= 250ms (soft), 99.9p \u003c= 750ms (hard).
- Expected steady-state throughput: ~1,200 req/min (~20 rps) across a handful of front-end services.
- Typical concurrency: 60–90 active concurrent streams during business hours; peak bursts up to ~450 concurrent for short windows (3–8 minutes) during product events.
- Streaming behavior: partial token streaming acceptable; clients can tolerate a few partial updates as long as first-byte lat...

messages.subject: Re: Autoscaling follow-up: call recap and a couple of clarifying questions
messages.body:
Hi Sergio —

Appreciate the recap — that looks accurate. A few clarifications from our side:

- Burst requirement: We need a best-effort guarantee to handle up to 500 concurrent streams for at least 5 minutes during a product push. If that can't be guaranteed, we need a documented fallback (e.g., reduced quality model variant or regional failover).
- Queueing: prefer short queueing (up to 8s) to avoid client-side timeouts; after that we prefer 429s so our front-end can surface an informative retry.
- Streaming: first-byte latency is the highest priority — if streaming partial tokens helps keep first bytes under the soft SLO, that's fine.

Can you confirm your expected scale-up window from...

to: Sergio Alvarez <sergio@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: RE: Autoscaling follow-up: call recap and a couple of clarifying questions
messages.body:
Sergio / team —

Following up with concrete numbers and a proposed approach after reviewing FinFlow's targets:

Scale behavior (measured & expected):
- Cold start (no warm pool): full worker initialization + model warmup averages ~110–140s depending on model size and quantization. First-byte can be delayed during that window.
- Minimal warm pool (pre-warmed runtimes, no model loaded): ~45–60s to be fully ready for high-throughput streaming; first-byte still impacted until model weights are loaded into GPU memory.
- Pre-loaded warm pool (model loaded into memory, weights pinned to GPUs): ~8–15s to accept streaming requests at low latency (this is what we recommend for aggressive first-byte...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.ai>

Mutual action plan (MAP) — proposed timeline:
1) Today: engineering note & attachments sent (slo_matrix_v1.xlsx, warm-pool-diagram.png) — done.
2) Tue 2026-03-17: Canary load test (30 min) — Redwood to run ramp to 500 concurrent for 5 minutes; FinFlow to provide representative sample prompts and the front-end test harness.
3) Wed 2026-03-18: Post-test review — analyze 95p/99.9p latencies, queue timeout behavior, and fallback effectiveness.
4) Week of 3/22: Production rollout plan (if test passes) — decide on warm-pool size (our baseline recommendation: 120 pre-loaded streams) and production autoscaler policy.

Act...

Redwood quick guidance (based on what you shared):
- Deployment mode: for a customer-facing recommendation endpoint with 4k peak qps and a hard 200ms p95 target, Dedicated is the simplest path to predictable latency. Hosted can work during evaluation but we'll likely recommend an initial Dedicated pool for predictable SLOs with autoscale/burst policies enabled. Private (VPC) is also possible once you ne...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: deploy-mode recap & dedicated capacity sizing asks
messages.body:
Quick sizing pass from my side (ballpark) — putting numbers so Opsync has something to work with in the workbook: 

Assumptions (please confirm):
- Peak qps: 4,000 requests/sec (burstable to 5k)
- Average tokens per request: 250 (prompt+completion)
- Batch window: 5–15ms continuous batching, KV caching for 65% of requests

Ballpark (conservative):
- Token TPS requirement: 4k * 250 = 1,000,000 token TPS
- Effective throughput per H100 (with batching & caching): ~350k token TPS -> ~3 H100s baseline
- Add 1–2 H100s for headroom and autoscale (4–5 total) depending on burst profile and failure domain requirements
- If you prefer A100 GPUs, plan +30–40% more GPUs for same throughput.

Concurren...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: deploy-mode recap & dedicated capacity sizing asks
messages.body:
Thanks Marissa and Aditya — this is very helpful. I filled out the top-level Workload inputs in the workbook and attached a small zipped sample traces.csv (10 rows) to the Drive link.

Answers inline:
- Token mix: median ~210 tokens, p95 ~480 tokens; we saw a lot of small completions (80–120 tokens) and a few heavy prompts at 500–600.
- Function/tool calling: yes, ~18% of requests call out to retrieval; those usually add 30–80ms depending on DB latency.
- Preferred region: us-west-2; procurement prefers A100 but engineering prefers H100 if cost allows.

I can do a 30-min sync Thu 10:00–10:30 or Fri 15:00–15:30 PT — Thu works best. In that sync I'd like you to:
- Walk through the provision...

messages.subject: Spike in inference latency — impacting nightly batch jobs
messages.body:
Hi Hannah,

We started seeing a ~4x increase in 95th percentile token latency across several of our model endpoints around 08:50 PT this morning. It coincides with a configuration push from your side (we saw a short spike in 503s). The nightly billing/claims job is now timing out and blocking downstream processing. Can you confirm whether any hotfixes or configuration changes were deployed from Redwood earlier today?

Please treat as P0 for us — our ops window is 10:00–13:00 and we may need a short maintenance window if you recommend a restart/rollback.

Thanks,
Sofia
Director of Platform Engineering
Acme Health

(attachments: none)

messages.subject: Re: Spike in inference latency — immediate investigation and mitigation
messages.body:
Sofia — thanks for the heads up and apologies for the impact. We are investigating now.

Summary of immediate actions: 
- I have paged the runtime on-call and Marcus is building a targeted hotfix to revert the config combo suspected to be the cause.
- Dylan is standing up a temporary routing rule to shift traffic to our stable pool in us-west-2 to reduce tail latency for your account.
- We will share a short maintenance window if we need to restart any workers. Expect an update within 30 minutes.

Proposed quick mitigation (if allowed): brief rolling restarts of affected pods (estimated 2–3 minutes per pod) + route shift. If you prefer no restarts, we will proceed with routing-only mitiga...

messages.subject: Re: Spike in inference latency — hotfix ready (v1)
messages.body:
Hannah / Dylan — prepping hotfix v1. Root cause notes: an automated quantization flag landed with the prior config push; under a narrow sequence-length distribution it forces a slower kernel path for certain attention patterns. I have a small patch that toggles the flag back and skips the kernel selection for affected models.

Plan:
1) Apply hotfix_v1.patch to canary pool (2 instances) — monitor 5m.
2) If canary good, apply rolling to remaining nodes in us-west-2.
3) If any regression, immediate rollback patch (already staged).

Estimated customer-visible impact: very short (rolling restarts ~30–90s per node), but I will sequence against your window.

Pushing patch now.

Attachments: hotf...

messages.subject: Re: Spike in inference latency — hotfix applied; short maintenance completed
messages.body:
Sofia — quick update: we applied hotfix_v1 to canaries at 09:45 PT; canaries looked stable and we rolled across us-west-2 between 09:50–09:58 PT. We executed the routing mitigation concurrently at 09:47 PT.

Observations: 95th percentile token latency for affected endpoints returned to baseline within ~3 minutes of full roll. We observed a small blip of 503s during the final pod restarts (09:52–09:55) but no failed customer jobs reported after 10:00 PT.

Ask: can you confirm your downstream batch jobs cleared? If you saw any failed files, please send the run IDs and timestamps so we can correlate and include in the postmortem.

We will follow up with a short postmortem and invite you to a...

messages.subject: Re: Spike in inference latency — confirmation and request for postmortem
messages.body:
Thanks Hannah — confirmation: the nightly billing job retried automatically and completed at 10:18 PT. We saw two job failures (run IDs: bill-20260522-a12, bill-20260522-a13) that required manual requeue; timestamps align with the 09:50–09:58 window.

We appreciate the quick response — please send the postmortem and include remediation timelines for preventing config-only deploys from flipping kernel selection flags without a canary. Also please send the calendar invite for the review meeting.

— Sofia

-----Original Message-----

messages.subject: Fwd: Spike in inference latency — scheduling postmortem
messages.body:
Dylan / Marcus — linking Sofia's confirmation. Please prepare a one-page timeline and proposed remediations. I created a short invite (postmortem-invite.ics) for 15:00 PT and a draft doc here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Qw_postmortem — add notes ASAP.

— Hannah
Senior Manager, Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference

Amelia —

Thank you for the heads up and the screenshots — I’m escalating now. I’m pulling in SRE and our engineering lead. We are opening a war-room and will provide minute-by-minute updates until we have stabilization.

Immediate steps we’re taking (next 30m):
- Triage with SRE to identify whether autoscaling or routing produced the surge.
- Validate model routing config and rollback any recent routing changes.
- Temporarily increase Dedicated capacity allocation for your account to mitigate backpressure.
- Capture detailed error logs and a Datadog link (sending below).

Point of contact: me (channel +1-415-555-0132, rachel.kim@redwood.com).
ETA for first technical update: 09:45 PST.

W...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: URGENT: Production latency + 5xx spike - Zenith Payments
messages.body:
Team —

Labeling this P0 and requesting immediate exec awareness. Stephanie, we need your sign-off for emergency capacity burst and to authorize credits if needed. Diego, please prioritize triage (SRE ownership) and loop in any kernel/runtime folks if it looks like KV-cache or batching contention.

Please attach the mitigation runbook (I’ve included a high-level doc) and surface any blockers. I want a short executive summary for the customer within 30-45 mins, and a separate internal RCA timeline.

Marcus
Marcus Lin
Head of Sales Operations | Redwood Inference

Attachments: mitigation_runbook.pdf

--- Internal tech 4/7 ---

to: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: URGENT: Production latency + 5xx spike - Zenith Payments
messages.body:
Quick diagnostic: initial signals point to a regression in the autoscaler metric ingester introduced in a rollout earlier this morning (tagged by infra). The metric stream for pipeline_dispatch_lat (used by the autoscaler) had a semantic mismatch; the scaler did not scale as expected for short, high-concurrency bursts. That produced queue build-up and a subsequent spike in token-generation timeouts -> 5xx.

Mitigation applied at 10:05 PST:
- Forced scale-up of dedicated pools for Zenith by +25% capacity.
- Inserted a temporary fallback policy to route traffic to the previous routing group that uses a slightly larger model instance (higher throughput).
- Reverted the metric ingestion chang...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Spike in latency and 5xxs on production endpoints (URGENT)
messages.body:
Amelia / Samir —

Status update: we identified an autoscaler metric regression and have pushed a targeted mitigation. We forced a +25% capacity increase for your dedicated pool and routed transient traffic to a higher-throughput path. Early signals show latency trending back to normal and 5xx rates declining.

Next steps/action items:
- Engineering to roll the hotfix to production at 10:40 PST (Diego).
- We’ll run a verification window for 30 minutes post-rollout; if green we’ll keep the temporary routing for an additional 1 hour to ensure stability.
- Postmortem and timeline to follow by EOD.

We understand the impact and will provide a formal exec summary. If it helps, we can schedule a...

messages.subject: Re: Spike in latency and 5xxs on production endpoints (URGENT)
messages.body:
Thanks, Rachel. Appreciate the fast response. Please include details on:
- An incident credit for the window where throughput dropped (we’ll need SKUs and numbers).
- A written postmortem with root cause, remediation, and timeline.

Impact note: we lost several live authorizations during a key shopping window; this affected merchant revenue. We need daily briefings until postmortem is complete.

Samir
Samir Nadeem
Head of Payments Operations, Zenith Payments

--- Internal close/update 7/7 ---

to: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.com>
