Final status: hotfix rolled at 10:40 PST, verification window passed by 11:20 PST. Latency and 5xxs returned to baseline; throughput normalized. No additional customer-impacting incidents since.

- Exec summary for Zenith leadership: Marcus & Rachel — 2026-06-01 EOD (sent).
- Follow-up SRE bridge and preventative playbook: Stephanie — schedule for 2026-06-03 10:00 PST.

Customer comms: I sent an executive note and confirmed daily briefings until the postmortem is delivered. Maris...

messages.subject: Spike in latency and 5xxs on production endpoints (URGENT)
messages.body:
Rachel —

We started seeing a large spike in latency on your /generate and /embed endpoints around 08:50 PST (approx). Our transactions are timing out and we’re seeing ~6% 5xx rate and throughput backpressure; authorizations are queuing. Can you confirm whether this is on your side or an issue with our routing?

Impact: our payment authorizations pipeline shows ~2% drop in throughput over the past 20m; we can’t take it for much longer — this is affecting live customers.

Can you please escalate and provide a point of contact right away? Screenshots attached.

—Amelia
Amelia Chu
Head of Integrations, Zenith Payments
amelia.chu@zenithpayments.com

Attachments: latency_snapshot_0601.png

---...

cc: Samir Nadeem <samir@zenithpayments.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Spike in latency and 5xxs on production endpoints (URGENT)
messages.body:
Amelia —

Thank you for the heads up and the screenshots — I’m escalating now. I’m pulling in SRE and our engineering lead. We are opening a war-room and will provide minute-by-minute updates until we have stabilization.

Immediate steps we’re taking (next 30m):
- Triage with SRE to identify whether autoscaling or routing produced the surge.
- Validate model routing config and rollback any recent routing changes.
- Temporarily increase Dedicated capacity allocation for your account to mitigate backpressure.
- Capture detailed error logs and a Datadog link (sending below).

Point of contact: me (channel +1-415-555-0132, rachel.kim@redwood.com).
ETA for first technical update: 09:45 PST.

W...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: URGENT: Production latency + 5xx spike - Zenith Payments
messages.body:
Team —

Labeling this P0 and requesting immediate exec awareness. Stephanie, we need your sign-off for emergency capacity burst and to authorize credits if needed. Diego, please prioritize triage (SRE ownership) and loop in any kernel/runtime folks if it looks like KV-cache or batching contention.

Please attach the mitigation runbook (I’ve included a high-level doc) and surface any blockers. I want a short executive summary for the customer within 30-45 mins, and a separate internal RCA timeline.

Marcus
Marcus Lin
Head of Sales Operations | Redwood Inference

Attachments: mitigation_runbook.pdf

--- Internal tech 4/7 ---

body:
Katie Summers <katie.summers@acme.com>: Hi Marissa,

Thanks for the quick intro call yesterday. As part of our RFP process we need a completed performance questionnaire describing Redwood's latency and throughput characteristics under the following test scenarios: single-request p99 at 128/512/1024 tokens, batch throughput vs batch size (1,4,8,16), sustained QPS at 95th percentile for 15-minute windows, and cold vs warm-start behavior for KV-cached chat...

marissa_cole@redwood.com: Team — forwarding Acme's perf questionnaire. Deadline request is EOD Nov 3 PT. Rishi / Connor: can you own the numbers and provide the spreadsheet + methodology draft? Lauren: please sanity-check the recommended Dedicated pool sizing and any SLAs language. Specific asks from Acme: p99 at 128/512/1024, batch throughput at batch sizes 1/4/8/16, sustained QPS 95th for 15-min windows, cold vs warm for KV cache...

Please include test methodology, hardware used (GPU type, memory), quantization settings, and whether numbers are from hosted, dedicated, or private deployments.

Attached is our perf questionnaire (Acme_RFP_Perf_Qs.pdf). Can you return responses and any supporting be...

Lauren: please sanity-check the recommended Dedicated pool sizing and any SLAs language. Specific asks from Acme: p99 at 128/512/1024, batch throughput at batch sizes 1/4/8/16, sustained QPS 95th for 15-min windows, cold vs warm for KV cache.

Important constraints: Acme is explicit that they may request a side-by-side run (hosted vs our Dedicated or their private deployment). Please mark all numbers with the environment used and include raw CSVs where possible. If any items require privileged test infra or longer runs, fla...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: Detailed performance questionnaire for RFP - Acme Financial
messages.body:
Marissa / team — draft responses attached (Redwood_perf_report_v1.xlsx) and a short methodology summary below. I focused on the gen-family open LLM variants we usually reference in RFPs (quantized 4-bit where noted). Quick clarifying question for Acme: do they expect numbers from Hosted (our multi-tenant), Redwood Dedicated (dedicated GPUs), or their Private (VPC) deployment? Side-by-side runs may change throughput by ~10-20% depending on network and tenant isolation.

High-level methodology (summary):
- Hardware: A100 80GB (Dedicated runs); NVIDIA H100 80GB for short warm-up comparisons; Hosted numbers are sample aggregates across multi-tenant pools (mixed G4/H100 in us-west-2).
- Sequen...

messages.subject: Re: Request: Detailed performance questionnaire for RFP - Acme Financial
messages.body:
Hi Katie — please find our completed questionnaire and supporting artifacts attached (Redwood_perf_report_v1.xlsx, perf_methodology_2025.pdf). Below is a short executive summary pulled from the attachment:

Executive summary:
- Provided environments: Hosted (multi-tenant aggregates), Redwood Dedicated (A100/H100 pools), and Private (VPC) estimated ranges. All figures include model and quantization tags.
- Tail latency (p99) examples: 70ms (A100, 128 tokens) to 420ms (A100, 1024 tokens). Hosted aggregates are higher due to mixed hardware.
- Throughput scaling: our continuous batching harness shows near-linear token/sec improvement up to batch size 16 for gen-family models; see sheet “batch...

messages.subject: Re: Request: Detailed performance questionnaire for RFP - Acme Financial
messages.body:
Marissa — thanks for the quick turnaround and the detailed attachments; I reviewed the XLS summary. A couple quick follow-ups:

1) Can Redwood run the side-by-side harness in Dedicated (option 2) and share the full CSVs and our exact prompt set? We want to include the results in our security/compliance packet.
2) For the Dedicated sizing recommendation (8 x A100 + 50% buffer), can you provide a short TCO note comparing that to a smaller H100 pool (e.g., 6 x H100) so we can present alternatives to infrastructure?
3) Do you have an internal runbook or audit log snippet showing how you capture per-token latency and p99 windows (we need this for the RFP evidence)?

On the side-by-side: please...

Thanks again for the demo today. Quick recap + a few clarifying questions so we can scope a focused perf validation run next week. I pulled the numbers we discussed into the attached perf_matrix.xlsx and annotated a suggested 3-phase test plan. Please confirm the items below so we can align the test harness and the Dedicated pool sizing.

Key asks / notes from our call:
- Latency SLOs: target P...

Samir Patel <samir@helioshealth.com>: Sofia — thanks for the recap and the rapid turnaround. A few clarifications below:

- Throughput / concurrency: the numbers I gave were conservative maxima during a product launch. For steady-state planning, assume 1,000 qps and ~2,500 concurrent active sessions; launch-day bursts could approach 8,000 concurrent.
- EU residency: must apply to all persisted user data and any PII/PHI. Telemetr...

messages.subject: Edge streaming qualification & geo-residency follow-up
messages.body:
Hi Samir / Priya —

Thanks again for the demo today. Quick recap + a few clarifying questions so we can scope a focused perf validation run next week. I pulled the numbers we discussed into the attached perf_matrix.xlsx and annotated a suggested 3-phase test plan. Please confirm the items below so we can align the test harness and the Dedicated pool sizing.

Key asks / notes from our call:
- Latency SLOs: target P95 token latency of 200–350ms for typical chat flows; want explicit targets per region (US / EU / APAC).
- Streaming: you require server-sent event streaming with per-token flushes (low-latency streaming) and graceful reconnection behavior.
- Throughput & concurrency: expected st...

messages.subject: Re: Edge streaming qualification & geo-residency follow-up
messages.body:
Sofia — thanks for the recap and the rapid turnaround. A few clarifications below:

- Throughput / concurrency: the numbers I gave were conservative maxima during a product launch. For steady-state planning, assume 1,000 qps and ~2,500 concurrent active sessions; launch-day bursts could approach 8,000 concurrent.
- EU residency: must apply to all persisted user data and any PII/PHI. Telemetry for performance can be sampled, but full request/response payloads must not leave the EU when tagged as EU.
- APAC targets: we'd like P95 end-to-end response time (from client request to final token) under 500ms for APAC customers. Per-token latency is useful for ops tuning, but the business requirem...

messages.subject: Re: Edge streaming qualification & geo-residency follow-up
messages.body:
Samir — appreciate the clarity, this helps a lot. Quick answers and an operational proposal below.

Residency / telemetry:
- We can ensure persisted request/response payloads and user profile storage for EU-tagged data remain in eu-central-1.
- Telemetry: by default we sample traces; for compliance we can enable full request logging for EU-tagged requests stored in EU buckets (audit-log extract attached).
- SOC2: attaching a short SOC2 summary (redwood-soc2-summary.pdf).

Latency & routing strategy:
- Routing: client IP geolocation -> preferred region routing (ap-southeast-1 / eu-central-1 / us-east-1).
- Fallback behavior: if preferred region reports degraded healthy capacity, traffic wi...

to: Samir Patel <samir@helioshealth.com>, Priya Das <priya.d@helioshealth.com>, Sofia Ivanova <sofia_ivanova@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Edge streaming qualification & geo-residency follow-up
messages.body:
Thanks all — great thread.

Operational next steps from Redwood:
- I'll book a 45-minute perf deep-dive with our infra and product leads for Wed 30 Jun at 10:00 PT (invite to follow).
- Action: Helios to confirm EU persistence scope and preferred validation week (reply to this thread).
- Redwood action: provision ap-southeast-1 and eu-central-1 temporary pools, share SOW draft and perf-test-plan prior to run.

Attachments: perf-test-plan.md, SOW_draft_HL-2026-058.pdf (cost estimate + provisional timeline).

If Helios confirms the EU persistence applies to request payloads, we will include an encrypted-in-region write path in the test and a short audit-log extract for your compliance team....

messages.subject: HealthBridge - DPA redline (runtime tooling / subprocessors)
messages.body:
Marcus — attaching our redline for the DPA. Two items we flagged that we need clarity on before legal can sign: 1) the runtime third-party tooling clause (they added a paragraph that would allow Redwood to route runtime requests to monitoring/observability vendors that may see request payload metadata) and 2) an expanded subprocessor list for analytics/telemetry exports.

Short ask: can you confirm whether those runtime routing paths include any PII or model input/outputs, and what controls (encryption/KMS, ephemeral keys, KV cache residency) you apply? We put suggested language in Exhibit C and highlighted an example carveout we need to accept if you cannot guarantee non-visibility.

Att...

cc: Olga Petrov <olga@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: HealthBridge - DPA redline (runtime tooling / subprocessors)
messages.body:
Thanks Sanjay — received and thanks for the clear highlights. I skimmed the redline and attached spreadsheet. I want to loop in product and security to confirm our exact runtime behavior before we propose edits back.

Olga / Karthik / Priya — quick context: HealthBridge is asking for stronger guarantees that runtime third-party tooling (monitoring, APM, analytics) will not have access to model inputs/outputs. Their redline proposes: (a) a ban on sending request payloads to third parties unless explicitly agreed, and (b) a broad telemetry subprocessor list in Exhibit C that includes our monitoring vendor and an analytics partner. They also request an explicit guarantee that KV cache snapsh...

messages.subject: Re: HealthBridge - DPA redline (runtime tooling / subprocessors)
messages.body:
Marcus / Sanjay — technical note from runtime/security operations below. I copied Karthik for product implications.

Summary:
- Observability: Our APM and metrics pipeline collects request-level metadata (request_id, timestamp, route, token_count, latency, error_code). We do NOT forward full request or response bodies to external monitoring vendors in the default pipeline.
- Sampling: We have a debug sampling path used for customer support that can capture deidentified prompts or short context when a customer explicitly enables debug logging; that path is gated behind an opt-in flag and access controls.
- KV cache: KV cache snapshots are stored encrypted at rest and are not exported to ex...

to: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Olga Petrov <olga@redwood.com>, Sanjay Mehta <sanjay.mehta@healthbridge.com>
messages.subject: Re: HealthBridge - DPA redline (runtime tooling / subprocessors)
messages.body:
Thanks for the clear breakdown. Product position and next steps below.

Position: we can accept the technical proposal Olga outlined with the following product guardrails:
- "Aggregated telemetry" = time-bucketed, route-level metrics (no per-request identifiers that would let HealthBridge or a subprocessor reconstruct request-level content). Examples: per-minute token_count_sum, error_rate_pct, avg_latency_ms.
- "Sampling" for debug: must be explicit customer opt-in from Console or API; samples stored only for 14 days by default and accessible to customer and authorized support staff; samples are flagged and searchable only by sample id (no cross-tenant correlation).
- Exhibit C: prune th...

messages.subject: Hardware choices for Dedicated capacity
messages.body:
Hi Marcus,

Thanks again for the demo last week. As we map the Dedicated deployment for Lumena's conversational and summarization features, procurement has asked for a short note on SKU tradeoffs so they can start internal approvals. High-level context:
- Mixed workload: ~40% short chat/LLM replies (<= 512 tokens), ~30% embeddings and semantic search, ~30% batch summarization jobs that sometimes use long context windows (up to 64k tokens).
- Concurrent steady-state QPS target: 160 short requests/s, 45 embedding calls/s (batchable), and nightly batch summarization that needs throughput rather than latency.
- Cost sensitivity: want to optimize for unit cost for embeddings and nightly jobs w...

messages.subject: Re: Hardware choices for Dedicated capacity
messages.body:
Simon — thanks for the clear breakdown. I put together a short brief (attached) and summarize the pragmatic recommendation below so you can paste into procurement. High-level TL;DR: use a mixed pool with L4s for cheap, high-throughput short-context traffic and embeddings; H100s reserved for large-context models and peak heavy-generation; A100s can be used as a middle ground if you need more VRAM per-slot without H100 pricing.

Key tradeoffs (short):
- Memory (OOM risk): H100 (80 GB HBM) > A100 (40/80 GB variants) > L4 (24 GB typical). If your 64k-token pipelines require large KV caches, prioritize H100 for those slots; otherwise A100 80GB is an acceptable fallback.
- Latency / throughput:...

messages.subject: Re: Hardware choices for Dedicated capacity
messages.body:
Marcus — this is great, thanks. I uploaded a slightly richer workload export (attached) with 90th/99th token counts and a rough concurrency profile; a couple clarifying items below before procurement: 

- For Phase 1 sizing you suggest 120 steady L4 QPS. Our internal QPS estimate is 160; the attached sheet includes hourly peaks. Can you update the workbook to show a conservative baseline (160 steady, 250 burst) and the H100 headroom required to keep tail latency under 300ms for chat?
- Can you include an estimate of monthly unit cost delta (approx % difference) for short chat on L4 vs A100-40 vs H100? Procurement wants a ballpark delta to evaluate TCO.
- Procurement also asked whether Red...

messages.subject: Fwd: Hardware choices for Dedicated capacity — Lumena (procurement ask)
messages.body:
Folks — looping you both in to finalize answers for Lumena procurement. Summary of asks from Simon: update sizing workbook (conservative baseline 160 steady / 250 burst), provide a simple % delta table of unit cost for short chat L4 vs A100-40 vs H100, and a procurement-friendly paragraph on lead times / cancellation / credits. Procurement deadline 2026-06-09.

Quick notes / recommended approach:
- Aditya: can you run the quick estimate for unit-cost deltas using our current pricing bands? Use these assumptions: 1) short chat avg 40 tokens per request, 2) typical batching for embeddings (batch size 8) distinct lane. Provide a range (low/nominal/high) rather than a single value.
- Lauren: ...

Specific asks from our side:
- A CSV that lists subprocessors, region of operation, and roles (monitoring, backup, operator, telemetry).
- Whether Redwood ever receives unencrypted model context for requests originating in a customer VPC if customer managed k...

Runtime KVs used by the model are encrypted under customer keys; operator access to decrypted tokens requires an explicit break‑glass workfl...

messages.subject: RE: PrivDep subprocessor map & keystore handling — legal notes
messages.body:
Team —

I've reviewed Sam's vendor questionnaire. A few legal clarifications we should capture in the draft: 

1) Subprocessor visibility: provide an explicit table of subprocessors by role and whether they have any cryptographic key material access. For ones that do, add a short justification and whether they are bound by a DPA addendum.

2) Data egress guarantees: for VPC private deployments we should commit to "no egress of plaintext customer content to Redwood systems" except under break‑glass. For on‑prem packaged installs, same plus a statement that updates/patches are delivered as signed packages; remote support only runs with customer approval.

3) Escrow: we can offer a keystore ...

messages.subject: Re: PrivDep subprocessor map & keystore handling — operational confirmation
messages.body:
Karthik / Rachel —

Confirmed on the operational side. Summary of how it works today for Private (VPC/on‑prem):

- Customer keys: supported via two modes — (A) Bring‑Your‑Own‑KMS (customer gives us a keystore endpoint in their VPC) or (B) Customer keystore appliance (on‑prem) with an HSM. In both modes we never persist unencrypted symmetric keys in Redwood control plane. Runtime artifacts (model KV/attention caches) are encrypted (envelope encryption) under the customer key.

- Operator access: break‑glass is a GRANT issued via the customer's identity provider with a signed approval token. Requests to unlock are logged locally and a copy of the approval token and audit log is pushed to th...

cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Soojin Lee <soojin_lee@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Private deploy questions — package + next steps
messages.body:
Sam / Ivan —

Thanks — attached is the Private‑scope package we discussed: the subprocessor map, EU transfer matrix, and the keystore escrow protocol draft. High level commitments: 

- For Private (VPC/on‑prem) deployments with customer‑managed keys, Redwood will not access plaintext customer request content except under a documented and auditable break‑glass event.
- Break‑glass requires two independent approvals (customer approver + Redwood Ops approver), an expiry window, and an automated export of decrypted session logs to a customer audit bucket.
- Subprocessors in the EU/EEA will be used for monitoring/ops; non‑EEA subprocessors will be covered by SCCs and called out in the CSV.

Ne...

Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. As we scope the EU pilot for MediPlus, our security team has asked for three items before the next procurement step: a recent SOC 2 report (Type 2), a filled SIG/CAIQ-style response matrix, and a simple VPC control matrix that shows private endpoint topology and tenancy boundaries for EU-only deployments.

Can you confirm timeline for providing the documents and whether we can get a short mapping that highlights which controls are addressed by your private deployment (VPC + KMS partitioning)? We\u2019d like to circulate to procurement by EOD Friday.

Best,
Harriet
Head of IT Risk, MediPlus

--
Attachment: request-checklist.pdf (1 page summary)

messages.subject: Re: Request: SOC 2 + SIG answers and VPC controls for EU pilot
messages.body:
Hi Harriet \u2014 thanks for the clear list. Short answers up front: yes, we can provide the SOC 2 Type 2 report and a completed SIG-style matrix, and we\u2019ll include a concise VPC control matrix that maps to the MediPlus requirements for EU-only hosting.

Plan/Timing:
- SOC 2 (redacted): attached PDF (will send official redacted copy via secure Drive link).
- SIG matrix: we\u2019ll attach the XLSX with per-question notes; Kimberly will own any follow-ups on items marked \"partial\".
- VPC control matrix: CSV attached showing private endpoint, region routing policy, KMS partition notes, and fallback behavior.

Next steps: Kimberly, can you review the VPC matrix and add any operator con...

messages.subject: Re: Request: SOC 2 + SIG answers and VPC controls for EU pilot
messages.body:
Hi all \u2014 uploaded the SIG/CAIQ responses and the VPC control matrix. A few callouts inside the matrix:

- Network topology: EU pilot uses dedicated VPCs in eu-west-1 with private endpoints. We include peering to a dedicated NAT/egress appliance in the same region; egress is restricted by customer-configured outbound rules.
- KMS separation: Customer keys are supported via a customer-controlled KMS (BYOK) or our dedicated tenant partitioning with separate CMKs. See row KMS-05 in the CSV for specifics.
- Operator contacts: listed column H with on-call rotation and SRE escalation playbook link.
- Partial controls: a small set of SIG items related to vendor-hosted logging retention are m...

messages.subject: Re: Request: SOC 2 + SIG answers and VPC controls for EU pilot
messages.body:
Hi Harriet \u2014 quick operational note to complement the docs:

- Failover behavior: primary serving in eu-west-1; automatic regional fallback to eu-central-1 only if customer policy allows cross-region failover. We default to \"no cross-region fallback\" for EU residency-sensitive deployments unless explicitly enabled in the Dedicated/Private onboarding form.
- KV/cache eviction: per-tenant KV caches are never egressed to other regions. That is enforced in runtime routing.
- Onboarding checklist: we will run a brief network validation (privatelink handshake + traceroute) during provisioning and provide a signed connectivity snapshot for procurement.

If you want a short one-page summar...

messages.subject: Post-call recap: token budget snapshot & service allocation
messages.body:
Claire — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and explicit asks so we can lock the MAP and pricing proposal: 

Call highlights (my take):
- AtlasAI expects heavy conversational usage for search-summarization and follow-on clarification flows (demo looked great).
- Initial pilot: 3 months, targeting production rollout by Q4.
- Preliminary throughput: ~1.0–1.5B tokens/month after week 1 ramp (you mentioned 1.2B as a mid estimate).
- Model mix we discussed: chat generation (dominant), embeddings for retrieval, and a small rerank path for high-value queries.

Asks / confirmation items:
1) Final expected ramped monthly tokens (pick one): 800M | 1.2B | 1.5B — please confirm the number...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call recap: token budget snapshot & service allocation
messages.body:
Jordan — thanks for the thorough recap. Quick answers below: 

1) Token anchor: please use 1.2B tokens/month for the pricing scenarios. That is the most realistic steady-state after pilot.
2) Target $/1M: our internal stakeholders would prefer to see a base target of $850 per 1M tokens as the budgeted baseline; anything below $800 will be a win and anything above $1,100 stretches the ROI case.
3) Model split: the initial ramp will skew embeddings higher as we seed the retrieval layer, so adjust the split to:
 - Chat: 55%
 - Embeddings: 35%
 - Rerank: 10%

SLA: For pilot, hosted shared is acceptable. If we move to pilot->prod (after month 2), we'll want to revisit dedicated reserved capaci...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call recap: token budget snapshot & service allocation
messages.body:
Claire — perfect, thanks for confirming. Based on 1.2B tokens/month and your $850/1M target, we've sketched two scenarios (attached):

Scenario A — Hosted (shared):
- Assumes smart batching + global prefix caching; default model routing (chat:55 / embed:35 / rerank:10).
- Estimated blended unit price: $0.89 per 1K tokens (blended), which maps to ~$890 per 1M tokens.
- Estimated monthly cost: ~$1,068,000.
- Pros: fastest to start, no infra commitments.
- Cons: slightly higher unit cost; potential variability under extreme burst.

Scenario B — Dedicated reserve (3-month commit):
- Dedicated GPU pools with reserved throughput and quantization profile.

messages.subject: Re: Post-call recap: token budget snapshot & service allocation
messages.body:
Thanks team — quick technical + pricing notes from infra side: 

1) Quantization & model family: if AtlasAI is comfortable with an 8-bit quantized chat model for a portion of the chat traffic (we can route lower-confidence/less-sensitive queries to that variant), we can knock another ~10–12% off the blended unit cost in Scenario B. I modeled a split: 40% full-precision chat / 15% q8 chat / 35% embeddings / 10% rerank; that gets the blended price closer to your $850 target without a larger commit.

2) Dedicated reserve sizing: for 1.2B tokens/month with your mix, a small dedicated pool (2–3 A100-equivalent workers with burst) should cover steady-state throughput with headroom. I attached a...

messages.body:
Hi Priya,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. Medisys has one final set of asks before procurement can sign: they require (1) the option to deploy a version of Redwood Private in their on-prem environment behind their own firewall (air-gapped), and (2) a 120-day audit window during onboarding where their internal auditors can run live tests against access controls and log exports.

We understand standard policy restricts live audits; can Redwood accommodate a limited-window live audit and an on-prem packaging path? If so, procurement wants pricing and any contract addenda they should expect. I'm attaching the updated SOW and our SOC questionnaire notes for context.

Attachements: Medisy...

messages.subject: Fwd: Request: on-site hosting + audit window for Redwood Private
messages.body:
Marcus / Vivek — forwarding Medisys' asks. Summary: on-prem deployment (air-gapped) + 120-day live audit window during onboarding. They want pricing and contract language. They are a mid-enterprise healthcare customer, MRR potential ~55k/month, 12‑18 month commitment likely.

My read: engineering can ship an on-prem artifact but we need legal guardrails around data egress, KMS, and support scope. Security will balk at 120 days of live auditor access; 90 days is our usual compromise for on-prem carveouts. Procurement is pressing for a deeper discount if we allow live tests.

Proposed ask to Deal Desk / Execs: approve a one-time 15% contract-level discount and allow a 90-day live audit wind...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: on-site hosting + audit window for Redwood Private
messages.body:
+1 on Priya's framing. A few operational asks before we take to Laura for exec sign-off:
- Confirm SLA expectations for the on‑prem deployment (we should require a 12‑month minimum support addendum and limit SLO-backed commits for on‑prem installs).
- Clarify the scope of the auditors (systems, only auth/logs, or full synthetic traffic tests?).
- Recommend commercial concession: 20% discount if they commit 18 months and sign a support addendum; otherwise cap at 15% for 12 months.

If Laura is comfortable with the risk matrix, I'm prepared to approve up to 15% now. Anything above should be routed to finance + CEO.

-Marcus
Head of Sales, Enterprise
Redwood Inference

---

messages.subject: Exec decision requested: Medisys on-prem + audit window + pricing
messages.body:
Laura — quick summary and recommendation for exec sign-off.

What Medisys is asking:
- On‑prem packaging of Redwood Private (air‑gapped option), deployed and managed by Medisys IT.
- Live audit access during onboarding for up to 120 days (they will run internal controls and live auth/trace tests).
- Request for 20% discount tied to an 18‑month commitment; fallback asks for 15% on 12 months.

Risks and mitigations:
- Security: extended live audits increase exposure. Proposed mitigation: allow a 90‑day audit window (local auditors only, no persistent remote shells), supply redacted logs and scripted test harnesses, require KMS with customer‑managed keys, and sign an audit playbook restricti...

messages.subject: Re: Exec decision requested: Medisys on-prem + audit window + pricing
messages.body:
Priya — thanks for the concise framing and the redlines. Approved with conditions below:

1) Commercial: approve up to 15% discount for a 12‑month committed term. Approve 20% only with an 18‑month term + prepayment of 6 months (or equivalent security).
2) Security/Audit: cap the live audit window at 90 days. Auditors must be on‑site and signed into our audit playbook (no production query/traffic injection allowed); we will supply redacted logs and a vetted test harness for their checks.
3) On‑prem: allow delivery of an on‑prem install artifact restricted to a hardened runtime image; no persistent remote maintenance unless under separate support addendum and with customer‑managed KMS.
4) L...

messages.subject: Re: Request: on-site hosting + audit window for Redwood Private
messages.body:
Hi Elena — thank you for the clear ask and the SOW. Laura (VP, Enterprise) has approved a path forward with the following contract and operational conditions: 

- Commercial: up to 15% discount for a 12‑month committed term. 20% requires 18 months + prepayment of 6 months.
- Audit window: we can accommodate a live audit window capped at 90 days. Auditors must be on site and follow our audit playbook; Redwood will provide redacted logs and a validated test harness. No traffic injection into production systems.
- On‑prem deployment: we can provide an air‑gapped/hardened runtime artifact; persistent remote maintenance will require a separate paid support addendum and customer‑managed KMS.

N...

title: LatticePoint connector — synthesis review and pre-flight for sandbox testing
body:
Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>: Hi Maya,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — the connector looked promising. Before we allocate a private sandbox and schedule perf runs, can we do a short synthesis review to align on:

- Reference architecture (how your ingestion service talks to Redwood Private over VPC peering)
- Compatibility matrix (supported protocols, auth modes, expected payload sizes)
- Failure/fallback behavior (backpressure, replay,...

Maya Patel <maya@latticepoint.io>: Hi Alex — great running with you.

Quick replies inline:

- Reference architecture: We currently support a push model over HTTPS and an optional Kafka sink. For Redwood Private we can either push to an internal load balancer or publish to a VPC-to-VPC Kafka bridge. We prefer the HTTPS route for initial testing to simplify network config.
- Auth modes: OAuth2 client-credentials or mTLS — we can support b...

messages.body:
Hi Maya,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — the connector looked promising. Before we allocate a private sandbox and schedule perf runs, can we do a short synthesis review to align on:

- Reference architecture (how your ingestion service talks to Redwood Private over VPC peering)
- Compatibility matrix (supported protocols, auth modes, expected payload sizes)
- Failure/fallback behavior (backpressure, replay, retry semantics)
- SLA and security gating (data retention, encryption-in-transit & at-rest, audit logs)

Can you share a quick sketch or bullet list for the above? If it looks good we'll:
1) Stand up a 2-week private sandbox (dedicated capacity)
2) Run baseline latency and thro...

messages.subject: Re: LatticePoint connector — synthesis review and pre-flight for sandbox testing
messages.body:
Hi Alex — great running with you.

Quick replies inline:

- Reference architecture: We currently support a push model over HTTPS and an optional Kafka sink. For Redwood Private we can either push to an internal load balancer or publish to a VPC-to-VPC Kafka bridge. We prefer the HTTPS route for initial testing to simplify network config.
- Auth modes: OAuth2 client-credentials or mTLS — we can support both.
- Payloads: typical batch size 500–2,000 events; single event size < 64KB.
- Failure behavior: persistent retry with exponential backoff (up to 12h) + local durable queue for 72h; dead-letter to S3 for permanent failures.
- SLA asks: 99.9% ingestion availability, 200ms median per-reque...

messages.subject: Re: LatticePoint connector — synthesis review and pre-flight for sandbox testing
messages.body:
Perfect — thanks Maya. Looping in Sergio (SRE) and Kimberly (Security) to decide on the network pattern and minimal gating checklist.

Sergio/Kim: brief context below — LatticePoint can push via HTTPS or Kafka bridge into the customer's VPC. They can do mTLS or OAuth. They anticipate bursty batches up to ~2k events. They plan durable local queue + dead-letter into S3.

Questions we should resolve on the call:
- Preferred network pattern for private sandbox (VPC peering + internal LB vs. Kafka bridge).
- mTLS certificate exchange and rotation steps for the pilot.
- Minimal test plan to validate graceful degradation (injecting 500ms latency, dropping connections, simulating replay).
- Shoul...

messages.subject: Re: LatticePoint connector — synthesis review and pre-flight for sandbox testing
messages.body:
Alex / Maya — quick thoughts from SRE:

- For an initial pilot we'll take HTTPS to an internal gateway (simpler firewall rules, fits our test harness).
- We will provision a small dedicated pool (2x A100-equivalent) for the sandbox so results resemble production latency.
- Test plan: baseline throughput (steady 1k events/sec), burst tolerance (5k events over 30s), and backpressure behavior with 5% packet loss and added latency (250–1000ms).
- Replay validation: will run a replay bench against the durable queue and confirm idempotency for 5 concurrent replays.

Maya — can you confirm whether your HTTPS endpoint supports chunked transfer / keep-alive? Also please share any rate-limit header...

messages.subject: Re: LatticePoint connector — synthesis review and pre-flight for sandbox testing
messages.body:
Hi Maya,

Thanks for the details. From a security gating perspective we need the following before we allow any ingest into the private sandbox:

1) Signed partner MRSA (minimal risk & security agreement) — this is standard.
2) mTLS certs or client-credential OAuth client id + JWKS endpoint — confirm rotation policy.
3) Data classification statement for the test payloads (PII? STIX? or synthetic only).
4) Audit-log integration: we need event IDs surfaced back to our audit pipeline for ingestion events.
5) Confirmation that dead-letter S3 buckets are encrypted with customer-controlled KMS (or equivalent).

If you can provide the MRSA and a short note on auth and data class by EOD Mon (8th) ...

to: Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: LatticePoint connector — synthesis review and pre-flight for sandbox testing
messages.body:
Hi all — apologies for the delay, team was finalizing legal and infra checks. Quick update and attachments:

- MRSA: attached (latticepoint-mrsa-signed.pdf).
- Auth: we will use mTLS for the pilot; cert rotation window is 90 days, automated via our cert-rotation service. We also support OAuth2 as fallback.
- Data class: synthetic + redacted PII for smoke tests; we will not send live customer data.
- Endpoint: supports chunked transfer and keep-alive; rate-limit header present (X-LP-RateLimit: 1000/s) and we can turn down to 200/s in test config.

I've attached the compatibility matrix and ref-arch diagram. We're good for Tue 10:00 PT for the 30m sync — looking forward to the runbook and c...

messages.body:
Hi Jonas / Marissa,

Thanks for the quick onboarding last week — Nimbus is excited to move the pilot to a production-ready posture. A couple of audit/compliance constraints popped up from our security team while they reviewed the integration:

- They need a per-tenant audit slice (filtered export) for 90-day retention windows that excludes cross-tenant telemetry. This is for an external audit scope and must be extractable on-demand.
- We also need to schedule a short planned key-rotation blackout/maintenance window for our BYOK configuration. We prefer a guaranteed 20-minute window during which keys are rolled (HSM-backed) and traffic is routed to a fallback variant. We need to coordinate...

messages.subject: Fwd: Tenant audit slice & key rotation window — triage request
messages.body:
Team — forwarding a customer ask that touches product, infra, and ops. Key asks summarized below:

- Tenant-scoped audit export on demand (CSV/JSON) with 90-day retention carveout for Nimbus auditors.
- Scheduled BYOK/KMS rotation window (20 mins) that must:
 * guarantee fallback to a compatible variant during rotation,
 * emit webhook/Doc event when complete, and
 * provide signed evidence of rotation (audit log entry + key id change).

Questions I have for triage:
- Marissa / Product: Can we expose a filtered audit export endpoint without a significant infra change? Any perf risks?
- Marcus / Infra: How feasible is a hard 20-minute rotation window with a deterministic fallback and guara...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Tenant audit slice & key rotation window — triage request
messages.body:
Jonas — thanks for pulling this in. Quick responses and asks below.

Product perspective (short):
- Tenant-scoped export: We have an internal export job today for compliance teams, but it isn't filtered down to tenant-level in the public API. Building a per-tenant filter + access control layer is straightforward but will take ~3 sprints to harden (multi-tenant ACLs + perf tests).
- Short-term workaround: We can add a scoped export job in the infra-admin console that ops runs on behalf of the customer (manual for now) and include a signed manifest. That gives Nimbus what they need in <2 weeks.

Infra/KMS rotation:
- Marcus, please confirm, but my read is we can support a scheduled window. ...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Tenant audit slice & key rotation window — triage request
messages.body:
Thanks — technical notes from infra side:

1) Tenant-scoped audit slice
- We currently ingest audit events into a partitioned pipeline keyed by customer_id. Querying and exporting per-tenant is possible without data duplication; we'll need an export role with RBAC and a throttled job to avoid cluster pressure. Estimated effort: 8-12 engineer days to add an authenticated export API + CI tests.

2) KMS rotation window feasibility
- 20-minute window requirement is reasonable. Plan:
 a) Preflight: run compatibility check (30s), snapshot relevant caches (KV prefix), verify fallback model variant present.
 b) Switch: mark tenant routing to fallback variant at the LB layer (1-2s), perform KMS ro...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Tenant audit slice & key rotation window — triage request
messages.body:
Jonas / team — quick compliance note from ops/security:

- Auditor acceptance: Most auditors accept a signed manifest (timestamped JWS) plus a hash of the exported dataset. If Nimbus requires HSM-level signing, we need to route the signing call to their KMS or produce an HSM-attested envelope. Clarify whether they need Redwood-signed evidence or HSM-signed from their key.
- Temporary evidence path: we can produce Redwood-signed manifests and push the export to a pre-authorized Nimbus S3 bucket. That will likely satisfy 3rd-party auditors for short-term evidence.
- On the KV/cache risk: for very large session states we should add a monitoring alarm that fails the rotation and marks the ten...

messages.subject: Re: Tenant audit slice & key rotation window — triage request
messages.body:
Elena / Daniel — thank you for the clear ask and the spec. Next steps / mutual action plan (MAP):

1) Short-term (ops) — owner: Marissa Cole — deliverable: manual tenant-scoped export + signed manifest pushed to Nimbus S3. ETA: 2026-05-04.
2) Infra POC — owner: Marcus Lin — deliverable: export API POC (auth + throttle) + rotation preflight flow prototype. ETA: 2026-05-01.
3) Rotation playbook — owner: Grace O'Connor — deliverable: documented rotation playbook (fallback behavior, staged rotation criteria, evidence format). ETA: 2026-05-03.
4) Follow-up: 30-min planning call to review MAP and schedule rotation date. Proposed slots: Mon 2026-05-04 09:00-09:30 PT or Tue 2026-05-05 16:00-16:30...

Kimberly —

Thanks for the notes and the sheet. Filling it out now and attaching a short sample of last week's traffic (traffic_sample.csv) so you have real numbers to look at. Quick answers inline below: 

- TPS: steady-state ~12 TPS, peak bursts up to ~120 TPS (10–15 minute bursts).

Attached earlier: 20260528-sizing_qs.xlsx and traffic_sample.csv

If we can get a 1–2 slide PDF with SKU recs (rough cost and rationale) by EOD, I’ll forward to their procurement.

Thanks —
Kim

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Questionnaire: regional recovery expectations and RTO/RPO
messages.body:
Hi all — draft answers below, and I've attached the runbook + RTO/RPO matrix. Kept it concise as requested; happy to expand any item.

1) RTO / RPO (hosted API):
- Typical RTO (regional outage, automatic routing to healthy region): 10–20 minutes for control-plane DNS and routing convergence; 20–45 minutes to reach pre-failure latency percentiles once caches warm.
- Worst-case RTO (large model families cold-starts / model rehydration): up to 3 hours for cold model load in a region without pre-warmed artifacts.
- RPO: customer metadata and config are replicated across regions with sub-minute propagation for control-plane state (near-zero RPO for configuration). For in-flight inference reque...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Questionnaire: regional recovery expectations and RTO/RPO
messages.body:
Samantha — thanks for the clear list. Please see our numbered responses below and the attached artifacts (runbook and RTO/RPO matrix) for more detail. Short answers are provided first with a one-line pointer to where we document evidence.

1) RTO / RPO (summary):
- Hosted API typical RTO: ~10–20 minutes for routing; 20–45 minutes to fully warm caches. Worst-case (cold rehydration for very large models): up to 3 hours.
- Dedicated (reserved pools) RTO: 5–15 minutes when warm-standby is enabled; 30–90 minutes if new model weights must be loaded.
- RPO: control-plane metadata — near-zero (sub-minute replication). In-flight requests — best-effort; recommend idempotent client retries. Evidence...

2) Can operator actions (breakglass, rotation, emergency access) be made ephemeral and fully lo...

Hi Jasmine,

Thanks for the demo last week — the private deployment looks promising. Before we proceed to procurement we have three specific asks from InfoSec and Legal: 

1) Who holds the customer master key (CMK) in a VPC/on‑prem scenario? We need to understand if Redwood ever has a key that can decrypt customer data.
2) Can operator actions (breakglass, rotation, emergency access) be made ephemeral and fully logged without retaining plaintext transfer logs?
3) Cross‑border routing: if part of the control plane is in another region for redundancy, what data (if any) crosses jurisdictions?

I've attached our consolidated questionnaire (betahealth-questions.xlsx).

Can your team propose a...

messages.subject: Re: Private deploy questions: operator access and key custody
messages.body:
Claire — thanks for the clear list. I circulated your questionnaire to Product Security and the Deploy team and sketched a focused approach (attached: access-fence-proposal.pdf) we think would meet BetaHealth's requirements without heavy operational overhead. High level: 

- BYOK model by default: customer supplies CMK and Redwood never stores an unencrypted copy. We support KMS integrations (customer KMS or HSM appliance) and an escrow option only on explicit, contractually limited request.
- Ephemeral operator keys: operator actions that require elevated access are performed using short‑lived ephemeral service keys derived by a split‑knowledge protocol — operator control plane never hol...

messages.subject: Re: Private deploy questions: operator access and key custody
messages.body:
Claire / Jasmine — quick technical clarifications from Infra & Security (Marcus here added a couple of implementation constraints in line):

- Ephemeral operator keys: we use an HSM‑backed key‑wrap where the CMK (customer) never leaves the customer's KMS in plaintext. For operator tasks that require unwrapping, we require multi‑party attestation: (a) customer service account signs an approval token, (b) Redwood operator identity performs the job using a one‑time key issued by an internal ephemeral CA. The CA keys are short‑lived (15min) and logged.
- Audit logs: we can separate two audit streams — (1) operator activity (who/when/action) with redacted payload hashes, (2) forensic logs avai...

messages.subject: Re: Private deploy questions: operator access and key custody
messages.body:
Claire — thanks for the quick review and the questions. Summarizing agreed proposal and action items (for the DPA annex / procurement):

1) Default posture: BYOK (customer KMS) — Redwood does not retain any customer plaintext CMK.
2) Operator access: ephemeral operator keys with split‑approval; operator audit stream kept; forensic payloads only released under documented MAU/escrow process.
3) Audit retention: full forensic logs are not stored by default; we will surface redacted operator activity logs for 180 days. If BetaHealth requests escrowed forensic logs, the DPA annex must specify legal triggers and retention window (we will provide an option, not default).
4) Cross‑border routing:...

messages.subject: Re: Private deploy questions: operator access and key custody
messages.body:
Claire / Jasmine — quick technical clarifications from Infra & Security (Marcus here added a couple of implementation constraints in line):

- Ephemeral operator keys: we use an HSM‑backed key‑wrap where the CMK (customer) never leaves the customer's KMS in plaintext. For operator tasks that require unwrapping, we require multi‑party attestation: (a) customer service account signs an approval token, (b) Redwood operator identity performs the job using a one‑time key issued by an internal ephemeral CA. The CA keys are short‑lived (15min) and logged.
- Audit logs: we can separate two audit streams — (1) operator activity (who/when/action) with redacted payload hashes, (2) forensic logs avai...

Hi all — attaching the artifacts Alex requested. Summary below and files attached.

Summary (high level):
- SCC mapping: matrix that maps each SCC clause to Redwood controls and evidence (see redwood_scc_mapping_20260609.xlsx). Rows include: data categories, lawful basis, subprocessor controls, SCC clause ref.
- Data flow diagram: simplified diagram showing Hosted API path, optional Dedicated hop, and US-based asynchronous analytics pipeline (see redwood_data_flow_diagram_20260609.png).
- KMS & keys: we use AWS KMS for Hosted and Dedicated by default; for Private deployments customers can provide their own KMS (BYOK). We rotate asymmetric signing keys annually and symmetric data encryptio...

messages.body:
Mina / Alex — this is excellent, thank you. The artifacts look like they will meet our DPO's needs. Two quick follow-ups: 

1) Can you confirm the AES-256-GCM for at-rest is applied to customer data in backups as well?
2) Please include the brief engineering note about KV cache behavior (Rishi) — that will help with the DPIA writeup.

If you can confirm 1) and add 2) by end of day Thursday (2026-06-11) we'll incorporate into the packet and get back if we need a signed attestation.

Thanks again,
Julie Barnes
Head of Legal Operations
Oxbridge Health
---

- Primary goals: predictable latency for live features, safe background ingestion with dedup semantics, and clear quotas for cost forecasting.

Immediate technical notes / recommendations:
1) Auth & keys
 - We support API key...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: ByteFlux integration — auth, routing, retries & idempotency
messages.body:
Thanks Marissa — this is super helpful. A couple of follow-ups from our engineering lead: 

1) Long-running ingestion: we have a daily bulk feed that currently retries on any failure. What's the recommended pattern so we don't double-index when retries happen? Is Idempotency-Key per-document sufficient, or do you recommend batching with a job_id approach?

2) Reranker latency: for our search UI we need sub-150ms p95. Can the reranker with embedding candidates meet that without blowing cost? If not, should we do a hybrid (local cheap rerank + server-side final rerank)?

3) Quotas: procurement will want hard caps and contact points before we move to a contract. Can you send an initial sugge...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: ByteFlux integration — auth, routing, retries & idempotency
messages.body:
On Mon, Jun 29, 2026 at 4:05 PM Liam Carter <liam@byteflux.com> wrote:
> 1) Long-running ingestion: we have a daily bulk feed that currently retries on any failure. What's the recommended pattern so we don't double-index when retries happen? Is Idempotency-Key per-document sufficient, or do you recommend batching with a job_id approach?

Good questions — short answers and practical suggestions below.

Ingestion / dedup patterns:
- Best practice: use job-based async ingestion for large batches. Client uploads chunk(s) and receives a job_id. The job processing is server-side and idempotent by job_id; if the client retries the upload you get the same job_id and can query status.
- For stream...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: ByteFlux integration — auth, routing, retries & idempotency
messages.body:
Adding a few concrete SDK/config knobs that make implementation straightforward for ByteFlux engineers: 

SDK config suggestions (defaults we propose in the integration YAML):
- max_retries: 6 # for idempotent endpoints
- retry_backoff_base_ms: 250
- retry_backoff_cap_ms: 30000
- jitter: true
- idempotency_window_seconds: 86400 # 24h dedup window
- idempotency_enabled: true # opt-in per route
- async_job_chunk_size: 500 # docs per chunk for your pipeline (tunable)
- sparse_candidate_cutoff: 200 # for embedding -> reranker pipeline

Operational implementation notes:
- Rate limiting: tenant-level token bucket implemented in Redis with per-route keys. Use a small leaky bucket for interactive...

Kimberly, thanks — received the annex draft and the map. High-level feedback from our InfoSec:
- The PII pathing map is helpful but please annotate which subprocessors persist telemetry for >24 hours vs ephemeral caching only.
- We need explicit language in the annex about KMS custody and whether keys are customer-separable (they prefer a KMS enclave reference rather than generic KMS language).
- Onshore requirement: For the initial pilot, we can accept hybrid as long as the inference for PII-tagged records for US patients stays within US regions. We will require a line item in the SOW for region-lock options if we scale.

Procurement is ready to open DocuSign once Jordan's SSQ tab and a ...

To move fast, we sketched a short checklist and a preferred rollout runway attached (HB_onboarding_checklist_v1.xlsx). High-level asks:
- Confirm the JSON schema contract we should use for initial delivery (sample schema attached: hb_json_schema_v1.json).

messages.body:
Emily — thanks for the clear backlog. Friday 10:00 AM PST works for us; I scheduled a 45-minute block and invited the people below. Quick notes before the kickoff so we can use the time for decisions, not discovery: 

1) Scope split (what we'll aim to ship in phase 1):
 - Streaming: server-side server-sent events (SSE) for low-latency token streaming to your UI; prefix caching enabled.
 - Function/tool calling: declarative schema + invocation framework; we'll propose an idempotent retry pattern for external tool calls.
 - Structured outputs: agreed root-level JSON envelope per your schema; we'll propose a versioning strategy (v1/v2) to avoid runtime mismatch.

2) Data & retention: Redwood...

messages.subject: Re: Conversion corridor: enablement matrix & engineering kickoff
messages.body:
Emily — thanks for the checklist; Ben thanks for the MAP. Ahead of our 10:00 AM slot I'm dropping a concrete implementation matrix and a minimal schema compatibility guide so engineering can pair on a versioned contract instead of re-authoring during the call.

Key artifacts attached: hb_json_schema_v1.json (sample), plus a short implementation matrix below.

Implementation matrix (phase 1) — owner / acceptance / target:
- Streaming (SSE, continuous tokens): Lauren / Redwood runtime / acceptance = end-to-end token latency < 300ms on 99th pct / target = 3 weeks to prod parity
- Function calling (stateless tool invocation): HealthBridge + Redwood SE / contract test harness / acceptance = su...

1) Schema: Approved with two required additions (patient_id hashed string, and source_channel enum). We'll provide a short addendum by Monday.
2) Trace retention: We accept Redwood's default ephemeral token retention but request aggregated traces retained for 90 days; please include this in the SOW as a line item.
3) Timeline: Phase 1 target is 3 weeks for streaming parity and 2 weeks for tool-calling tests after the kickoff (aligned with your implementation matrix).
4) Contract: Emily will route a redline for a one-off SOW amendment to cover trace retention and a short SLA for high-p...

Hi Dylan — quick follow-up with the details you requested. Apologies for the long note; tried to be thorough.

- Pricing breakdown (annual 12mo term):
 * Base platform license (200 seats): $48,000

 Total (list): $81,500

- 24mo option: we can reduce to $75,000/year if you commit 24 months (applies the 10% retention credit up front).

to: Procurement Team <procurement@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
messages.subject: RE: PagerGuard renewal — summary & recommended path (PROC-4123)
messages.body:
Team — summary for approvals and next steps: 

- Vendor: PagerGuard (account lead Sarah Lee / Tim Ng).
- Current usage: ~160 active seats, we should renew for 180 seats to cover growth.
- Recommended term: 24 months at the $75k/year rate (saves ~8k vs 12mo).
- Required contract changes: add a signed SOC2 addendum, include SAML+SCIM, restrict data residency to US, and include explicit \"no auto-renew without procurement & finance signoff\" clause.
- Attachments (from vendor): Quote, SOW, Security Annex. Link to JIRA: https://redwood.atlassian.net/browse/PROC-4123

Marcus — can you confirm finance approval for $150k committed over 24 months and whether we should push for a 12% retention cre...

messages.subject: Re: PagerGuard renewal — summary & recommended path (PROC-4123)
messages.body:
Monica — finance is comfortable approving the $150k (24mo) commitment under two conditions: 

1) Include SLA credit table in the contract and a maximum annual cap on vendor liability tied to fees paid.
2) Add the explicit \"manual renewal\" / \"no auto-renew\" amendment and require a 60-day renewal notice instead of automatic renewal.

If PagerGuard accepts those, we will sign and expedite PO processing. Also push for the 12% retention credit — but willing to approve at 10% if the SLA + manual-renewal items are present.

Marcus
VP, Finance | Redwood Inference

to: Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: PagerGuard renewal — acceptance of amendments & final DocuSign
messages.body:
Thanks everyone — good news. PagerGuard can accept the two contract conditions Marcus listed: SLA credit table will be appended and we'll include the manual-renewal amendment with a 60-day notice requirement. We can also confirm the 12% retention credit if a 24mo commitment is signed before 2026-05-15.

I'll have Procurement issue an updated DocuSign packet with the revised SOW and the Security Annex included. Expect the packet in your inbox within 24 hours: https://docusign.net/prepare/packet/PA-789234

We'll also provide a temporary read-only auditor link to our SOC2 report for Evelyn. Let me know if there's anything else.

Regards,
Tim
Head of Customer Success | PagerGuard

This is impacting our customer-facing inference path. Can you confirm if this is on Redwood's side or if this is an integration/config change? messages.subject: Fwd: Production latency + 5xx spike (US-west) — urgent triage
messages.body:
Team —

Just received the note from BrightBridge. They report ~18% 5xx and elevated p95 latency. This is their production customer-facing route (high volume, pinned to dedicated pool). We need:
- Immediate confirmation of control-plane events or autoscaler activity (Logan)
- Kernel/runtime probe for GPU saturations & memory pressure (Marcus)
- Quick mitigation options (model fallback, reduced concurrency, soft-quotas) with customer-facing ETA (Marissa)

I will loop back to Sean within 30 minutes with an interim status and a 2-hour remediation ETA. Please mark any artifacts/attachments you produce with the run id.

This thread must remain within leadership/sales/ops (no external comms besi...

messages.subject: RE: Fwd: Production latency + 5xx spike (US-west) — quick findings
messages.body:
Quick summary from runtime side:
- No recent deploys to that dedicated pool. No control-plane maintenance windows.
- Observed sustained GPU utilization >96% on two nodes in pool-dedicated-west-02 starting 07:48 PST.
- KV cache miss rates spiked concurrently (likely invalidation storm from a cold prefix flush).
- Error signatures show OOM-retries on CUDA inference worker leading to transient 5xx.

Immediate actions I recommend (in order):
1) Drop concurrency on pool-dedicated-west-02 to 30% of current to reduce OOM churn (expected p95 improvement but throughput reduction).
2) Enable fallback routing to quantized LLM-qa-2q for non-critical routes (compatible but slightly lower quality).
3) ...

messages.subject: RE: Fwd: Production latency + 5xx spike (US-west) — runbook output
messages.body:
Runbook output (run id: rb-20260318-01):
- Autoscaler: no scale-up events; quota saturation hit on pool (concurrency cap).
- Node health: two nodes reporting CUDA OOM and kernel restart loops; drained 3 lower-util nodes prematurely.
- Network: no inter-region networking errors.
- Logs attached: stability_playback-08-12.txt (2MB text, sample traces)

Proposed immediate mitigation:
- Authorize me to apply policy: reduce concurrency to 30% on pool-dedicated-west-02 and enable fallback route for 40% of requests to model-llm-qa-2q.
- If authorized, I will also schedule a rolling replacement of the two faulty nodes (expected 12–18 min).

Impact: throughput will drop ~35% while p95 should drop t...

messages.subject: Re: Production latency + 5xx spike (US-west) — interim status and next steps
messages.body:
Sean —

Thank you for the traces. Short update and next steps:

What we see:
- Root cause appears to be GPU memory churn in the dedicated pool serving your pinned model. This caused a cascade of OOM retries and transient 5xx.

Immediate mitigations (being applied now):
- We are reducing concurrency on your dedicated pool by 70% to stabilize p95 (expect immediate reduction in errors).
- We will route ~40% of non-critical inference requests to a quantized model variant (slightly lower quality but faster) to preserve responsiveness for high-priority traffic.
- A rolling node replacement is in progress (ETA 15–20 minutes to clear kernel fragmentation).

Customer impact: you should see fewer 5...

messages.subject: Request: quota increase for Orlando launch
messages.body:
Hi Marissa,

We’re ramping up for our Orlando product launch on 2026-03-15 and expect a spike in inference traffic for the first 72 hours. Our initial estimate is ~1.2M tokens/hour peak across 8k concurrent sessions, tapering to ~300k tokens/hour after day three. Can Redwood provide a temporary quota/rate-limit increase for our Hosted account and confirm any per-minute burst constraints?

Attached is our traffic profile and an initial capacity plan. We’re on a tight timeline and need confirmation by EOD 2026-03-03 so we can finalize the go/no-go.

Thanks,
Amanda Lopez
Head of Integrations, GlowAI
---
Attachments: glowai_traffic_profile_mar2026.csv (text/csv), capacity_plan_orlando_v1.xlsx...

to: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Fwd: Request: quota increase for Orlando launch -- triage + asks
messages.body:
Team — forwarding GlowAI’s request. We need cross-functional confirmation on the following by EOD 2026-03-03:

1) Can we provision a temporary quota bump (tokens/hour and concurrent sessions) for GlowAI’s hosted account?
2) Any risk to shared pools or precedence for other launches that week?
3) Engineering: preferred approach for rate-limit enforcement (hard limits vs token-bucket burst) and whether we can add a grace policy for 72 hours.
4) Product/CS: what SLA/credit exposure if we make this exception?

Jordan — please coordinate the MAP with the customer and confirm dates. Laura — need your input on fallback policies (model routing to cheaper variants) that we can enable automatically ...

to: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: quota increase for Orlando launch -- triage + asks
messages.body:
Thanks for the heads-up. Quick assessment after a run against the upcoming capacity window: we can support a temporary bump but with the following constraints and operational steps:

- Proposed temporary limits for GlowAI (72h window):
 * Token throughput: increase from 150k -> 1.5M tokens/hour (soft enforcement at 1.2M, hard cut at 1.7M)
 * Concurrent sessions: allow up to 9k sessions (current plan supports 6k)
- Implementation: create a per-customer policy in the routing layer with burst token-bucket (burst capacity 2x base for first 15 minutes of each hour)
- Monitoring: enable per-route latency/cost alerts and an automatic rollback if error rate > 2.5% or p50 latency > 650ms for 10m w...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: quota increase for Orlando launch -- triage + asks
messages.body:
Amanda / GlowAI — thanks for the details. We can proceed with Vivek’s proposed numbers. I’ll share a short mutual action plan and get a signed exception approval from our ops team. Items for GlowAI to confirm by EOD:

- Final launch date (confirmed: 2026-03-15)
- Peak window(s) (they confirmed 14:00–17:00 ET on day 1)
- Contact for rapid incident comms (prefer Slack + phone)

Marissa — I’ll also open a short ticket with procurement for a one-off quota change and link to the MAP. Amanda, attaching the MAP draft in next email.

-Jordan
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference

to: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: quota increase for Orlando launch -- triage + asks
messages.body:
Quick product notes:

- Fallback: auto-route to quantized model B is acceptable for first 24 hours provided we label prediction confidence in responses. Product wants a teardown of any quality drift metrics post-launch (we’ll run evals against the agreed prompt set).
- Billing/SLA: this is an exception — any credits for sustained degradation will be calculated against the standard Hosted SLA. We’ll need CS to record acceptance in the MAP; no automatic crediting.
- Telemetry: please ensure we capture token-level and route-level logs for the 72h window and persist them to the customer’s retention bucket for 30 days.

Note: if GlowAI expects sustained traffic beyond the 72h window we should ...

cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Request: quota increase for Orlando launch — confirmation and next steps
messages.body:

- Window: 2026-03-15 00:00 UTC through 2026-03-18 00:00 UTC (72 hours)

- Fallback: during sustained pressure, traffic will auto-route to quantized model B for the first 24 hours (quality labels included).
- Monitoring & comms: we’ll add you to the incident Slack channel and have on-call SREs monitor token-level dashboards; Jorda...

- Scope: Inference for the search rerank + semantic recall pipeline against a 50k-doc test slice. Controlled traffic: 2k queries/day.
- Success criter...

Ben — quick notes to include before you send the baseline ask to Alex:
- Add explicit ask for SOC2 auditor contact or report receipt timeline (procurement will want it).
- Add a short list of what we consider PHI/PII in their dataset and a checklist for redaction steps (helps security team triage).

Director, Security & Compliance | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

messages.subject: Re: Post-demo: Proposed pilot sprint & acceptance checklist
messages.body:
Ben — thanks for the clear plan and charter. A few follow-ups from our side after an internal sync: 
- Can we shorten to a 4-week pilot? Our product sprint window is tight and we prefer a 2-week ramp + 2-week evaluation. Is that feasible without losing signal?
- Procurement will need an estimated bill for the pilot (even a T&M cap) and a copy of your SOC2 Type II evidence.
- Security: we require data isolation (VPC peering or dedicated bucket access) and an audit log slice for the first 48 hours of testing. Can you confirm those are possible in the private trial?
- Who will be the day-to-day contact for day-1 troubleshooting?

If you can answer the above and share an updated timeline, I c...

messages.body:
Alex — appreciate the quick response and the constraints. Short answers and an updated plan below.

1) 4-week pilot: feasible. Revised milestone plan: Week 0 (kickoff, access), Week 1 (ramp/deploy baseline), Weeks 2–3 (evaluation/load runs + tuning), Week 4 (final review & decision). We'll focus on a tighter test matrix to surface signal earlier.

2) Pilot estimate: we can offer the 4-week private pilot at a capped T&M not-to-exceed $12,000 (covers reserved infra + 2 dedicated infra hours/day for tuning). I'll share a short statement for procurement.

3) Security/data isolation: yes — private trial supports VPC peering and dedicated bucket access. Marissa will own the audit-log handoff. W...

messages.subject: SLA ask: availability & latency for batch-heavy model endpoints
messages.body:
Hi Karthik / Ben,

Thanks for the product walkthrough yesterday — the demos looked great. As we kick off procurement, we wanted to raise two SLA items that are must-haves for our compliance team before we can sign:

1) Availability baseline: we need clarity on the availability % that will be contractually guaranteed for the hosted endpoints used in production. Given our customer commitments, Stratify is targeting 99.99% if possible.

2) Latency SLO + batch carveout: most of our traffic is batched inference (business-hour, heavy tails). We understand batching can affect per-request latency during peak windows. We need explicit carveouts that: (a) allow higher latency thresholds for opt-in ...

to: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Fwd: SLA ask: availability & latency for batch-heavy model endpoints
messages.body:
Team — forwarding Stratify's ask. Quick thoughts before I prepare the redline draft for Elena:

- Availability baseline: 99.95% is our standard hosted SLA. 99.99% is achievable only for Dedicated / Reserved capacity with operational commitments. We should offer a dual-path: 99.95% for hosted, and 99.99% as an upsell for Dedicated (with a minimum commitment).

- Remedy tiers (proposal):
 * Availability >= 99.95%: no credit
 * 99.90% <= Availability < 99.95%: 10% service credit of monthly invoice
 * 99.00% <= Availability < 99.90%: 30% service credit
 * Availability < 99.00%: 50% service credit (cap at X months)

- Monthly cap: recommend max 30% of monthly invoice for hosted; we can offer 1...

to: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: SLA ask: availability & latency for batch-heavy model endpoints
messages.body:
Karthik — thanks for the outline. A couple constraints from pricing/legal to keep in the redline:

1) Credit caps: our standard hosted cap historically sits at 30% of the monthly invoice; legal prefers a per-calendar-month cumulative cap (not rolling). We should avoid open-ended caps for hosted. 10% for Dedicated seems reasonable.

2) Maintenance notice: 7 days is fine for planned maintenance. For emergency maintenance that affects availability we need an internal escalation clause and a requirement to provide a post-incident report — that reduces risk for the customer without increasing credit exposure.

3) Batched endpoints: I like the opt-in idea. Important to note the customer's respo...

to: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: SLA ask: availability & latency for batch-heavy model endpoints
messages.body:
Couple of product/ops edits to keep the template accurate for Stratify's workload: 

- For Dedicated customers (which Stratify is evaluating), we can offer 99.98% if they commit to a 12-month reserved pool and agreed scaling controls (burst windows, scheduled retrain blackout). That should get them closer to the 99.99 they asked for.

- Latency: define p50/p95/p99 buckets and make clear the remedy only applies to p95 for interactive endpoints. Batched endpoints opt-in will have latency targets expressed as a per-batch completion window (e.g., 5 minutes) and will be excluded from per-request latency credits.

- Scheduled maintenance: propose a maximum 6-hour per-month scheduled maintenance...

cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: SLA ask: availability & latency for batch-heavy model endpoints
messages.body:
Elena — thanks again for the clarity on requirements. We prepared a redline-friendly SLA attachment that addresses the availability envelope, monthly remedy tiers, and a specific "Batched Inference Exception" section that you can opt into. High-level summary below: 

- Hosted path: 99.95% availability baseline. Remedy tiers: 99.90–99.95 => 8% credit; 99.00–99.90 => 25% credit; <99.00 => 50% credit (per-calendar-month cap at 3x monthly invoice). Max monthly credit cap: 30% of monthly invoice.

- Dedicated path: 99.98% (with 12-month reservation + operational commitments). Credited cap reduced to 10% of monthly invoice.

- Scheduled maintenance carveout: up to 6 hours scheduled maintenance ...

messages.body:
Jordan — thanks for the recap and the materials. Priya and I reviewed the targets and the MAP looks reasonable as a starting point.

We can do Option B: Tue, May 19 — 1:00–1:30 PM PST. Priya will be the engineering lead in the meeting and I’ll join for the first 15 minutes to cover procurement/contract questions. Also copying our security reviewer, Maya (maya@novaai.com), who may join if you plan to surface SOC2/ML data handling details.

Attendees expected from Nova:

- Signer availability: CEO (R. Tan) is available 11/18 14:00-16:00 PT; CIO (M. Ellis) prefers 11/20 09:00-11:00 PT. Legal can sign after their final review (we expect completion by 11/17 COB).
- Start date: we prefer the staggered approach you included — soft start 2025-12-01 with full launch 2025-12-08 if the canary passes.
- Integration gating: we'll need two sandbox smoke tests by 11/25; can your team own the test plan?

If that all aligns, we'll route internally and confirm the exact signer names for DocuSign.

Claire
Head of Product Ops
Novus Retail

Also recommend we include a formal canary acceptance checklist inside the MAP (3 items: auth flow smoke, KV-caching validation, latency <120ms P95) so legal and engineering can sign off on day 14.

Priyom
Director, Customer Success
Redwood Inference

- Clarify the SLA rollback clause to specify a 48-hour remediation window before rollback.

Those changes are acceptable to Novus if incorporated as a short appendix pre-signature. We will confirm signer emails by 11/12 morning. Please include the data-retention appendix as a separate PDF in the DocuSign envelope. 

Regards,
Novus Retail Legal
(legal@novusretail.com)

messages.body:
Team — quick recap from today’s discovery with Medivance (Karen + Rajesh). High-level: strong product fit for claims triage (they care about latency and audit logs), interested in a 3-month pilot tied to a small production slice. Key notes below and asks where I need quick input.

What they told us
- Use case: automated triage for incoming claims, expected peak 45 RPS during business hours, average 6k tokens/day to start.
- Decision makers: Karen Wu (Sr Dir, Data & Analytics) is the technical sponsor, Rajesh Patel (IT procurement lead) will own procurement, and CFO (TBD) is the economic approver.
- Compliance: will require SOC 2 evidence, data retention policy, and network architecture di...

messages.body:
Hi Karen / Rajesh — thanks again for the time on Friday. Short recap and next steps from our side.

Recap
- Pilot scope: claims triage for inbound POC, ~50k tokens/month cap, 3-month pilot.
- Tech asks we can provide quickly: SOC 2 summary, data retention policy, architecture diagram, and a small SLA addendum for the pilot window.
- Business ask: team requested a pilot discount (roughly 35%) to shorten evaluation friction and confirm value.

Immediate asks (can we get these this week?)
1) Procurement contact: please confirm the right person at procurement (name + email) and the legal rep who would review a pilot addendum.
2) Economic approver: who is the CFO or exec who approves pilots wi...

Priya — thanks for the clear recap. Quick answers:
- Procurement lead: Rajesh Patel (I cc’d him above) — he’ll own the template and internal gating.
- Legal reviewer: procurement@medivance.com (internal counsel will triage).
- Economic approver: CFO (Anita Rao) — I’ll intro you if we proceed to contract.

Procurement notes:
- We will need SOC 2 type II evidence, a plain-English data retention summary, and an architecture/network diagram that shows where tokens and logs are stored. Can you attach those?
- For the pilot: procurement prefers to avoid changing SLA language, but will consider a timeboxed addendum if it’s limited and we agree clear rollback criteria.

I attached the template pr...

messages.subject: Internal: ask for pilot discount + temporary SLA concession approval (Medivance MDV-2381)
messages.body:
Ben / Laura — looping you in with the procurement asks from Medivance and requesting internal clearance on two items so we can proceed to the procurement walkthrough next week:

Proposed pilot terms (Priya’s recommendation)
- Length: 3 months
- Token cap: 50k tokens / month maximum (hard cap)
- Discount: 30% off list for the pilot (I recommend 30% instead of 35% to keep margin buffer)
- SLA: temporary concession to 99.5% for the pilot only, with clause to revert to standard SLA (99.9%) on full purchase; require notice and a maximum exposure cap of $X (TBD by finance).

Rationales
- Limits our exposure with a hard token cap and explicit rollback to standard SLA.
- Shorter pilot reduces pro...

to: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Internal: ask for pilot discount + temporary SLA concession approval (Medivance MDV-2381)
messages.body:
Thanks for the summary, Priya. I approve proceeding with the proposed pilot terms with the following conditions:

1) Hard token cap must be implemented in the billing pipeline and enforced at the API layer — no exceptions.
2) Finance (Ben), please define the maximum monetary exposure (dollar cap) for the SLA concession; keep it <= $5k for the pilot.
3) Legal (Laura), provide the 1-page pilot addendum and confirm we can produce SOC 2 summary + architecture diagram within 3 business days.
4) Sales: Priya, include a clause that pilot discount expires on signing of the full contract.

If all the above are confirmed, Priya — proceed to schedule the procurement walkthrough and share the draft a...
