messages.subject: Livechat stream latency spike (SF peak)
messages.body:
Hi team,

We started seeing severe delays in our livechat streaming responses around 09:15 PDT this morning. Instead of incremental tokens arriving every 200-300ms, we often see 2–6s gaps, and a few sessions stalled entirely (no more tokens after initial prompt). This is affecting our checkout support flow — conversion impact is visible.

What we observed:
- A3 instances serving us showed higher p95 latency; session traces attached (telemetry-sample-20260604.csv).
- Problem persisted across regions (SF primary, us-west-2 fallback) for ~30 minutes.

Can you confirm if there's an ongoing incident and what the recommended immediate mitigations are? We can change client-side behavior if you s...

messages.subject: Re: Livechat stream latency spike (SF peak)
messages.body:
Hi Samir — thanks for the report and the telemetry. We're looking into it now and have opened SUP-3287. Short update and immediate mitigations you can apply while we triage: 

Short-term mitigation recommendations (client-side):
1) Switch to non-streaming responses for high-priority checkout flows (set stream=false) — this avoids token-by-token dependence when the network path is experiencing stalls.
2) Reduce max_tokens by 20% for streaming sessions to shrink average token emission time.
3) Implement exponential backoff for reconnects (start 250ms, max 3s) and accept partial responses; we can fill any missing content via follow-up generation.
4) Use per-session routing to your us-west-2 ...

messages.subject: Re: Livechat stream latency spike (SF peak)
messages.body:
Quick ops note and what we changed:

What happened (summary):
- Around 09:12 PDT a recent rollout (model variant v3.1.7 with a new attention-kernel) caused degraded token emission performance on a subset of our A3 fleet in us-west-1 and us-west-2 edge shards. The degradation appears input-length sensitive and shows up as intermittent multi-second token gaps.

Immediate actions taken by Redwood:
- Rolled back v3.1.7 on affected pools at 09:25 PDT — this restored normal token cadence for the majority of sessions.
- Applied a short-term routing policy to prefer dedicated routing for CleverPay sessions while we complete a deeper kernel analysis.
- Throttled background batch jobs that were con...

messages.subject: Incident summary & next steps — CleverPay streaming latency 2026-06-04
messages.body:
Hi Samir / Priya — following up with the agreed post-incident summary and recommended remediation steps. Thank you for the detailed traces — they were instrumental.

Incident summary:
- Timeline: 09:12 PDT (deploy of v3.1.7) → 09:25 rollback → 09:40 majority recovery.
- Root cause: a performance regression in a new attention kernel under specific input-length distributions that amplified token emission jitter on a subset of edge shards.
- Impact: ~18% of streaming sessions for CleverPay experienced intermittent multi-second token gaps; session stall rate <1%.

What we did for you:
- Immediate rollback and routing preference to Dedicated pool for your livechat traffic.
- Temporary credit o...

title: Post-call: perf targets, regional routing & data residency for BrightLane PoC
body:
Elena Morales <elena@brightlane.ai>: Thanks for the time today, Karthik — good discussion. Quick recap from our side and the initial SLO targets we need to validate in a PoC: 

- Use case: real-time assistant embedded in our mobile app (voice -> tokenize -> streaming LM responses).
- Expected concurrent active sessions at peak: 200 (regionally distributed; US ~120, EU ~60, APAC ~20).
- Average request size: 80 tokens in, expected 150 tokens out (stre...

Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>: Elena — thanks for the clear summary and the SLO draft. Quick recap and a few clarifying items so engineering can size the PoC properly: 

1) Streaming latency: when you say "first token" targets, do you mean time from client audio chunk arrival at BrightLane -> first token visible to user, or from our API receive time? We should instrument both.
2) Model family: are you planning to use a 13B-c...

messages.subject: Intro + perf targets for BrightLane PoC
messages.body:
Thanks for the time today, Karthik — good discussion. Quick recap from our side and the initial SLO targets we need to validate in a PoC: 

- Use case: real-time assistant embedded in our mobile app (voice -> tokenize -> streaming LM responses).
- Expected concurrent active sessions at peak: 200 (regionally distributed; US ~120, EU ~60, APAC ~20).
- Average request size: 80 tokens in, expected 150 tokens out (streaming).
- Throughput target (aggregate): ~600 requests/minute (10 rps) sustained global; regional burst up to 50 rps in US.
- Latency targets (end-to-end, first token streaming): p50 < 60ms, p95 < 150ms, p99 < 350ms for US; EU targets can be ~1.5x US; APAC we accept slightly high...

messages.subject: Re: Intro + perf targets for BrightLane PoC
messages.body:
Elena — thanks for the clear summary and the SLO draft. Quick recap and a few clarifying items so engineering can size the PoC properly: 

1) Streaming latency: when you say "first token" targets, do you mean time from client audio chunk arrival at BrightLane -> first token visible to user, or from our API receive time? We should instrument both.
2) Model family: are you planning to use a 13B-class model (full LM) or a smaller, quantized variant for tail-sensitive workloads? Knowing this will shape per-instance throughput and memory footprint estimates.
3) KV/session caching: will sessions reuse past KV (i.e., multi-turn context), and average context length? You noted ~1–2 turns in mobile...

messages.subject: Re: Intro + perf targets for BrightLane PoC
messages.body:
Karthik / team — capturing engineering notes from my side: 

- For streaming-first-token, there are three measurable segments: client network -> our edge, edge -> model-server scheduling & KV lookup, and model response generation. We should timestamp at each boundary during tests.
- For throughput: 600 req/min global is light; the concurrency requirement (200 active sessions) is what determines instance sizing and batch/latency tradeoffs.
- Suggested PoC config: US (g4dn equivalent) x2 instances with continuous batching disabled for latency-critical path; EU (same) x1 instance pinned to EU region. If BrightLane wants stronger p99 we should consider reserved Dedicated capacity.
- Fallback ...

messages.subject: Re: Intro + perf targets for BrightLane PoC
messages.body:
Thanks — quick clarifications from BrightLane infra: 
- We will keep EU traffic within EU; our GDPR controls require EU customer data (including transient audio) never leave EU processing nodes.
- Session context: we keep last 2 turns, average 300 tokens total context for active sessions.
- For APAC: small percentage of users in Japan and Australia — if local nodes are not available we prefer to keep them in APAC, but routing to US is acceptable only if we have documented consent and a fallback policy.
- Timeline: procurement signs off late May; ideal PoC window is late April/early May.

We'll prepare IP allowlist and sample traffic patterns for Jordan.

—Tom

cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio_alvarez@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Intro + perf targets for BrightLane PoC
messages.body:
Appreciate the clarity, Tom/Elena. Internal plan and owners below: 

- Jordan: build perf harness + test plan for US and EU (APAC optional) — needs IP allowlist and sample payloads from BrightLane. Target: initial run within 5 business days after we receive artifacts.
- Marissa: commercial lead for the PoC (SOW, DPA, procurement cadence).
- Sergio: I'll loop you for Dedicated capacity sizing and pricing options for guaranteed p99 SLAs.
- Me: coordinate scheduling and capture any early legal requirements for EU data residency.

Next steps for BrightLane: send test payloads, IP allowlist, and a short list of peak-minute traffic windows you expect. We'll publish a short PoC SOW (attached) an...

messages.subject: Re: Intro + perf targets for BrightLane PoC
messages.body:
Team — agree with the plan. A couple of notes from sales/commercial: 

- I'll own the SOW + DPA cadence and will circulate a templated DPA that matches our EU residency controls. Expect a first draft by Wed.
- Given the p99 targets, propose we present a Dedicated Reserved option alongside a Hosted API PoC. Dedicated gives easier isolation and predictable performance for p99. Sergio, please prepare a conservative Dedicated sizing + rate card we can show BrightLane in the Monday sync.
- Deal details: provisional pricing and a PoC credit structure will be added to the HubSpot deal. Deal id BL-2026-042 created.

Marissa
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference

On Sun, Apr 11, 2026 at 8:05 AM Karthik...

messages.subject: QBR prep: April KPI synthesis + action items (latency, cost, uptime)
messages.body:
Team —

Attached is the April KPI dashboard export for PriorityHealth and a short synthesis of notable signals. We're consolidating for the QBR next Friday (05/08). Please review and add any blockers or suggested slides by EOD Monday so I can finalize the deck. High-level notes: 

- Latency: 95p latency increased ~18% week over week around 4/12–4/16. Initial look points to a routing change that increased cold-starts on dedicated pools.
- Cost: Token spend up 12% month over month; effective cost/token rose ~6% driven by re-generated completions on a small subset of high-retry prompts.
- Uptime: Service-level uptime for the account is 99.92% (monthly), two brief incidents during maintenance...

messages.subject: Re: QBR prep: April KPI synthesis + action items (latency, cost, uptime)
messages.body:
On Sat, May 2, 2026 at 9:12 AM Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Team —
> Attached is the April KPI dashboard export for PriorityHealth...

Quick read —
- I dug into the routing logs around 4/12–4/16. We had a policy change that started preferring the new lower-cost pool (dedicated-pool-3) for mid-length sequences. That pool has a smaller warm buffer and no pre-warm on scale events; result: higher cold-start tail.
- Recommendation: revert preference for the lower-cost pool for production routes serving PriorityHealth OR enable pre-warm for that pool for peak hours. Revert is fastest; pre-warm is more sustainable.

I'll prepare a short comparison export (latency by pool + cost ...

messages.subject: Re: QBR prep: April KPI synthesis + action items (latency, cost, uptime)
messages.body:
Thanks — attached a quick per-route cost export and a notes sheet (KPI_notes_20260503.xlsx). Highlights below: 

- Top 5 cost drivers for PriorityHealth: session-retry loop on the claims-reconciliation route (~28% of token spend), long-context embeddings use for historical search (~18%), high volume of short responses (amortized overhead), frequent streaming sessions (overhead + tokens), and a small number of large-batch reruns for testing.
- For the retry loop I flagged two prompts that re-generate on server-side validation; suggests a prompt-level fix or a client-side de-dup dedupe check.
- I added a suggested slide: "Cost by route (April) — proposed mitigations" with before/after cost ...

messages.subject: Re: QBR prep: April KPI synthesis + action items (latency, cost, uptime)
messages.body:
Evelyn —

Thanks for the dashboard. A couple of quick questions ahead of the QBR: 
1) The cost breakdown is helpful — can you confirm whether the token cost shown includes penalty billing for retries? Our billing team is seeing a mismatch versus the invoice.
2) SLA: you noted two maintenance events — would that qualify for any service credit? We track incident windows for our compliance.

Also, if you have a one-slide explanation of the latency spike root cause and proposed timeline to remediate that would help us explain to our operations folks. Can you send by Tuesday?

Thanks,
Tom Sweeney
Director of Integrations — PriorityHealth

cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.ai>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.ai>

The invoice shows billed units after our retry de-duplication policy; I asked Ava to include a reconciled view in KPI_notes_20260503.xlsx (tab: billing-reconcile). That should explain the mismatch — I'll confirm numbers with Billing and loop back by EOD.
- SLA credit: Rachel and I reviewed the incidents; both were scheduled maintenance windows that impacted a small subset of PriorityHealth workers. Our SLA (section 4.2) provides for credit evaluation for downtime exceeding monthly thresholds — Rachel will finalize the MTTR/RCA and eligibility for ...

messages.body:
Tom / PriorityHealth team —

Thanks for the earlier questions. Attached is the finalized QBR deck (QBR_deck_v3.pptx) and a separate single-slide summary that explains the latency root cause, remediation plan, and estimated timeline. Quick summary: 

- Root cause: routing preference that shifted a portion of production traffic to a lower-cost pool with limited warm buffer, increasing cold-start tail latency.
- Remediation: we reverted the routing preference at 14:00 PDT on 05/05; observed 95p latency drop back to baseline within 6 hours during ramp verification.
- Billing reconciliation: Ava attached the reconciled billing tab. We will issue any eligible SLA credits as per section 4.2; Rac...

title: Exec sponsor request for NorthBridge (pilot + security) — who should attend exec sync?
body:
Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>: Team —

NorthBridge is trending toward a real Private/VPC opportunity, and I’m hearing the security questionnaire + SSO edge cases are becoming the gating item. I want to get ahead of this with an exec sync and show we’re serious about supporting a regulated rollout.

Ask:
1) Do we need exec involvement on the next NorthBridge meeting(s)?
2) If yes, who should be the exec sponsor on our side (me vs Aisha vs Camila...

Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>: Yes — I think this is the right move.

Context:
- Champion is the GenAI Platform lead, but CISO team has the pen on the security checklist.
- They asked specifically about audit log export fields + retention controls and got stuck on SAML metadata validation (we have SUP-51214 running).
- They’re comparing us to two alternatives; one is “big cloud managed endpoint”...

messages.subject: Exec sponsor request for NorthBridge (pilot + security) — who should attend exec sync?
messages.body:
Team —

NorthBridge is trending toward a real Private/VPC opportunity, and I’m hearing the security questionnaire + SSO edge cases are becoming the gating item. I want to get ahead of this with an exec sync and show we’re serious about supporting a regulated rollout.

Ask:
1) Do we need exec involvement on the next NorthBridge meeting(s)?
2) If yes, who should be the exec sponsor on our side (me vs Aisha vs Camila), and what’s the best format?

Rationale (why now):
- They’re already investing platform time in a pilot and asking for evidence pack items.
- They want confidence on audit logging/retention and SAML/SSO readiness before they pull in procurement.
- A crisp exec-level “yes, we ca...

cc: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Exec sponsor request for NorthBridge (pilot + security) — who should attend exec sync?
messages.body:
Yes — I think this is the right move.

Context:
- Champion is the GenAI Platform lead, but CISO team has the pen on the security checklist.
- They asked specifically about audit log export fields + retention controls and got stuck on SAML metadata validation (we have SUP-51214 running).
- They’re comparing us to two alternatives; one is “big cloud managed endpoint” and the other is a self-hosted runtime vendor.

Recommendation:
- Exec sponsor should be you for commercial seriousness + escalation path.
- Aisha should join for the security posture portion (10 mins) to keep it crisp and credible.
- Markus + Avery to cover technical plan + next steps.

Proposed format (30 mins):
1) (Jordan) W...

cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Exec sponsor request for NorthBridge (pilot + security) — who should attend exec sync?
messages.body:
+1 on Steph’s plan.

A few meeting prep notes from last week’s check-in:
- Their success metric is “prove we can hit sub-300ms p95 for short prompts in-VPC + have auditable logs” (they keep repeating “auditability”).
- They want to see that our fallback/routing policies won’t break their governance model (i.e., no surprise model changes).
- They have internal pressure to pick a direction by mid-June so they can budget Q3 capacity.

What I’d like Jordan/Aisha to reinforce:
- We can support a controlled rollout with pinned model versions and documented change management.
- We have a repeatable evidence-sharing process (not ad hoc email forwarding).
- We will staff the pilot properly (SE + S...

cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>, Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Exec sponsor request for NorthBridge (pilot + security) — who should attend exec sync?
messages.body:
I can join for 10–15 mins.

If we do this, I’d suggest we keep the security segment tightly scoped and consistent with the regulated-workloads FAQ language:
- What we log (audit logging at control plane + request lifecycle) and how customers can export
- Encryption basics + KMS integration options (where applicable)
- Access controls (RBAC, SSO/SAML) at a “capabilities and process” level
- How we handle evidence pack requests and who reviews responses

Important: I will not answer “yes/no” to any compliance framework in a way that sounds like a blanket certification. I can speak to our posture and the artifacts we provide under NDA.

Also: if SAML metadata validation is the live issue, le...

cc: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>, Dr. Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Exec sponsor request for NorthBridge (pilot + security) — who should attend exec sync?
messages.body:
Decision:
- I’ll attend as exec sponsor.
- Aisha joins for the security segment (10–15 mins).
- Markus + Avery stay on for technical + next steps.

Camila — no need for you to join this one unless you think there’s a specific marketing/vertical asset angle we should bring in. I’d prefer we keep the room small.

Avery: please lock the time with their admin and send me (1) attendee list, (2) 1-page agenda, (3) top 5 “must not say” items.

Markus: please give Avery a short status on SUP-51214 and the current workaround so we can speak confidently without over-promising.

Thanks all.

— Jordan

If you can send these within the ne...

Looping you in — MedSecure wants explicit confirmation for CCPA/CPRA role and a short transit-encryption artifact. They also asked for the subprocessor manifest and notice timeline (30 days requested).

Can legal/legal-tech confirm wording we should provide and whether the manifest we share needs redaction? Also: do we have an onshore-fallback SLA statement we can share if they press onshore-only for telemetry?

Thanks,
Jordan
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0183

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: CCPA/CPRA role confirmation + transit encryption evidence
messages.body:
Jordan —

Legal confirms the short answers below; feel free to paste into a reply to Anna but attach the redline and the current subprocessors CSV. Suggested language (keeps to CPRA/CCPA bright-line):

• Role: "Redwood Inference will act as a Service Provider (as defined under the CCPA/CPRA) and, to the extent applicable under other U.S. state privacy laws, as a Processor. Redwood will process personal information only on behalf of and per the documented instructions of the controller and will not sell personal information."

• Transit encryption artifact: "Redwood secures in-transit data using TLS 1.2+ (we recommend TLS 1.3), employing AES-256 or equivalent ciphers for session encryption...

1) Role: Redwood confirms we will act as a Service Provider / Processor as applicable. We will process personal information only per MedSecure's instructions and do not sell personal information.

2) Encryption in transit (short artifact): "Redwood secures customer traffic in transit using TLS 1.2+; we recommend TLS 1.3. Customers requiring additional proof (certs, KM...

• Subprocessor notice: your 30-day standard is fine. Please confirm whether critical subprocessors (for inference runtime or logs) are included in the manifest or redacted; we need names for third-party risk scoring.

If you can loop in operations (or Marissa) to confirm onshore fallback timing and whether names can be shared, we can proceed with our redli...

messages.subject: Re: Infra OKR sync: capacity scenarios & GPU unit economics
messages.body:
Rachel — thanks for pulling this together. Quick read-through and notes below.

Summary answers:
- Utilization targets: the 42/58/72% bands look fine as long as we explicitly call out expected p50 vs p95 latency in each. For Dedicated, assume reserved:spot = 80:20 for conservative, 60:40 target, 40:60 aggressive (that changes unit-cost by ~9% in the workbook).
- Quant and cache assumptions: 4-bit on t2 is plausible for many of our smaller models; for L4-family large-chat models I'd call it risky without additional evals. If we gate quantization by model family the aggressive curve should be annotated as "partial quantization".
- Batch/caching impact: we saw ~22% token-cost reduction in th...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Infra OKR sync: capacity scenarios & GPU unit economics
messages.body:
Adding a couple of quick items from the ops experiments — pasted highlights and a Drive link to the latency plots (public within company):

Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1fK_capacity-scenarios

Highlights:
- Aggressive batching (batch size tuned to 3-6 tokens/req) reduced cost-per-token by ~28% in long tail, but for interactive flows p95 latency increased 100–180ms depending on shard placement.
- KV prefix cache: moving from 70% -> 90% hit reduces GPU token cost by ~18% but requires ~2.5x memory footprint for KV store; plan tradeoff vs dedicated node counts.
- Suggest we include a 'user-impact' row in the QBR slide: expected latency delta for high-frequency interactive cus...

- If we consider reserved-only, what is the minimum monthly committed spend that retains the <100ms p95 SLA for our production workload?

I can loop in our procurement team (procure@orion.ai) if you want them to see the draft commitment language.

– Alex
Alex Duarte
Infrastructure PM, Orion Analytics

messages.subject: DPA: roles, telemetry, and embeddings — redlines & signing timeline
messages.body:
Hi Jordan,

Thanks for the quick demo last week. As we start the procurement loop, legal ran into a few open items in the DPA draft that need clarification before we can execute:

- Controller vs Processor: the current draft is a bit ambiguous around embeddings (are embeddings considered "customer data" or a derived service output?).
- Logs & telemetry: our security team needs clear retention limits and what telemetry Redwood considers "operational" vs "diagnostic".
- Subprocessors: please confirm current list and provide the notification cadence for additions.
- Cross-border: we need to confirm the mechanism for transfers from EU to US (SCCs or alternative).

Can you share a redline and ...

messages.subject: Re: DPA: roles, telemetry, and embeddings — redlines & signing timeline
messages.body:
Hi Sara — thanks for the clear list. Looping in Laura (legal) and Rishi (privacy/security) to propose concrete language. Quick responses to your bullets before we send a redline: 

- Embeddings: our position is that embeddings generated from customer content are treated as "derivative outputs" used to serve the customer and are covered by the DPA; we can add explicit language that Redwood has no rights to sell or use embeddings for its own models.
- Logs & telemetry: we differentiate "operational telemetry" (routing/latency/error rates) and "diagnostic logs" (request traces, sample payloads). Operational telemetry will be retained for analytics as aggregated/anonymous by default; diagnost...

messages.subject: Re: DPA: roles, telemetry, and embeddings — redlines & signing timeline
messages.body:
Sara and Jordan — I've reviewed the DPA and prepared a redline + short summary. High-level points below (see attached ACME_DPA_redline_v2.docx and redline_summary_onepager.pdf):

1) Role definitions (Controller / Processor)
- Clause update: add definitional sentence: "For clarity, Customer is Controller of Customer Data; Redwood is Processor with respect to processing performed on behalf of Customer, except where Redwood independently determines aggregated telemetry for service improvement which is controlled by Redwood in anonymized form."
- Add explicit carve-out: "Embeddings derived from Customer Data are Processor outputs and not used by Redwood for model training or product benchmark...

messages.subject: Re: DPA: roles, telemetry, and embeddings — redlines & signing timeline
messages.body:
Laura / Jordan — thanks for the thorough redline. A few quick notes from Acme legal/security: 

- Embeddings: we appreciate the explicit carve-out. We still need a short sentence that these embeddings are "not personal data for purposes of GDPR when pseudonymized/aggregated" — happy to accept Redwood's language if you add that clause.
- Diagnostic logs retention: Acme's baseline is 90 days for US entities; for EU entities we require 30 days unless otherwise agreed. Can Redwood confirm a mechanism to set region-specific retention? (If not, we will ask for the 30-day limit in the EMEA addendum.)
- SCCs: please send the SCC text you intend to rely on and a suggested timeline for the EMEA add...

messages.subject: On-prem VPC onboarding & ops handoff — cutover readiness
messages.body:
Hi Sam / CloudEdge team,

Thanks again for partnering with us on the Acme on-prem VPC deployment. We completed infra provisioning over the weekend and I'd like to run the ops handoff/checklist with your team so we can lock the cutover window next week. Key items I want to validate in this thread: 

- Firewall ingress/egress rules for control plane (ports + CIDR allowlist)
- WireGuard peer configs and NAT traversal details
- KV cache persistence and S3-compatible endpoint for model artifacts
- Runbook acceptance: rollback criteria, canary traffic %, escalation matrix

Hi Mia / Tyler —

Use case summary:
- Primary: real-time customer support summarization + short-context retrieval for chat (multi-turn).
- Average request: ~512 tokens input + 256 tokens output (peak sequences ~1k).
- Current active users: 120 concurrent chat sessions during peak. Expected growth to 350 in 6–9 months.

Throughput and latency goals:
- Target steady-state throughput: ~3–4M tokens/day initially, moving to ~10M tokens/day in next 6 months (burst to 15M).
- Latency target: 150–250ms p90 for single-turn generation; p99 target acceptable up to 500ms if batching helps unit econ.

...

messages.subject: Re: Follow-up: Dedicated capacity fit & target $/1M — recap and ask
messages.body:
Hi Jordan — thanks for the clear recap. Quick answers from our side: 

- Steady-state: expect roughly 4M tokens/day at launch (conservative). If the pilot goes well, we expect to hit ~9–10M/day within 3–4 months.
- Budget / $/1M target: our procurement target is roughly $60–80 per 1M tokens for the committed pricing band. We can consider higher rates for shorter pilots but would need a path below $60 at scale.
- Pilot vs reserve: prefer a 3-month pilot that can convert to a 12-month reserved contract if performance and unit economics check out.

We lean toward lower latency (so H100 is attractive), but we need a clear cost comparison vs A100. Can you send the model with both GPU-family sc...

messages.subject: Re: Follow-up: Dedicated capacity fit & target $/1M — pricing bands attached
messages.body:
Thanks Mia — great, that helps. See attached pricing bands (high level) and assumptions; summary below. I'll leave the technical throughput validation to Camila.

Assumptions used for the model:
- Average sequence length: 768 tokens (512 in + 256 out).
- Effective batching and KV cache reuse reduce token cost by ~18% on steady-state chat patterns.
- We assume non-stop reserved usage during business hours with some burst capacity for peaks; pilot modeled as 12h/day sustained usage with higher bursts.

Estimated unit economics (illustrative):
- Option A — 2x H100 (reserved pilot footprint): estimated $/1M tokens: $55 — $68 (depends on burst hours and quantization). Pros: meets latency targe...

messages.subject: Re: Follow-up: Dedicated capacity fit & target $/1M — technical notes
messages.body:
Hi all — quick technical notes to align expectations before the infra test: 

- Sequence and model: assume 1k context support; we recommend serving a quantized LLM variant for chat that preserves quality at lower memory. That enables more concurrent KV caches and fewer GPU swaps.
- Throughput validation: for the pilot footprint (2x H100), we estimate sustainable steady-state of ~4–5M tokens/day with p90 < 250ms if we get ~80% cache hit-rate on repeated contexts.
- Test plan: 1) synthetic load using representative prompt distribution (I can share the script), 2) 2-hour soak at 1.5x expected peak to validate autoscaling triggers, 3) capture token counts and latency percentiles.
- Risks: ver...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: SDK quickstart & architecture handoff for embeddings + tool-calling
messages.body:
Jordan — thanks for the clear recap. A couple quick clarifying Qs before Mar 22 run: 
1) For the on-prem vector DB we plan to use Milvus. Will the sample app in the zip show an example insert/lookup against Milvus or just in-memory?
2) Our security team requires: API calls from our VPC must present a signed JWT that maps to a service principal, not a static key. Is that on your roadmap for the private deployment?
3) For reranking, we want to compare your built-in reranker vs a lightweight logistic ranker trained on our data — is that easy to plug in?

I’ll try the quickstart tonight and report back.

— Alice
CTO, Acme Platform

- Annualized SKU commitments (committed nodes/GPUs, annual prepay) with a lower platform fee vs
- A smaller committed SKU + platform fee amortization and discrete implementation credits during rollout.

We attached our list of questions (Nebula_Questions_v3.xlsx) — highlights: whether platform fee is refundable or creditable against node spend, how support tiers map to response windows for on-prem incidents, and whether we can str...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment pricing questions and SKU crediting
messages.body:
Hi Rebecca — thanks for the thoughtful list and for sharing the spreadsheet. I looped in Ben (pricing) and Jordan (solutions) so we can put a crisp comparison together. Quick answers up-front:

- Platform fee: typically a non-refundable recurring fee that covers the private control plane, licensing, and baseline observability; however, we can offer one-time implementation credits (or a transient reduced platform fee) for a staged roll-out.
- Crediting: we can apply implementation credits against your first two quarters of node invoices (not as a cash refund) or convert them to additional committed capacity at quote time.
- Support: our Standard/Enhanced/Premier tiers have defined SLOs; fo...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment pricing questions and SKU crediting -- initial scenarios
messages.body:
Team — drafting two compact scenarios below. I’ll produce a spreadsheet and a one-pager for Rebecca. Notes in-line:

Scenario A — Annualized SKU (Anchor)
- Commit: 24 x reserved nodes (NVIDIA H100-equivalent) annual prepay (invoiced quarterly)
- Platform fee: $40k / month (reduced rate due to commit)
- Support: Enhanced included; Premier add-on at +15% of platform fee
- Credits: 10% implementation credit applied as invoice credit across first 2 invoices
- Pros: predictable unit pricing, capacity reservation for spikes
- Cons: higher upfront commit, less flexibility for burst/region failover

Scenario B — Hybrid (Lower Commit + Amortized Platform)
- Commit: 8 x reserved nodes, metered burs...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment pricing questions and SKU crediting -- clarifications from procurement
messages.body:
Thanks Ben — these are helpful. Procurement has a few follow-ups so we can run internal approvals: 

1) Can implementation credits be structured as a true rebate on the node line (so it appears in our run-rate calculations) rather than only as a platform invoice offset?
2) We need a worked example that shows cashflow timing (invoices and credits) and the net effective price per GPU-hour at 50%/75%/100% utilization.
3) What are the guaranteed patching and security update windows in the Enhanced tier for a healthcare customer (SOC2 evidence required)?

On the early-adopter tranche: Nebula is open to a starter pilot (3–6 months) with a capped node count if we can get a 6-month reduced platfo...

messages.subject: Internal: suggested contract language and security notes
messages.body:
Internal note — copying key points I’ll add to the term sheet: 
- Credit treatment: propose we allow credits to be posted as \"Implementation Credits\" on the node line for the first 6 months; they reduce net invoiceable node units and are visible in Nebula's run-rate model (satisfies procurement ask #1).
- Conversion mechanism: credits convert to additional committed nodes at the quoted list price less the applied credit amount; conversion window months 6–18.
- Burst clause: automatic burst surcharge = list-burst-rate * (utilization / committed) for any sustained monthly average >110% (with a cap at 200% uplift).
- SOC2 / Patching: Enhanced tier includes monthly security patch windows an...

- Credit visibility: Implementation credits will appear on the node invoice l...

Jordan —

Thanks for the demo yesterday. We’re getting close on procurement but have a few gating items from our infra and compliance teams related to cross-region disaster recovery. Specifically, Acme needs a contractual commitment for:

- Regional failover RTO ≤ 120s for our production traffic (automatic failover to secondary region)
- RPO ≤ 30s for session state/short-lived cache 
- 95th percentile inference latency ≤ 200ms in the primary region under normal load
- An on-call escalation path and quarterly DR failover tests with results shared with Acme

We understand this is outside standard public SLA language. Please advise if Redwood can provide a scoped exception (for Acme account ...

messages.subject: Re: SLA request: cross-region failover guarantees (RTO/RPO)
messages.body:
Marta —

Thanks for the clear summary and the attachment. I’ve looped in Hannah (Reliability) and Laura (Legal/Procurement) to assess feasibility and put together a proposed addendum. High level: we can support account-scoped exceptions but there are trade-offs (warm standby vs active-active, replication window, and cost for reserved capacity).

Could you confirm: is Acme willing to consider a Dedicated pool with guaranteed capacity in both regions? That’s the most straightforward path to hit the RTO/RPO numbers. If not, we can propose a runbook + regular DR rehearsals with a slightly longer RTO.

I’ll follow up with our internal assessment shortly.

—Jordan
Jordan Blake
Senior Account Ex...

messages.subject: Re: SLA request: cross-region failover guarantees (RTO/RPO)
messages.body:
Jordan / Laura —

Quick engineering read on feasibility (high level):

- Warm-standby (our standard multi-region architecture) typically yields RTO in the 90–180s range due to DNS propagation, KV cache rebuild, and model warmup. Achieving consistent RTO ≤ 120s is possible but requires:
 - Prewarmed model instances in the secondary region (reserved Dedicated capacity)
 - Continuous KV prefix replication (we can get RPO ≤ 30s with cross-region streaming replication)

- Latency 95p ≤ 200ms: depends on model size and token length. For lightweight models and colocated region traffic we can meet this. For larger models we recommend a quantized variant and per-route routing to the nearest region...

messages.subject: Re: SLA request: cross-region failover guarantees (RTO/RPO)
messages.body:
Jordan —

From a contracting/procurement perspective we can do a scoped SLA addendum for Acme, but we need to be explicit on a few points so Legal can draft:

1) Scope: single Acme account and specific production clusters/regions (list to be defined)
2) Metrics & Measurement: exact RTO/RPO measurement windows, measurement methodology (start-of-failover timestamp, steady-state criteria), and observability proof artifacts to be shared after each test
3) Testing: quarterly DR test requirement, pre-notice windows, success criteria and who signs off
4) Remedies: service credits capped at a negotiated % of monthly committed spend; no unlimited liability
5) Preconditions: Acme must run recommend...

messages.subject: Re: SLA request: cross-region failover guarantees (RTO/RPO)
messages.body:
Marta —

Thanks again for the clear requirements. After internal review we can offer a scoped SLA addendum for Acme with the following summary (attached full addendum draft):

- Commitment: account-scoped cross-region failover guarantee with target RTO ≤ 120s and RPO ≤ 30s, subject to the preconditions below
- Preconditions: Dedicated reserved capacity prewarmed in primary + secondary regions, agreed DR runbook and quarterly DR exercises, and shared observability for verification
- Latency target: 95th percentile inference latency goal documented per-route; final target depends on model selection and will be set in the addendum
- Remedies: service credits up to a capped percentage of mont...

- Regional failover RTO ≤ 120s for our production traffic (automatic failover to secondary region)
- RPO ≤ 30s for session state/short-lived cache

High level: we can support account-scoped exceptions but there are trade-offs (warm standby vs active-active, replication window, and cost for reserved capacity).

Could you confirm: is Acme willing to consider ...

messages.body:
Hi Alex,

Thanks for the product overview last week — Medix is keen to move forward with a private deployment for our PII-heavy workflows. Before procurement and legal can sign off we need clarity on a few privacy and control points so we can finalize our DPA annex. Please see the attached questionnaire (Medix_DPA_Questionnaire.pdf) and confirm the following at a high level:

- If we elect customer-managed keys (customer KMS / BYOK) will Redwood personnel ever be able to access plaintext inference data or model inputs/outputs?
- Do you maintain an operator "breakglass" process that could allow temporary elevated access? If yes, what controls and audit evidence are provided?
- Please provi...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment privacy questions and subprocessors inventory
messages.body:
Sara — thanks for the clear list. Looping in Marissa (AE), Rishi (Legal/Security), and Kimberly (Infosec) so we can provide a consolidated response and the requested artifacts. Quick summary for procurement while we assemble the docs:

- Customer-managed keys (BYOK): supported. When BYOK is enabled, Redwood does not hold the plaintext encryption keys in our control plane; by default we do not have access to unencrypted inference payloads.
- Breakglass / operator access: we have an emergency operator process (used extremely rarely) — it requires multi-person approval, justification, and is fully audited. Rishi/Kimberly will propose acceptable wording for the DPA annex.
- Subprocessors: we'...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment privacy questions and subprocessors inventory
messages.body:
Team — drafting notes and redline suggestions for the annex. Key points to include for Medix: 

- Operator access: add an explicit clause that, for BYOK configurations, Redwood personnel will not have access to customer plaintext or model inputs/outputs except under documented emergency breakglass. The breakglass clause should require (a) two-person approval from separate roles, (b) real-time notification to a named customer contact, and (c) write-once audit record delivered to customer within 72 hours.

- Subprocessors: include commitment to provide a 30-day notice for additions and an ability for Medix to object to specific subprocessors for reasonable cause. For the CSV we will flag an...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment privacy questions and subprocessors inventory
messages.body:
Sara — appreciate the patience. Summary and attachments (see files): Redwood_PrivDep_DPA_Annex_v1.pdf, Subprocessors_Inventory_202605.csv, Export_Control_Compliance_Form.pdf. Key confirmations below: 

- No operator plaintext access with BYOK: When Medix enables customer-managed keys, Redwood does not possess the plaintext keys and therefore cannot read inference inputs/outputs.
- Breakglass: we support a documented, auditable breakglass workflow (two-person approval, documented justification, notification to customer contact). We will include the breakglass clause and audit delivery timelines in the annex.
- Subprocessors: attached CSV lists current subprocessors and notes access levels....

title: Post-call: RTBF / purge playbook and backup constraints
body:
Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>: Hi Elena — great to meet earlier. Quick recap of what we heard and proposed next steps so everyone is aligned.

Notes from the call:
- Requirement: Right-to-be-forgotten (PII delete) requests must be actionable via API and UI; median SLA target requested: 7 calendar days.
- Scope: user PII stored in primary DB + derived embeddings (search index).
- Backups: They cannot have guaranteed immediate deletion from backu...

Elena Rivera <elena.rivera@mediapps.com>: Thanks Marissa — appreciate the clear recap. A few clarifications from our side:

- 7-day SLA: This is our target for surface systems (primary DB + search index). It is not negotiable for customer-facing deletes due to regulatory commitments in a couple of our markets.
- Backups: We understand technical constraints; for our compliance team, anything beyond 90 days for full purging from bac...

messages.subject: Post-call: RTBF / purge playbook and backup constraints
messages.body:
Hi Elena — great to meet earlier. Quick recap of what we heard and proposed next steps so everyone is aligned.

Notes from the call:
- Requirement: Right-to-be-forgotten (PII delete) requests must be actionable via API and UI; median SLA target requested: 7 calendar days.
- Scope: user PII stored in primary DB + derived embeddings (search index).
- Backups: They cannot have guaranteed immediate deletion from backups; ask: acceptable max purge window? MediApps team signaled desire for shortest possible.
- Residency: Customer data must remain in US region; legal wants audit trail for deletions.

What Redwood can provide (high level):
- API endpoint for RTBF requests and an ops playbook for ...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: RTBF / purge playbook and backup constraints
messages.body:
Thanks Marissa — appreciate the clear recap. A few clarifications from our side:

- 7-day SLA: This is our target for surface systems (primary DB + search index). It is not negotiable for customer-facing deletes due to regulatory commitments in a couple of our markets.
- Backups: We understand technical constraints; for our compliance team, anything beyond 90 days for full purging from backups will be problematic. If some backup media requires a longer window, we need a compensating control and a written attestation.
- Auditability: We need a signed deletion manifest with timestamps and the hashes of records removed (or a comparable proof-of-deletion).
- Integration: We will manage RTBF i...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: RTBF / purge playbook and backup constraints
messages.body:
Hi all — thanks, looping in technical details below so legal/compliance teams have concrete language. I annotated the attached playbook and the retention matrix; summary here.

Technical summary (concise):
- Primary DB (Postgres): delete operations are executed immediately when RTBF is processed. Records removed from the active DB within the same transaction; dependent rows (derived data) are flagged and removed by a downstream job that runs within the RTBF process. Expected completion: within 24 hours for small volumes, within 7 calendar days for large/complex accounts.
- Embeddings / search index (Redis + vector store): we can remove vectors and associated metadata within 24–72 hours de...

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: RTBF / purge playbook and backup constraints
messages.body:
Elena — thanks for the quick clarity and Rishi for the technical run-through. Proposed immediate next steps and owners:

1) Rishi: final retention matrix + deletion-manifest schema (deliverable: annotated matrix + sample manifest) — due EOD Friday 2026-06-19.
2) Marissa/Jordan: draft pilot contract language (SLA 7d primary/index + 90d backup purge OR upgrade path to Dedicated) — due Tue 2026-06-23.
3) MediApps (Elena): confirm whether 7 days is strict and whether we should include a webhook integration for ticketing — please reply by Mon 2026-06-22.
4) Schedule: I'll send a 30-minute follow-up meeting for next week to review the retention matrix and legal language.

If that plan looks goo...

Hi Alice and FinBank team,

Thanks for the time on Friday — following up with a concise plan for the private-hosted deployment (your "VPC islands"). Goal: minimize blast radius while giving you ownership of egress/edge TLS and a practical cert rotation model that works for air-gapped segments. Attached is a simple diagram (vpc-island-tls-arch-v1.drawio).

Key proposal (summary):
- DNS: split-horizon / conditional forwarder so internal FQDNs for redwood endpoints resolve to private IPs inside your VPCs; public resolution remains unchanged. We recommend a short TTL (60s-300s) during initial rollout.
- TLS termination: two-tier approach:
 1) Customer edge (your ingress) terminates external c...

messages.subject: Re: VPC island DNS override, CSR workflow & multi-tier TLS termination
messages.body:
Rachel — thanks for the clear breakdown. A few clarifying points from our side: 

1) PKI: Our internal PKI team will sign CSRs for internal-facing certs. We do not allow external ACME flows from air-gapped subnets. We can provide the CA cert and fingerprint (attaching finbank-internal-ca.pem).

2) DNS: We can create conditional forwarders to our resolver in each VPC region; however, we would prefer a per-tenant namespace so ops can control delegation without touching global zones. Is redwood-private.{tenant}.finbank.local a supported pattern?

3) mTLS: We like the mTLS path between internal edge proxy and Redwood connector. Two operational asks: a) ability to preregister certificate thumb...

messages.subject: Re: VPC island DNS override, CSR workflow & multi-tier TLS termination
messages.body:
Hi Alice — quick technical responses and a recommended implementation sequence.

Implementation sequence (recommended):
1) Zone and FQDN pattern: Use per-tenant subdomains under your private authority, e.g. redwood-private.TENANT.finbank.local. That avoids global zone edits and lets you manage ACLs per-tenant. Redwood accepts arbitrary hostnames — we just need consistent CN/SANs when generating CSRs.

2) DNS mechanics: Configure conditional forwarders from your VPC resolvers to a trusted resolver that we can register in our records, or simply return private A records via your resolver. For cross-VPC reachability please confirm VPC peering/transit gateway rules. TTL guidance: 120s during r...

messages.subject: Re: VPC island DNS override, CSR workflow & multi-tier TLS termination
messages.body:
Great — thanks for the fast turn. Meeting confirmed: Tue 13 May 2025 11:00 ET (I sent a calendar invite). Agenda for the 30-min call:
- 5m: quick diagram walk (vpc-island-tls-arch-v1.drawio)
- 10m: CSR template & upload flow (we'll share the script before the call)
- 10m: DNS delegation pattern and per-tenant namespace decisions
- 5m: action items & owners (we'll capture in HubSpot HS-53421)

Action items (owner):
- FinBank: share internal CA PEM + PKI contact (Alice) — DONE (attached).
- Redwood (Karthik): share CSR template, rotation script, and canary API docs — ETA: 24 hours.
- Redwood (Rachel): update runbook with conditional-forwarder patterns and example route53/Bind snippets — ETA...

title: Residency playbook: approval, governance and EU/APAC switchover kickoff
body:
Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>: Hi all,

Thanks for moving quickly on the ACME private deployment. We're at the point where we need governance approval and a mutual action plan for EU/APAC residency + warm-standby failover. Attached is the residency playbook draft (residency_playbook_draft_v1.pdf) and a lightweight topology sketch.

High-level asks in this kickoff:
- ACME to confirm acceptable cutover windows and change-control lead for each reg...

Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>: Jordan, Mina —

Quick technical notes from infra:
- For failover we should prefer regional-egress routing with per-region load balancing + BGP tie-break rather than cross-region NAT traversal. That keeps egress locality intact and reduces audit surface.
- We'll present two options for prefix ownership: (A) Redwood announces customer-specific prefixes from regional edges (Redwood-managed, quicke...

to: Mina Tan <mina.tan@acmecloud.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Residency playbook: approval, governance and EU/APAC switchover kickoff
messages.body:
Hi all,

Thanks for moving quickly on the ACME private deployment. We're at the point where we need governance approval and a mutual action plan for EU/APAC residency + warm-standby failover. Attached is the residency playbook draft (residency_playbook_draft_v1.pdf) and a lightweight topology sketch.

High-level asks in this kickoff:
- ACME to confirm acceptable cutover windows and change-control lead for each region (APAC/EU).
- Agree on ownership for prefix announcements and DNS failover (Redwood ops vs ACME network team).
- Confirm KMS custody model for any EU-resident keys (bring-your-own vs Redwood-managed).
- Approve a canary and rollback plan for the first switchover exercise.

Ple...

messages.subject: Re: Residency playbook: approval, governance and EU/APAC switchover kickoff
messages.body:
Jordan, Mina —

Quick technical notes from infra:
- For failover we should prefer regional-egress routing with per-region load balancing + BGP tie-break rather than cross-region NAT traversal. That keeps egress locality intact and reduces audit surface.
- We'll present two options for prefix ownership: (A) Redwood announces customer-specific prefixes from regional edges (Redwood-managed, quicker), or (B) ACME announces their own prefixes via a shared peering (ACME-managed, preferred by many regulated customers).
- KV cache ownership: during warm-standby the cache TTL and sync window will determine RTO. I recommend short TTLs on user-level KV and explicit cache-warmup steps in the canary.
...

messages.subject: Re: Residency playbook: approval, governance and EU/APAC switchover kickoff
messages.body:
Thanks Jordan and Marcus. A few points from ACME side:

1) Change window: APAC prefers weekends 02:00–06:00 JST; EU prefers 00:00–04:00 CEST on Sat/Sun. We need a documented rollback SLA (target 30 minutes) and a post-mortem within 24h.

2) Prefix ownership: ACME legal and network ops prefer option B (ACME announces prefixes). We'll need the peering contract and an IP ownership attestation.

3) KMS: ACME will insist on BYOK for EU-resident keys. Please send the technical steps and timeline for HSM provisioning.

4) Canary: we'll run a DNS-based canary for 48h routing 2% of traffic to Redwood warm-standby. We also require a read-only access token for Redwood's audit logs during the canary ...

messages.subject: Re: Residency playbook: approval, governance and EU/APAC switchover kickoff
messages.body:
Mina — thanks, that's helpful. Action items I'm capturing here:

1) ACME to confirm peering contact and provide IP attestation (owner: Mina) — target 2025-07-03.
2) Redwood (Marcus) to publish a BYOK/HSM provisioning doc and estimated lead time — target 2025-07-06.
3) Legal (ACME + Redwood legal) to exchange SOW redlines — target 2025-07-07.
4) Redwood infra to produce a short 'canary runbook' that includes DNS failover steps, metrics we will monitor, and rollback triggers — target 2025-07-10.

I'll prep a mutual action plan doc (link: https://redwood.app/drive/residency-playbook-v1) and will send a calendar invite for a 2025-07-08 governance call (30 minutes) to review final cutover wind...

body:

We're seeing a sharp increase in 503s and 95th percentile latency for the /generate endpoint from our prod tenant starting ~08:45 PDT. Error rate jumped from ~0.5% to ~6% and many of our payment flows are timing out. This is impacting a set of merchants.

We've attached a short perf trace and a couple of sample request IDs (trace files attached). Can you confirm whether our reserved pool is saturat...

Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>: Tom — thanks for the quick heads up and the trace. I appreciate the request IDs. I'm escalating this now to our capacity & infra team and copying Jordan + Marissa (your reps). We'll triage immediately and share a remediation plan in the next 30 minutes.

Preliminary ask from our side: can you confirm whether the traffic spike correlates with a campaign or a scheduled job? Also: are any of thes...

normalized.subject: FinApps: sudden 95th pct latency and reserved capacity shortfall

messages.subject: Sudden errors and latency on production (FinApps)
messages.body:
Hi Stephanie —

We're seeing a sharp increase in 503s and 95th percentile latency for the /generate endpoint from our prod tenant starting ~08:45 PDT. Error rate jumped from ~0.5% to ~6% and many of our payment flows are timing out. This is impacting a set of merchants.

We've attached a short perf trace and a couple of sample request IDs (trace files attached). Can you confirm whether our reserved pool is saturated or if this looks like a model/regression issue? We need an ETA for mitigation.

Sample request IDs: req_6a2b, req_6a33, req_6a47

Thanks,
Tom
Director, Platform Ops
FinApps Inc.

Attachments: finapps_perf_trace_0315.zip (zip)

messages.subject: Re: Sudden errors and latency on production (FinApps)
messages.body:
Tom — thanks for the quick heads up and the trace. I appreciate the request IDs. I'm escalating this now to our capacity & infra team and copying Jordan + Marissa (your reps). We'll triage immediately and share a remediation plan in the next 30 minutes.

Preliminary ask from our side: can you confirm whether the traffic spike correlates with a campaign or a scheduled job? Also: are any of these requests hitting function/tool-calls or long context windows > 4k tokens?

I'll follow up with an action plan shortly.

— Stephanie
Senior AE, Customer Success
Redwood Inference

to: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Fwd: FinApps perf incident — start escalation
messages.body:
Forwarding Tom's note — marking as P1 for Dedicated reserved capacity impacts.

Immediate steps I'm asking for:
- Vivek: run pool observer and confirm per-gpu utilization and queue lengths for tenant finapps_prod (priority tagging).
- Jordan: reach out to FinApps exec ops (Priya) to set expectations while we triage.
- Stephanie: pull any SLO credit history and existing compensation commitments.

If we confirm reserved capacity saturation, we should propose a temporary routing fallback to hosted lower-latency replicas AND start an emergency add of +2 GPU capacity to the customer's reserved pool (subject to approval). I want an owner for the approval path now.

Marissa
Head of Sales — Enter...

to: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: FinApps perf incident — pool metrics & recommended mitigation
messages.body:
I'm seeing clear evidence of pool saturation: GPUs in pool finapps-prod-1 are at sustained 98–100% SM utilization and queue depth has hit >300 waiting requests. The load pattern shifted at 08:46 GMT-7 — traffic fanout increased from a handful of clients to a wide cohort (likely a campaign). Attached trace matches KV-cache misses on hot prefixes, increasing per-token work.

Short-term mitigations we can execute immediately (10–30m):
- Activate per-tenant throttling at the load-balancer replacing token-based burst limits with request-rate caps to preserve latency for in-flight critical flows (we can set a rule to favor /payments/* endpoints).
- Spin up +2 GPU emergency nodes into the reserv...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek@redwood.ai>, procurement@finapps.io
messages.subject: Status update and mitigation plan — FinApps production impact (P1)
messages.body:
Hi Priya / Tom —

Thanks again for the quick flag earlier. Summary and next steps:

What happened: at ~08:46 PDT a FinApps campaign caused a volume fanout that produced sustained hot-prefix KV-cache misses and pushed the dedicated pool (finapps-prod-1) to saturation. This increased queueing and raised the 95th percentile latency and 503s.

What we did (completed):
- Activated request-rate caps to protect critical /payments endpoints (applied at 09:05 PDT).
- Provisioned +2 emergency GPUs into the reserved pool; nodes came online by 09:22 PDT and reduced queue depth by ~70%.
- Enabled temporary routing of non-critical traffic to hosted replicas (keeps user flows alive while preserving cust...

title: Realtime stream dropouts and heartbeat failures — urgent
body:
Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@crestlineanalytics.com>: Hi Rachel —

Heads up: starting ~08:45 UTC our production customers saw repeated interruptions on the realtime WebSocket feed for our inference-backed product. Symptoms:
- Token stream halts (no further chunks) for 3–30s, then resumes mid-token
- Client sees repeated websocket CLOSE/REOPEN cycles and increased 502/504s from the proxy layer
- Error rate spiked from ~0.3% to ~7% and p99 latency for initial token jum...

Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>: Liam —

Thanks for the fast report and the logs — this is high priority. I've looped in Soojin (runtime/serving) and David (infra) to investigate. Initial asks while we triage:
1) Can you share the full connection-id for a failing session and a HAR-like capture if you have it?
2) Any correlation with model variant changes or traffic spikes (we'll pull metrics but your side ...

Hi Rachel —

We captured client-side traces and a short server log snippet (attached). This is impacting live demos and feed-backed UIs. Can your team advise immediate mitigations and confirm whether this is an infra routing issue or something in the chunked-transfer/...

Immediate short-term mitigations to consider (we'll spin these up if you approve):
- Route your traffic to the reserved/dedicated pool we have for you (if available) to rule out noisy neighbors
...

messages.subject: Fwd: Realtime stream dropouts — urgent escalation / initial findings
messages.body:
Forwarding initial triage notes — see below for summary.

On Mon, 21 Jun 2026 at 09:27 -0700, Rachel Kim wrote:
> Liam —
>
> Thanks for the fast report and the logs — this is high priority...

Triage summary (so far):
- Observed across multiple edge nodes in us-west and us-east; not isolated to a single host
- Server-side logs show repeated "heartbeat_ack_timeout" events correlated with connection refresh (websocket close/reopen)
- We see intermittent stalls in the chunk assembly pipeline: some partial chunks arrive but are not emitted for 2–20s while the kernel waits on the KV-cache flush path
- Metric snapshots: 5m error rate jumped to 6.9%, 1m p95 token latency = 820ms, p99 token laten...

messages.subject: Re: Realtime stream dropouts — multiple missed heartbeats and chunk stalls
messages.body:
Rachel / team —

Appreciate the quick response and the suggested mitigations. We are on hosted pool for this environment. We authorise the following immediately:
- Failover to dedicated pool for the next 2 hours
- Enable heartbeat suppression for the maintenance window

We expect:
1) A timeline for root-cause and an ETA for a permanent fix (we need a preliminary RCA within 24 hours)
2) A formal note on SLA impact and proposed credit/compensation — this disruption hit three high-touch demos and one customer trial today
3) A confirmed contact for exec-level communication (we will escalate if outages continue)

We'll also attach a HAR from a failing session to the Drive link you provided. Pl...

cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwoodinference.com>, David Kim <david.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Realtime stream dropouts — multiple missed heartbeats and chunk stalls (summary & next steps)
messages.body:
Priya / Liam —

Quick update and action plan after the 24hr triage window:
- Failover to your dedicated pool completed at 09:55 UTC on 21 Jun. We observed immediate reduction in stall incidents (error rate down to ~0.9% in the dedicated pool).
- Heartbeat suppression enabled at 09:58 UTC; this reduced false-close events and stabilized p99 token latency to ~310ms during the window.
- Deployed a reversible hotfix at 15:30 UTC (bypasses the KV-flush path for small partial-chunks). Early results on canary nodes show elimination of the 2–20s holds in chunk emission. Patch attached: workaround-patch.diff

Owners & next steps:
- Soojin / Serving runtime: complete post-deployment verification acr...

body:

Thanks again for the call this morning. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have alignment before we spin up the trial environment: 

High-level trial goals (from today's call):
- Validate end-to-end inference latency under your baseline traffic (peak: ~180 qps).
- Verify end-to-end tracing/correlation (client request -> Redwood -> model -> response).
- Confirm retention and export of request/res...

Elena Parks <elena.parks@tempodata.com>: Rafael —

Appreciate the write-up, this is really helpful. A few clarifications from our side: 

Success criteria tweaks:
- Can we tighten the latency SLO to p95 <= 200ms for small payloads (<128 tokens)? For larger payloads you can keep the 220ms cap.
- Tracing: we need end-to-end sampling at 100% for the POC window (we can relax after evaluation).
- Logs: export to S3 within 15 minutes ...

Quick note on mapping Redwood metrics to TempoData acceptance criteria so there's no ambiguity during verification: 

- Latency: p95 <= 200ms for token_count < 128 -> Redwood metric: inference.latency.ms (percentile, filter token_count:<128)

- Log export: export_success.latency.ms -> we will alert if any export takes > 900 seconds (15 minutes) and keep an export retry counter visible on dashboard

Marissa —

Thanks for the clear recap. Nice to meet you, Rachel/Vivek. Quick technical questions from our side before the kickoff: 

1) Auth: we prefer customer-managed secrets in AWS Secrets Manager with short-lived tokens. Is there a recommended flow for exchanging a short-lived Redwood token scoped to the POC project?

messages.subject: Re: Post-call: Implementation plan & SDK samples for BrightWave POC — quick code examples
messages.body:
Helena —

Thanks for the questions. Below are concise answers and two tiny snippets you can paste-run locally. I've attached redwood_sdk_sample_python.zip with a small sample app and an env setup script.

Auth flow recommendation
- For your requirement (AWS Secrets Manager + short-lived tokens) we recommend issuing short-lived Redwood API tokens from a backend that authenticates via your KMS-backed secret. Flow: Backend authenticates to AWS Secrets Manager -> exchanges for a short-lived Redwood token (exp 1h) via control plane (authenticated with mTLS/brokered credential) -> passes token to client for calls. See attached redwood_api_auth_guide.pdf for diagrams.

Model variants
- Generator...

Thanks Sam — we can do that. I sketched a short MAP and a technical evaluation plan (attached) that we can iterate on. Summary below; full results will be delivered by EOD tomorrow after we run additional TTFT/streaming experiments against an Optima-like prompt set.

- TTFT (time-to-first-token) for chat (redwood-chat-13b-q4_0, streaming): p50 <= 200 ms, p95 <= 450 ms, p99 <= 850 ms

- Throughput: sustained 1200 tokens/s per GPU for short sequences (avg 80 tokens) with continuous batching; peak burst up...

messages.subject: RE: Performance MAP: TTFT & streaming latency benchmarks for Optima POC

Hi Sam / Marta — attaching the completed benchmark pack (streaming_bench_results.csv, ttft_latency_plots.png) and a short summary here. We ran the tests on Dedicated H100 80GB (driver + runtime tuned for continuous batching). Model: redwood-chat-13b-q4_0 (production quantized profile). Test harness: 10k trials across a 150-sample prompt set representing clinical triage dialogs (40–120 tokens). Measurements taken from client-side request start to first-byte emitted (TTFT) and inter-token intervals for streaming latency. All measurements reported as p50/p95/p99.

- TTFT (ms) — redwood-chat-13b-q4_0, stream...

title: Zenith Health — latency windowing, measurement methodology, and service credit examples
body:
Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>: Hi Anna,

Thanks for the RFP items on SLOs — attached are the evidence pack and a measurement matrix that map our SLA language to telemetry artifacts.

High-level measurement approach:
- Uptime: calculated per-route as the percent of probe+production-successful requests over the billing window. We use a moving 30-day window for monthly SLA checks and a 24-hour burst window for short outages.
- Latency: p95 and p99...

Anna Weston <anna.weston@zenithhealth.com>: Hi Alex,

Thanks for the quick turnaround and the attachments — helpful. A few clarifying items before we schedule:
1) Burst windows: when you say a 24-hour burst window, does that mean a contiguous outage of X minutes within 24 hours or an aggregated set of small incidents? Our legal team is sensitive to aggregated micro-failures summing to a large credit.
2) Cache exclusions: we rely...

normalized.subject: Zenith Health — latency windowing, measurement methodology, and service credit examples

messages.subject: Zenith Health — initial SLO evidence and measurement notes
messages.body:
Hi Anna,

Thanks for the RFP items on SLOs — attached are the evidence pack and a measurement matrix that map our SLA language to telemetry artifacts.

High-level measurement approach:
- Uptime: calculated per-route as the percent of probe+production-successful requests over the billing window. We use a moving 30-day window for monthly SLA checks and a 24-hour burst window for short outages.
- Latency: p95 and p99 measured on user-facing requests at ingress, excluding cache hits (we include cache hit rate as a separate metric). Windows are computed per-hour and aggregated to daily and monthly percentiles using weighted sampling to avoid spike skew.
- Sampling & telemetry: 100% sampling fo...

messages.subject: Re: Zenith Health — initial SLO evidence and measurement notes
messages.body:
Hi Alex,

Thanks for the quick turnaround and the attachments — helpful. A few clarifying items before we schedule:
1) Burst windows: when you say a 24-hour burst window, does that mean a contiguous outage of X minutes within 24 hours or an aggregated set of small incidents? Our legal team is sensitive to aggregated micro-failures summing to a large credit.
2) Cache exclusions: we rely heavily on in-memory result caching in our app tier. For latency p99/p95, will Redwood exclude cache hits entirely or provide parallel metrics (with-cache / without-cache)? We need reproducible dashboards.
3) Dedicated vs Private: do the same telemetry and crediting rules apply to Dedicated pools and Privat...

messages.subject: Re: Zenith Health — clarifying Anna's questions
messages.body:
Alex / Dev,

Anna's four bullets look like the ones we usually get — proposed short answers below for Alex to send back. I also attached a one-line crediting-calculator that maps severity/duration -> percent of monthly invoice.

1) Burst window definition: propose this wording — "Burst window is a 24-hour rolling window used for short outage detection. For credit calculations, incidents are considered contiguous when two outage events are separated by less than 5 minutes; otherwise they are separate incidents and credited independently." The 5-minute stitch rule prevents micro-flapping from inflating credit.

2) Cache handling: provide both metrics. We'll deliver p99_user_requests_excludi...

messages.subject: Zenith Health — finalized measurement definitions and sample credit calculations
messages.body:
Hi Anna,

Thanks for the questions — we reviewed internally and documented the precise definitions below. I've also uploaded a short walkthrough to the Drive link and attached the crediting CSV.

Definitions (final):
- Uptime (Availability): percent of successful HTTP 2xx/3xx responses to user-facing API endpoints measured by combined production telemetry and our global probe network. Measurement window: 30-day rolling window for monthly SLA; 24-hour rolling "burst" window used to detect short outages for operational response.
- Burst aggregation rule: two outage events within 5 minutes are stitched and treated as one contiguous incident for crediting.
- Latency: p95 and p99 computed on u...

title: Probe Loan: onboarding corridor & usage constraints
body:
Jesse O'Neill <jesse.oneill@brightretail.com>: Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for the demo last week — our ML team is keen to run a short probe against Redwood to validate latency and slice-level quality for our retail re-ranking use case. Before we open a sandbox, we need to align on a Mutual Action Plan and the usage constraints for the assets we will loan you. High level ask: a two-week canary followed by a 6-week evaluation window.

What we plan to provide:
- Pr...

Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>: Jesse — thanks for the clear spec and the manifest. This is tractable and aligns with our standard probe flows. Short answers up front: yes on isolated sandbox (we support VPC peering or short-lived sandbox instances), yes on token caps, and yes on automated takedown.

Ill draft a concise MAP with milestones and an operational checklist and send it by tomorrow EOD. Proposed high-level tim...

Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for the demo last week — our ML team is keen to run a short probe against Redwood to validate latency and slice-level quality for our retail re-ranking use case. Before we open a sandbox, we need to align on a Mutual Action Plan and the usage constraints for the assets we will loan you. High level ask: a two-week canary followed by a 6-week evaluation window.

What we plan to provide:
- Prompt pack (approx. 150 curated prompts)
- Testcases (~2k examples; mix of annotations and golden outputs)
- Small metadata manifest (see attached)

Key constraints from our side:
- Assets are provided under an ephemeral, evaluation-only agreement (no production use).
- Access mus...

messages.subject: Re: Probe Loan: onboarding corridor & usage constraints
messages.body:
Jesse — thanks for the clear spec and the manifest. This is tractable and aligns with our standard probe flows. Short answers up front: yes on isolated sandbox (we support VPC peering or short-lived sandbox instances), yes on token caps, and yes on automated takedown.

Ill draft a concise MAP with milestones and an operational checklist and send it by tomorrow EOD. Proposed high-level timeline (subject to your signoff):
- Day 0: MPA sign + SOC2 evidence exchange + account provisioning
- D1-2: Sandbox provisioning + one-time key issuance (ephemeral keys)
- D3-9: Canary (2-week equivalent window can be compressed for a small burst; we recommend 10 business days)
- D10-42: Expansion evaluati...

messages.subject: Fwd: Probe Loan: onboarding corridor & usage constraints
messages.body:
Jesse — looping in Marissa who will own provisioning and the technical runbook. Forwarding Rachel's note with an initial operational MAP below. Marissa: see Jesse's manifest and the constraints; please confirm resource estimates.

--- Forwarded message ---

messages.subject: Re: Probe Loan: onboarding corridor & usage constraints
messages.body:
(quoted above)

Operational MAP (draft):
- Kickoff (Day 0): legal MPA signed; SOC2 certificate and AICPA contact provided.
- Provisioning (D1): allocate a single isolated sandbox; create ephemeral service account + keys (expires after 90 days by default; we will set to 60 if you confirm).
- Canary (D2-D11): run targeted subset (~200 testcases) to validate perf.
- Expansion (D12-D50): full testcase run, monitor quality metrics and cost telemetry.
- Acceptance gate (D51): Redwood and BrightRetail run acceptance suite; if pass, Redwood delivers an evaluation report.
- Takedown (D52-D60): automated takedown scheduled; export archived metrics and remove artifacts.

Operational constraints / co...

messages.subject: Re: Probe Loan: onboarding corridor & usage constraints
messages.body:
Thanks Ben/Rachel — that MAP looks good and the operational notes match what we need. Two quick items:
1) Please set the sandbox retention to 60 days (not 30) and make the ephemeral keys expire at the end of the evaluation window automatically.
2) I'll introduce our procurement/legal contact (Lena Park) in a separate note so we can get the MPA amendment and a short PO issued.

Once we have the MPA draft and SOC2 redacted report, we're ready to sign and start provisioning next week.

Jesse
Jesse O'Neill
Head of ML Ops, BrightRetail
[Attachment: none]

Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>: Hi all —

We’re ready for cross-functional review on the refreshed Support escalation playbook (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). Goal is to publish the Confluence pages early next week and start using the new severity + SLA language immediately for new escalations.

Docs to review:
- Escalation Playbook Refresh v0.9 (attached)
- Sign-off checklist (attached)
- Working notes (Drive): shared_drives/customer-success/escala...

Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>: Reviewed. Mostly good — one blocker + a couple nits.

BLOCKER:
- SLA table currently implies we page SRE for “P1 enterprise” by default even if impact is isolated to a single tenant with an available workaround.
 - Proposed wording change: make paging trigger “customer-impacting outage/degradation meeting Sev1+ thresholds” rather than “customer tier.” Tier should influence u...
