Hi Sam / Leah —

Thanks again for your time today. Quick recap from our conversation and proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 

Key pain points you shared
- Vendor lock-in: heavy reliance on proprietary API semantics (OpenAI function-calling + Anthropic chat formats) that make safe cutover brittle.
- Cost: ~8M tokens/month across gen + embeddings; current spend has been trending up with peak bursts.
- Latency & forecasting: p95 spikes during batch processing affecting UX and burst auto-scaling is unpredictable.
- Compliance: need for residency and audit logs (SOC2 + more granular access logs).

Current stack you described
- Primary generation: OpenAI API (chat completions + function-ca...

messages.subject: Re: API dependency map & success path — next steps for Open model migration
messages.body:
Thanks Rachel — this is a great summary. A few clarifications from our side: 

1) Token volumes: the 8M tokens/month is a blended figure. For generation alone it's closer to 6.2M, embeddings ~1.6M. We can share a traffic slice and a sample prompt set for the PoC.
2) Function-calling: our gateway currently uses the OpenAI function schema. We need to preserve the typed JSON outputs; losing structure during adapter translation is a non-starter.
3) Privacy: for our EU customers we’ll need a regionally isolated deployment for storing conversation history.

Engineers to join the deep-dive:
- Leah Chen (platform lead) — leah.chen@acmelabs.com
- Marco Ruiz (infra lead) — marco.ruiz@acmelabs.com (...

messages.subject: Re: API dependency map & success path — next steps for Open model migration
messages.body:
Appreciate the clarifications, Sam — that aligns with what we'll use for the PoC. Two quick items I added:

- PoC scope: 2-week pilot targeting the main chat flow (generation + function-calling + embeddings for intent matching). We’ll provide an adapter shim that preserves the OpenAI-style function schema so your gateway sees minimal changes.
- Data residency: we can propose a dedicated VPC/private deployment for EU historic data flows — engineering will outline the network and KMS options in the deep-dive.

I’ve scheduled the technical deep-dive for Wed Jul 7, 2026 at 09:30 PST — calendar invite sent to the list (please accept). Agenda attached in the invite; primary asks for your team: ...

Team —

I pulled together the attached retention cost model and a short checklist (see Drive link) to help us decide sequencing for the Q2 renewals across Datadog / Grafana Cloud / Sentry. High-level asks:

- Confirm contract end dates and any auto-renew windows you own (Hannah, you had a list last quarter).

Initial thoughts: if Datadog's retention add-on is significantly more expensive per TB than Grafana Cloud's logs tier, we should explore shifting archived logs to Grafana and keeping Datadog for metrics...

Quick summary from my side:

- Sentry renewal is 2026-05-15.

Recommendation: prioritize Datadog negotiation first (shortest notice). We should get an updated quote from them that separates ingest vs retention so we can compare apples-to-apples with Grafana. If Datadog /retain-add-on is >40% more /TB than Grafana, push to archive tier in Grafana but get a mock egress estimate.

Also: I’ll pull the usage distribution by service so we can see hot vs cold log ratios.

—Hannah

messages.subject: Re: Renewal sequencing & retention add-on options
messages.body:
Naomi / team —

Adding a couple of options to consider before we pull vendors into the room: 

1) Consolidate cold retention to Grafana Cloud (cheapest per TB) and keep Datadog for 14–30d hot logs + metrics/traces. Could cut Datadog bill ~25% to 40% but adds cross-vendor restore complexity.
2) Negotiate a blended rate with Datadog for retention if they can match Grafana within X% (we ask for 12 months pricing guarantee).
3) Explore short-term SRE-run compression/retention job to reduce peak ingest while we renegotiate (ops cost vs vendor savings).

Legal / procurement flags: Datadog's MSA has an outbound-data-export clause that can be expensive if egress is charged on their side — we shou...

We’ve attached our retention model (expected growth and current usage b...

1) Per-TB pricing (list price) — 30d: $120 / TB / month; 90d: $85 / TB / month; 365d: $42 / TB / month. We can provide a tiered volume discount above 50 TB / month.

4) Legal contact — cc’d is our commercial counsel (procurement@datadog.com). We can set up a call to walk through the outbound-data-export clause and any waiv...

Option A — Bridge (recommended for minimal paperwork)
- Duration: 3 weeks (starts on sign)
- Model: credit pool (prepaid) with graduated top-up pricing:

- Single invo...

If you can confirm those three items I can get PO approved and sign today. Prefer Option A and net 7 if possible.

messages.subject: Onboarding load verification protocol & timeline
messages.body:
Hi Alyssa —

Following our kickoff last week, attaching a first-pass load verification protocol for Skyline Health. Goal here is to make the acceptance gate explicit so your engineering team and ours know what success looks like for production rollout. High level:

- Acceptance targets (staging -> dedicated capacity):
 - Latency: p95 <= 220ms, p99 <= 480ms for typical 512-token prompts; tail measured as end-to-end API call (network + inference).
 - Throughput: sustained 120 RPS for 10 minutes on our dedicated staging pool; spike test to 240 RPS for 60s to validate autoscale/fallback.
 - Error budget: <= 0.5% 5xx rate during sustained tests.

- Test plan:
 1) Baseline single-client replay ...

messages.subject: Re: Onboarding load verification protocol & timeline
messages.body:
Thanks Dev — this looks thorough, appreciate the concrete targets. A few clarifying Qs from our side:

1) Do the acceptance counts toward our consumption? We need to know if the synthetic test traffic will be billed to our account or handled under Redwood test credits.
2) For the spike test to 240 RPS — will that require temporary capacity increase that we need to approve with our cloud team?
3) We have a small set of privacy constraints: any payload with patient-like tokens must be anonymized; I'll attach a short spreadsheet outlining fields we consider sensitive.

We can do the proposed window May 7 14:00 PT but Ops wants to run jointly. Can Marissa and Rachel join the run?

Attaching: ...

- Capacity spike: the 240 RPS spike will be executed using our dedicated staging pool; it does not require provisioning new production GPUs. If we need to do a larger stress test against your prod-like cluster, we'll run that separately with explicit sign-off.
- Privacy...

messages.body:
Team — quick sync on results from the May 7 run and a short remediation plan. Summary below; full CSV attached.

- Test summary: full ramp + spike + 2h soak completed (see attached load-results-20260509.csv).
 - Sustained 120 RPS: success. Mean latency ~180ms, p95 210ms, p99 510ms.
 - 60s spike to 240 RPS: recovered within 30s; brief tail increase to p99 ~820ms for ~45s during spike window.
 - Error rate: 0.22% 5xx (within acceptance).

- Observations:
 1) Cold KV cache runs (first 3 minutes) show a higher p99 (~900ms) — recommend we document cache-warm strategies and include a warm-up script in the deployment checklist.
 2) Some prompt variants with long output lengths caused tokenizatio...

Hi Soojin — thanks for the summary and the quick turnaround. A few clarifications from our side: 

- The 120M figure refers to a single-window peak across a 48h holiday promotion (not cumulative across month). Typical off-peak weeks are closer to 25–30M.
- We’re aiming for procurement approval for a pilot at the $800/1M target. Pilot would cover 40–50M tokens/month for 2 months.
- Critical ask: what happens if we breach burst caps during our pilot (automated claim flows)? We cannot afford hard throttling during promos.

Questions: 
1) Is the 48h soft-burst guarantee (3x) written into the hosted terms or is it an operational policy? We need explicit language for the pilot quote.
2) If over...

messages.subject: Re: Post-demo recap: expected token profile, budget, and burst/overage terms
messages.body:
Hi Priya — great, thanks for clarifying the single-window peak. Short answers below, then next steps.

Burst handling (short):
- The hosted soft-burst behavior (accept requests up to 3x committed sustained rate for up to 48h) is part of our operational policy and we will include explicit language in the pilot quote. That language can read: "Hosted: temporary burst allowance up to 3x committed sustained rate for a contiguous 48-hour window; overage tier pricing applies for sustained usage beyond that window. Redwood will provide rollbacks or throttling only when usage materially threatens overall region capacity."
- For pilot contracts we typically include a clause that gives Acme notice a...

messages.subject: Re: Post-demo recap: expected token profile, budget, and burst/overage terms
messages.body:
Hi Priya — quick technical note ahead of a short sync: 

- Based on the sample transcripts, a 10–15% reduction in effective tokens per call is achievable with prompt normalization and server-side prompt templates; that would move you closer to ~630–720 effective $/1M at the same list prices.
- For KV-cache and batching: our hosted runtime supports prefix caching for repeated system messages and continuous batching inside the same connection; we typically see 8–12% latency improvements and token savings when customers enable these patterns.
- Regarding surge protection: there are two mechanisms we recommend combined in pilot runs: soft-burst allowance (policy described by Soojin) plus a co...

messages.subject: Re: Post-demo recap: expected token profile, budget, and burst/overage terms
messages.body:
Thanks Soojin / Alex — this all looks good and aligns with procurement constraints. Two asks before we mark ready: 
1) Please send the formal pilot quote that includes: pilot pricing at $800/1M, explicit 48h soft-burst clause, per-event overage cap at 2x committed monthly spend, and a statement on quota increase lead time.
2) Schedule the 30-min tech sync (Tue/Wed next week preferred).

If you can get the DocuSign quote to me by EOD Friday, we’ll route for internal approval next week and can start the pilot as early as Apr 1.

Best,
Priya
VP Product | Acme Payments
priya@acme.io

messages.subject: Rate card for tool-calls & JSON output (need baseline)
messages.body:
Hi Marissa,

We're putting together an internal SOW for the Acme Analytics POC and need a clear baseline for hosted API costs. Two specific questions: 1) how is metering applied when we use function/tool calling (i.e., do the function arguments and returned JSON count toward output tokens?), and 2) does the structured JSON output mode (your "JSON mode") change the tokenization or charge model compared with plain text generation?

Our expected workload for the POC: 2M tokens in prompts per month, and ~3M tokens returned as model outputs across tool invocations (many short JSON objects, 150-400 bytes each). We may have bursts and want to understand if there's any per-call overhead or per-fu...

messages.subject: Re: Rate card for tool-calls & JSON output (need baseline)
messages.body:
Jordan — thanks for the clear ask and the sample usage file. I looped in Connor (SE) and Rachel (AE) so we can give a concise price + implementation note. High level up front:

- Baseline hosted pricing (public rate card, attached PDF): generation output $0.06 / 1K tokens, prompt/input $0.03 / 1K tokens.
- Function/tool calling: tokens consumed are the same token model — that includes the serialized JSON you send as arguments and the model's serialized JSON return. There is no separate per-call meter; costs are computed from total prompt + output tokens.
- JSON mode: structured-output mode asks the model to produce valid JSON and does not change the per-token pricing. However, structured ...

messages.subject: Re: Rate card for tool-calls & JSON output (need baseline)
messages.body:
Jordan — quick SE notes on the metering and optimization side:

1) What counts: everything you send to the model (prompt + serialized tool args + system messages + any schema examples) and everything the model returns (including JSON keys) are tokenized and billed. Calls that stream partial tokens are billed on the tokens emitted.

2) Per-call overhead: there is no fixed per-request fee for hosted API function calls. The only rounding is at token granularity; we bill at the token level (1K token buckets for pricing). So lots of tiny, <50-token responses can cause inefficiencies relative to batching.

3) Strategies to reduce tokens for structured outputs:
 - Use compact field names (short ...

messages.subject: Re: Rate card for tool-calls & JSON output (need baseline)
messages.body:
Thanks — this is useful. A few follow-ups:

1) Can you confirm whether function/tool calling counts toward any separate "invocation" metric on Redwood's billing dashboard? Our billing team wants to reconcile per-call counts vs token charges.
2) For the pilot we were aiming at a 20% off list price for 6 months (we're not ready to commit annual spend yet). Is that feasible?
3) If we later move to Dedicated, will the metering model change (e.g., flat-rate per-GPU vs token-based)?

If it's easier, we can jump on a short call. We're trying to finalize budget by end of month.

—Jordan

(Attachment: acme_tooling_usage_sample.csv — updated)

messages.subject: Re: Rate card for tool-calls & JSON output (need baseline)
messages.body:
Jordan — quick answers and an offer to move forward: 

1) Invocation counts: hosted billing dashboard currently shows both token usage and request counts (requests are for visibility only; there is no additional request surcharge). We can export daily invocation counts for your team to reconcile.

2) Discount: for a 6-month timeboxed POC we can approve a 15% discount off list with a $40k total minimum spend across the pilot (that gets your finance comfortable and lets us prioritize capacity). We cannot approve 20% without a longer (12-month) commit.

3) Dedicated: moving to Redwood Dedicated changes the commercial model — typically a committed GPU capacity (flat monthly commitment) plus a...

title: Intermittent streaming/chat idle disconnects — elevated 5xx/429 and client timeouts (executive attention)
body:
Tom Hayes <tom.hayes@brightlayer.ai>: Rachel —

We're seeing a surge in user errors starting ~14:45 UTC today: clients experience empty responses mid-stream and then a client-side timeout; on retry they immediately get 429s and then 5xx spikes. Our CS team pulled logs and attached a sample bundle. This is visible in our product funnel — chat sessions dropped ~18% during the window.

Can you confirm whether this is on your side (idle stream disconnects...

Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.ai>: Tom / Natalie —

Thanks for the bundle and heads up. We're looking immediately. Quick notes from an initial triage:
- Our observability shows a correlated spike of 429s and 5xxs starting 14:40 UTC across eu-west-1 and us-west-2 routing pools.
- There are signs of a retry amplification pattern: several clients are reconnecting repeatedly after short client-side timeouts leading to increased admissio...

normalized.subject: Intermittent streaming/chat idle disconnects — elevated 5xx/429 and client timeouts (executive attention)

messages.subject: Re: Persistent client timeouts / idle disconnects on streaming chat
messages.body:
Rachel —

We're seeing a surge in user errors starting ~14:45 UTC today: clients experience empty responses mid-stream and then a client-side timeout; on retry they immediately get 429s and then 5xx spikes. Our CS team pulled logs and attached a sample bundle. This is visible in our product funnel — chat sessions dropped ~18% during the window.

Can you confirm whether this is on your side (idle stream disconnects) or something in the SDK that might be aggressively retrying? We're in the middle of a paid pilot and this is customer-visible.

- Tom
BrightLayer — Senior PM

Attachments: brightlayer_streaming_log_bundle_20260409.zip (log bundle, 12MB)

messages.subject: Re: Persistent client timeouts / idle disconnects on streaming chat
messages.body:
Tom / Natalie —

Thanks for the bundle and heads up. We're looking immediately. Quick notes from an initial triage:
- Our observability shows a correlated spike of 429s and 5xxs starting 14:40 UTC across eu-west-1 and us-west-2 routing pools.
- There are signs of a retry amplification pattern: several clients are reconnecting repeatedly after short client-side timeouts leading to increased admission pressure.
- We're prioritizing mitigation (rate limiting per-connection, temporary circuit-breakers for noisy clients) while engineering drills in on root cause.

I'll keep you posted every 30m; assigning Connor (serving runtime lead) and Marissa (CSL + account lead). If this is causing exec-l...

messages.subject: Re: Persistent client timeouts / idle disconnects on streaming chat
messages.body:
Short internal triage notes:

On the serving side we observed: p50 latency ~stable; p95/p99 token latency increased starting 14:36 UTC. Admission queue depth climbed and we returned 429 for several connection pools. The 5xxs are mainly RPC timeouts in the tokenizer/kvcache layer — stack traces attached from one pod.

Hypothesis: a subset of streaming clients that use small idle timeouts (~3s) are closing the stream while the server still sends keepalive frames. Clients then retry aggressively; this creates a transient feedback loop and bursts load on specific GPUs.

Immediate actions (executing now):
- Apply conservative per-API-key rate limit for BrightLayer to throttle retries.
- Enable...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Persistent client timeouts / idle disconnects on streaming chat
messages.body:
Tom —

We're applying a temporary throttle for your API key and enabling the server-side 10s idle grace as Connor described. You should see fewer empty responses and reduced client-side timeouts in the next 5–10 minutes.

We'll keep this throttle until we confirm the retry storm has cooled and we have a permanent mitigation. We'll also share a short KB for your engineers on recommended SDK backoff settings.

Apologize for the disruption; we'll loop in leadership if this impacts SLAs further.

— Marissa, Customer Success

Attachments: p50-p95-graph-20260409.png (latency chart)

messages.subject: Re: Persistent client timeouts / idle disconnects on streaming chat
messages.body:
Thanks — we see the throttle applied and the spike is coming down. One ask: can we get an exec-level note for our CTO — they're tracking incident impact for the pilot board tomorrow.

Also, we can push a client-side change to add jitter/backoff, but need clear recommended settings.

- Tom

cc: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Persistent client timeouts / idle disconnects on streaming chat
messages.body:
Tom —

We're drafting an exec brief now (one-pager) summarizing impact, root cause hypothesis, and mitigation steps (temporary throttle, server idle grace, SDK backoff). Will include recommended SDK settings: initial_delay=250ms, max_delay=5s, jitter=0.2, max_retries=3 for streaming reconnect attempts.

I'll send the brief and the KB within 60 minutes. If your CTO wants a quick sync, I'm available for a 15m call.

— Rachel

to: Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Incident follow-up: BrightLayer streaming idle-disconnect incident (INC-1456)
messages.body:
Internal follow-up notes for the record:

Summary: BrightLayer experienced client-visible chat session drops and a retry amplification pattern that led to elevated 429/5xx rates 14:36–16:10 UTC on 2026-04-09. We applied per-key throttle and a 10s idle grace window; the SDK backoff hotfix reduced reconnection rate and steady-state 429s dropped to baseline.

Root cause (provisional): Client-side idle timeouts (~3s) + SDK optimistic immediate retries -> retry amplification. Server admitted requests until resource pressure produced tokenization timeouts and 5xxs.

Action items (owner / ETA):
- SDK: release 0.9.3 with stricter streaming reconnects and longer backoff default (Connor, 2026-04-12...

title: Sealed-Key Ingress & Operator Proxy Flow — deployment review
body:
InfraOps Solutions <ops@infraops.com>: Hi Alex,

We're kicking off the on-prem deployment for the Acme pilot and want to validate one piece of the design: how sealed customer key material enters the private VPC and how operator accounts interact with it during normal ops and incident recovery.

High level asks:
- Confirm that sealed key blobs never transit an untrusted plane (no plaintext key material over public links).
- Describe the operator proxy f...

Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>: Jamie —

Thanks for the diagram. We can take this on. High-level notes before a deeper pass:

1) Sealed-key ingress: we recommend customers generate keys in their HSM/KMS and export a sealed blob encrypted to a Redwood public wrapping key (RSA-OAEP or ECIES) that we provide per-deployment. That blob is transported over TLS to the customer VPC only; our control plane never stores unwrapped k...

messages.subject: Sealed key ingress — quick design review request
messages.body:
Hi Alex,

We're kicking off the on-prem deployment for the Acme pilot and want to validate one piece of the design: how sealed customer key material enters the private VPC and how operator accounts interact with it during normal ops and incident recovery.

High level asks:
- Confirm that sealed key blobs never transit an untrusted plane (no plaintext key material over public links).
- Describe the operator proxy flow for wrapping/unwrapping service-account tokens.
- Outline rotation / emergency unseal steps and what operators can and cannot do.

Can your team review and confirm by EOD tomorrow? We've attached our preliminary flow diagram.

Thanks,
Jamie Reed
InfraOps Solutions

messages.subject: Re: Sealed key ingress — quick design review request
messages.body:
Jamie —

Thanks for the diagram. We can take this on. High-level notes before a deeper pass:

1) Sealed-key ingress: we recommend customers generate keys in their HSM/KMS and export a sealed blob encrypted to a Redwood public wrapping key (RSA-OAEP or ECIES) that we provide per-deployment. That blob is transported over TLS to the customer VPC only; our control plane never stores unwrapped key material.

2) Operator proxy flow: operators will authenticate via short-lived service accounts that are wrapped by a hardware-bound proxy (operator-proxy service) which has no ability to unwrap customer key material. Unwrap operations must be gated by a multi-party approval (MFA + manager approval) ...

cc: Jamie Reed <ops@infraops.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Sealed key ingress — quick design review request
messages.body:
Attaching a concise ops flow and the API call mapping. Summary below; see attached PDF for diagram and sequence numbers. (attachment: sealed-key-proxy-flow.pdf (pdf))

- Step 1: Customer KMS GenerateKey (CMK) inside their HSM -> call ExportWrappedKey(public_wrapping_key_redwood). Redwood provides ephemeral per-deployment public keys; customer stores the sealed blob in their private S3 inside the VPC or pushes directly to Redwood agent via mTLS.

- Step 2: Ingress endpoint accepts the sealed blob over mTLS to a customer-facing agent inside the VPC. The agent stores the sealed blob in local encrypted storage (never unwrapped).

- Step 3: When application needs to use the key, the local agen...

messages.subject: Re: Sealed key ingress — quick design review request
messages.body:
Thanks — adding a few specifics on rotation and emergency retrieval (attachment: operator-rotation-matrix.xlsx (xlsx)). Key points:

- Rotation cadence: recommend quarterly CMK rotation for high-sensitivity customers, semi-annual otherwise. Rotation steps: create new CMK, export wrapped blob to agent, stage rewrap of DEKs, validation traffic, final cutover.

- Emergency retrieval (escape hatch): define an "Incident Escrow" that stores an additional sealed copy of the CMK to an escrow HSM controlled by the customer's legal entity and 1 Redwood auditor key. Retrieval is only allowed with: (a) documented incident ticket, (b) 2 operator custodians present, (c) executive sign-off. We explicitl...

to: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa.morgan@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Sealed key ingress — quick design review request
messages.body:
Alex / team —

This is excellent and aligns with Acme's compliance goals. A couple of clarifications before we sign off:
- Can you provide the exact CSR/public key format and lifetime for the Redwood per-deployment wrapping key?
- We need a clear audit export path (signed bundle) that Acme can ingest into Splunk; can you confirm retention defaults and HMAC signing method?

We'll pull the attached rotation matrix into our runbook and run the smoke tests next week. Once Hannah adds the acceptance steps we'll schedule a short call for sign-off.

Thanks,
Jamie
InfraOps Solutions

body:
Tim Jensen <tim.jensen@clearbank.com>: Hi Rachel,

Thanks for the demo last week — ClearBank's infra team raised two items on the SLA and maintenance language that we need to surface before procurement will sign:

1) Maintenance windows — ClearBank is highly regulated and cannot accept routine maintenance between 07:00-19:00 ET. We need scheduled maintenance confined to a 02:00-06:00 ET window (and a maximum 4-hour window per event).
2) Service credits...

Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.ai>: Hi Tim — appreciate the clear ask and the PDF.

Short answer: we can get most of the way there but need to confirm a couple of operational tradeoffs with ops and legal. Two quick points up front:
- We can restrict routine maintenance to a narrow overnight window on a per-region basis, but some global upgrades require staggered windows; we can commit to a maximum 4-hour scheduled window per event a...

messages.subject: SLA concerns — maintenance windows & credits
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks for the demo last week — ClearBank's infra team raised two items on the SLA and maintenance language that we need to surface before procurement will sign:

1) Maintenance windows — ClearBank is highly regulated and cannot accept routine maintenance between 07:00-19:00 ET. We need scheduled maintenance confined to a 02:00-06:00 ET window (and a maximum 4-hour window per event).
2) Service credits — request a clearer staircase for uptime below 99.99, and a minimum floor (we propose 99.95 as the baseline with credits increasing at 0.1% decrements).

Can you confirm whether Redwood can commit to tighter blackout windows and the proposed credit formula? I attached a short PD...

messages.subject: Re: SLA concerns — maintenance windows & credits
messages.body:
Hi Tim — appreciate the clear ask and the PDF.

Short answer: we can get most of the way there but need to confirm a couple of operational tradeoffs with ops and legal. Two quick points up front:
- We can restrict routine maintenance to a narrow overnight window on a per-region basis, but some global upgrades require staggered windows; we can commit to a maximum 4-hour scheduled window per event and 72 hours' notice for non-emergent changes.
- For credits, Marissa (cc'd) will propose language that maps to a credit staircase; I expect Redwood to accept a 99.95 floor for the Order Form with a clarified credit calculation rather than the 99.99 absolute floor you proposed.

I'm attaching our ...

messages.subject: INTERNAL: Draft redlines — ClearBank MSA & Order Form (SLA/support)
messages.body:
Team — attaching tracked changes for the MSA + Order Form focusing on the SLA/support section. Key legal positions below (high level):

- Uptime: propose Order Form commitment of 99.95% monthly uptime for the production service in the primary region. We will not accept a hard 99.99% guarantee without a separate Dedicated commitment (higher tier).

- Credits: implement a staircase that pays credits as a percentage of monthly service fees: 99.95–99.0 => 10%; 99.0–97.5 => 25%; <97.5 => 50%. Credits capped at 100% of monthly fees for the affected service. We documented calculation examples in Appendix A.

- Response times: P1 acknowledged within 15 minutes, P1 remediation target 4 hours for i...

messages.subject: Re: SLA concerns — maintenance windows & credits
messages.body:
Tim / Claire — thanks for the patience while we looped ops and legal. Below is a concise summary of Redwood's counterproposal to address ClearBank's compliance concerns. The attached PDF is the clean Order Form with the SLA section called out.

Summary of counterproposal (high level):
- Uptime: 99.95% monthly in the primary region (we can discuss a higher commitment under a Dedicated capacity addendum).
- Credit staircase: 99.95–99.0 => 10% credit; 99.0–97.5 => 25%; <97.5 => 50%; credit capped at 100% of monthly subscription for the impacted service. Exact formulas/examples in the attached.
- Maintenance: routine maintenance scheduled to 02:00–06:00 ET (max 4 hours/event) with 72 hours' a...

messages.subject: Re: SLA concerns — maintenance windows & credits
messages.body:
Rachel — thanks, that summary helps. Procurement will review the clean Order Form and the credit staircase. I am looping procurement@clearbank.com to pick up DocuSign and run internal approvals. We may come back with one narrow final redline on the emergency maintenance notification SLA but that's procedural rather than substantive.

I'll aim to get procurement's input by EOD Monday. In the meantime, please send the procurement packet (we require a completed procurement_form.pdf attached).

Regards,
Tim
Attachment: procurement_form.pdf (PDF, 64 KB)

body:
Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>: Hi Amit — great to meet today, and thanks for the time. Quick recap from our 30-min call and proposed next steps so we keep momentum: 

Call highlights
- ClearWave goal: reduce inference cost for clinical NLP pipelines while keeping p99 latency under 150ms for triage workloads.
- Current infra: mixed cloud GPUs + some on-prem for PHI; security gate (SOC2 + NHS-style DPA) is mandatory.
- Trial wish-list: 2-week pil...

Amit Patel <amit.patel@clearwavehealth.com>: Thanks Rachel — great summary. A couple quick items: 

1) Economic buyer: I don't hold the budget for enterprise-wide infra. The finance owner is Lisa Breno (cc'd). She owns procurement and approval for vendor spend above $50k/year. If you can send a short one-pager aimed at the finance audience (cost/ROI), that would help.
2) Security: procurement@clearwavehealth.com handles the vendor secu...

Hi Amit — great to meet today, and thanks for the time. Quick recap from our 30-min call and proposed next steps so we keep momentum: 

Call highlights
- ClearWave goal: reduce inference cost for clinical NLP pipelines while keeping p99 latency under 150ms for triage workloads.
- Current infra: mixed cloud GPUs + some on-prem for PHI; security gate (SOC2 + NHS-style DPA) is mandatory.

Open asks / next steps
1) Economic buyer: can you introduce the person who owns the budget/purchasing authority? We want to understand procurement cadence and a...

messages.subject: Re: Follow-up: Recap + path to econ buyer & procurement contacts
messages.body:
Thanks Rachel — great summary. A couple quick items: 

1) Economic buyer: I don't hold the budget for enterprise-wide infra. The finance owner is Lisa Breno (cc'd). She owns procurement and approval for vendor spend above $50k/year. If you can send a short one-pager aimed at the finance audience (cost/ROI), that would help.
2) Security: procurement@clearwavehealth.com handles the vendor security intake. We use a standard questionnaire and require SOC2 Type II and a DPA for PHI. Expect a handful of legal redlines around data residency.
3) Pilot timeline: week of Mar 15 works for us — but I will need Lisa's sign-off on the trial scope (budget/time commitment).

Happy to jump on a 20-min mut...

messages.subject: Re: Follow-up: Recap + path to econ buyer & procurement contacts
messages.body:
Hi Lisa — nice to be connected. Per Amit: short ask below, and two suggestions to move the procurement/security conversation forward quickly.

Quick asks for Lisa (economic buyer / procurement):
- Can you confirm budget approval thresholds and whether a pilot under $10k needs formal PO?
- If helpful, I can summarize the pilot ROI in an executive one-pager (expected delta in throughput/cost) by EOD tomorrow.

Suggested low-friction steps
1) Security intake: Rachel to drop a single-slide SOC2 + data-flow summary and the test dataset handling plan into procurement@clearwavehealth.com. Vivek to be on standby for any technical follow-up.
2) Pilot contract: propose a 2-week non-production pilot...

messages.subject: Re: Follow-up: Recap + path to econ buyer & procurement contacts
messages.body:
Quick internal — I reviewed the MAP and pilot ask. Given ClearWave's profile and potential ARR, we can approve a concessional pilot price to remove friction here. 

- Approver: Marissa (rev ops) — authorize up to $8,500 (pilot credit) for two-week pilot if legal confirms DPA scope.
- Condition: must be limited to product evaluation (no production PHI processing without DPA signed).

Go ahead and send the exec one-pager and SOC2 slide. If Lisa asks for a PO, propose the $8,500 credit against first-year consumption so it’s reconciled during contract signature.

Marissa
VP, Revenue Operations — Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai

messages.subject: Re: Intro + Demo: Redwood Inference
messages.body:
Hi Alex —

Thanks for following up and for the deck. I shared it with Mark (analytics lead) and our Director of Product. After an internal prioritization exercise this morning, we’ve decided to pause the LLM/inference initiative for Q2–Q3. The product roadmap was reshuffled to prioritize a core data platform migration and a set of compliance deliverables; most of the teams who would evaluate inference are tied up with those efforts or traveling for customer workshops.

Given that context, we should pause the demo for now — we don’t want to take up your time while we’re blocked on resourcing. I expect we’ll revisit inference in Q4 when roadmap cycles open again.

If you want to stay on our...

messages.subject: Re: Intro + Demo: Redwood Inference
messages.body:
Thanks Emily —

Completely understand and thanks for the clarity. We’ll pause on scheduling. If it’s helpful I can:

- Send a one-page technical brief focused on quick wins (prefix caching, batching profiles) tailored to your infra constraints,
- Share a short cost/latency snapshot for your expected Q4 pilot size, and
- Set a single tentative outreach in mid-September (no calendar holds, just a note to re-open conversation).

Would you prefer the brief or the snapshot first? Also please confirm whether I can keep the HubSpot opportunity open with a Q4 note (SOF-2026-001) or whether you’d like me to close it and re-open later.

Thanks again — Laura and I are happy to accommodate whatever c...

messages.body:
Team — forwarding the thread from Emily / SofiaTech. TL;DR: prospect deprioritized inference for Q2/Q3 due to platform migration and travel. They asked we pause scheduling and offered to revisit in Q4.

Notes / suggested next steps:
- Mark the HubSpot opp (SOF-2026-001) as "stalled — roadmap reprioritized", set a reminder for 2026-09-15 to re-engage. (HubSpot: HS-48291)
- Send a one-page technical brief now (Alex) to seed Q4 planning packet — one pager to include: recommended pilot size, expected latency/cost delta for hosted vs dedicated, and two customer examples.
- No credits/discounts suggested now; keep outreach light. If leadership wants to extend a time-limited trial offer to keep ...

title: Redlines: SLA carveouts, service credits & maintenance windows (BluePeak)
body:
Daniel Cho <daniel.cho@bluepeak.ai>: Hi Rachel,

Attached is our redline to the MSA + Order Form focused on the SLA and Support sections. High-level asks from Procurement and Legal:

messages.subject: Redlines: MSA & Order Form (SLA)
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Attached is our redline to the MSA + Order Form focused on the SLA and Support sections. High-level asks from Procurement and Legal:

- Uptime target: asking for 99.95% for production API endpoints (we currently see 99.9% in your draft).
- Service credits: request credits calculated as a percentage of monthly fees with a floor (min) and a cap. They proposed a table tied to minutes of downtime.
- Response times: require P1 response within 15 minutes and P2 within 2 hours during business hours.
- Maintenance windows: asking for a 2-hour weekly window with no chargebacks; emergency maintenance only with 24-hour notice where possible.

We attached BluePeak_MSA_SLA_redline.pdf with...

to: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Fwd: Redlines: MSA & Order Form (SLA)
messages.body:
Folks — forwarding BluePeak's redline. Key asks above. They want lower uptime tolerance (99.95), table-based credits, faster P1/P2 response times, and a very tight maintenance request.

Quick questions for alignment before I reply to Daniel/Anna:
- Ops: do we have operational constraints that make 99.95 for hosted API (shared) untenable? I think our current SLA for hosted tiers was 99.9.
- Pricing: how painful is their proposed credit table vs a simpler pro-rata credit?
- Legal: any non-starter wording in the redline (maintenance carveouts or audit obligations)?

Propose we push back with: 1) keep 99.9 for hosted, 99.95 for dedicated (if applicable); 2) counter with a pro-rata daily credi...

messages.subject: Redlines: MSA & Order Form (SLA)
messages.body: (see above)

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Redlines: MSA & Order Form (SLA)
messages.body:
Thanks Rachel — quick operational counter-proposal below (happy to iterate).

Suggested positions for customer-facing counter:
- Uptime: 99.9% for Hosted API (shared pool); 99.95% for Dedicated instances / isolated capacity. Rationale: shared multi-tenant workloads have more variance; dedicated pools have stricter guarantees.
- Credits: replace their table with a tiered pro-rata model: credit = (minutes of downtime / total monthly minutes) * monthly fee for the affected service, subject to a monthly cap of 30% of fees for that service. No per-incident minimum credit.
- Response times: commit to P1 initial response within 30 minutes (24/7), P2 within 4 hours (business hours) with an escala...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Redlines: MSA & Order Form (SLA)
messages.body:
Legal notes / redline guidance — drafting points I want in the counter redline:

- Keep uptime language measurable: specify measurement methodology (rolling 30-day window, exclude scheduled maintenance windows, define metric endpoints and ingress hosts).
- For credits, avoid absolute tables tied to minutes; the pro-rata monthly model Marissa suggested is cleaner and easier to implement. Define the cap (30%) and an annual cap on cumulative credits if needed.
- Response SLA: we can accept a 30-min P1 initial response as standard and offer 15-min for Dedicated at higher commitment/price. Avoid committing to fix times (MTTR) — use acknowledgment and escalation steps.
- Maintenance notice: inc...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly_park@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom_das@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Redlines: MSA & Order Form (SLA)
messages.body:
Daniel / Anna — thanks for the redline and the clear asks. We reviewed this internally and attached a short counter-redline (Redwood_counter_sla_redline.pdf) addressing your concerns while reflecting our operational constraints. Summary of changes in our counterproposal: 

- Uptime: 99.9% for Redwood Hosted API (shared). 99.95% for Dedicated/Reserved deployments.
- Credits: pro-rata monthly credit calculation in place of the proposed minute-table; credit capped at 30% of monthly fees for the impacted service in any billing month.
- Response times: P1 initial response committed to 30 minutes (24/7). P2 initial response within 4 business hours; Dedicated customers can opt-in to a 15-minute ...

messages.subject: Redwood POC kickoff — preferred dates and throughput questions
messages.body:
Hi Kimberly,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday — AppliedAI's engineering team is excited to move to a hands-on POC. We'd like to lock a kickoff that includes: prompt review, routing walkthrough, and an initial load/throughput run.

Quick items from our side:
- Preferred kickoff week: May 18–21 (we're flexible on exact day)
- Target throughput to validate: ~150 QPS across mixed prompt sizes (short 128 tokens / long 1024 tokens)
- Latency target: p95 ~ 250ms for short prompts, 800ms for long prompts (subject to hardware)

Can your team confirm availability those days and whether Redwood can run a 2-hour steady-state load followed by a 30-minute spike test? Also please share any runbook o...

cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Redwood POC kickoff — preferred dates and throughput questions
messages.body:
Alex — thanks for the clear targets, that helps a lot. We're available May 19 or May 20 for a 3-hour hands-on window; proposed agenda below:

Agenda (90–180 min window)
- 0:00–0:20 Intro + success criteria (sales + infra)
- 0:20–0:45 Prompt review & routing rules (product)
- 0:45–1:30 Test harness setup + steady-state run (engineering)
- 1:30–2:00 Spike test + debrief

We're happy to run the steady-state 2-hour run if you'd prefer a longer validation — we can extend after the initial session. Vivek will lead the harness setup; Rachel will cover routing and fallback policies. Aditya will be there for commercial/next-steps.

Attached is a short runbook (poc-runbook.pdf) showing what we need...

messages.subject: Re: Redwood POC kickoff — preferred dates and throughput questions
messages.body:
Kimberly — thanks, May 20 afternoon works best for us (starting 13:00 PT).

Attendees (tentative):
- Alex Gomez (Platform)
- Priya Menon (Backend Eng) <priya.menon@appliedai.io>
- Samir Khan (SRE) <samir.khan@appliedai.io>
- Procurement rep: procurement@appliedai.io

We plan to use a JMeter-based harness with a distributed runner. We can supply the JMX and a sample load profile (steady 150 QPS for 90 min, then a 5x spike for 10 minutes). If Redwood prefers the 2-hour steady-state you mentioned, we can extend after the hands-on.

Also attaching a draft SOW for procurement to review (appliedai-sow-draft.pdf). On the latency targets: those are aggressive for the long prompts — are you able t...

messages.subject: Re: Redwood POC kickoff — preferred dates and throughput questions
messages.body:
Alex / Kimberly — quick technical note from infra:

- For a mixed workload (128 / 1024 tokens) targeting p95 250ms for short prompts and ~800ms for long ones, we recommend:
 1) Model variant: opt-quant-v2 (mixed quant) or a higher-memory fp16 instance if you need lower latency on long prompts.
 2) Use prefix/KV caching for repeated prompt prefixes during the hands-on.
 3) Harness: JMeter is fine but we'll need the JMX and CSV prompt set; our runner expects a compressed tar with prompts and an env sample (see attached redwood-test-harness-config.yaml).

- For load shape: steady-state 90–120 minutes + staged spike is the best for an initial validation. If you want us to run the full 2-hour ...

to: Alex Gomez <alex.gomez@appliedai.io>, Priya Menon <priya.menon@appliedai.io>, Samir Khan <samir.khan@appliedai.io>
cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwoodinference.com>, Procurement <procurement@appliedai.io>
messages.subject: Confirmed: May 20 13:00 PT — POC kickoff + hands-on load session (calendar invite)
messages.body:
Thanks everyone — confirmed for May 20, 13:00–16:00 PT. Calendar invite sent to included attendees. Final notes / ask list before the session:

1) Files we need from AppliedAI (please upload to the Drive link before 18:00 PT on May 19):
 - JMeter JMX + CSV prompts (named prompts.csv)
 - Sample API key (scoped, short TTL) or a placeholder we can swap during the session
 - Trusted IPs for runner whitelisting

2) Success criteria (we'll record these at start):
 - Steady-state: sustain 150 QPS mixed workload for 90 minutes with p95 <= 300ms for short prompts
 - Spike: observe behavior under 5x burst and validate graceful degradation + fallback routing

3) Logistics: join Zoom (link in calenda...

Hi Monica + Nikhil,

Sharing the proposed docs IA update for Quickstart Templates ahead of implementation. Goal is to make templates discoverable from the top nav without duplicating SDK docs, and to make Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private guidance visible but not overwhelming.

Proposed nav placement (high level):
- Build
 - Quickstart Templates (NEW)
 - RAG + Agents (Python)
 - RAG + Agents (TypeScript)

 - Observability (Templates)

- Reference

Key behavior:
- Each template page is “do the thing” oriented: setup, run locally, deploy options, then ops hooks (metrics/traces/logs) + cost controls.
- ...

messages.subject: Re: Docs IA changes for Quickstart Templates (review)
messages.body:
This looks good overall.

A few DevEx notes (trying to keep it tight for v1):
- +1 to putting Troubleshooting under the Templates section. Based on internal beta, people don’t scroll past the README; we should make “common errors” one click away.
- “RAG + Agents” naming: can we avoid the word “Agents” in the nav label, and keep it on the page itself? We’ve had confusion with folks assuming this is a Redwood product feature. Maybe nav: “RAG (with tools)” or “RAG + Tool Calling”.
- Template CLI: recommend we keep it as “Template CLI (optional)” in copy. We’re shipping init + doctor, but we don’t want users to assume “upgrade” exists yet.
- Observability: please include a small “How to verif...

messages.subject: Re: Docs IA changes for Quickstart Templates (review)
messages.body:
Thanks Elliot.

From Hosted API PM perspective, +1 to this structure. A couple of comments:

1) We should be explicit about what success looks like on the template landing page.
- e.g. “In ~10 minutes you’ll have: (a) a running app, (b) retrieval working on sample docs, (c) streaming responses, (d) traces + metrics in your local collector / Console.”

2) Please add a short note on headers/telemetry tags.
- We’ve had questions about whether tagging template-origin traffic is required.
- The current stance is: opt-in only + no user content in tags.
- If you can link to the observability page (or add a FAQ on the template landing page), that will prevent confusion.

3) Dedicated/Private: I a...

messages.subject: Re: Docs IA changes for Quickstart Templates (review)
messages.body:
Super helpful.

Action items I’m taking:
- Rename nav label to reduce “Agents” confusion.
 - Proposal: “RAG + Tools (Python)” and “RAG + Tools (TypeScript)” in nav.
 - On-page H1 can still say “RAG + Agent Loop” with a short definition + disclaimer.
- Add a “What you’ll build” checklist to the Templates landing page (Nikhil #1).
- Add an FAQ snippet re: telemetry tags/headers (opt-in, no content) + link to Observability page (Nikhil #2).
- Add “Dedicated/Private notes” section template to each walkthrough (Nikhil #3).
- Add “CLI is optional; init + doctor only” callout (Monica).
- Add “verify observability is working” checklist (Monica).

Alex: FYI we’re converging on scope; no major disa...

messages.subject: Re: Docs IA changes for Quickstart Templates (review)
messages.body:
Sounds right.

Strong preference to avoid “Agents” in the left nav for v1. We can keep the term on the page but should define it plainly (“an example loop that calls tools; not a managed product feature”).

Also: please ensure Troubleshooting includes the top 3 support drivers we’ve seen in internal beta:
- 401 / key not set
- 429 / rate limits (and how to dial concurrency down)
- OTel collector not receiving traces (docker compose / endpoint mismatch)

Thanks for keeping this moving.

Alex

—
Alex Martinez
Head of Developer Experience
Redwood Inference

---

messages.subject: Re: Docs IA changes for Quickstart Templates (review)
messages.body:
Got it.

I’ll make sure those three issues are the first section on Troubleshooting, plus we’ll add short “symptom / cause / fix” blocks so it’s skimmable.

Also updating nav labels today to “RAG + Tools” as discussed.

Elliot

---

messages.subject: Re: Docs IA changes for Quickstart Templates (review)
messages.body:
One more tiny suggestion: on the “RAG + Tools” pages, can we add a short callout under prerequisites like:

“Security note: Don’t paste API keys into source files; use env vars. Logs are redacted by default but you should avoid logging user prompts.”

This keeps us aligned with the security review notes without adding a separate page.

Otherwise good to go from my side.

Monica

4) Marissa will coordinate the operations handoff and verify capacity/SLAs for your committed tier.

We’ll aim to get the initial package to you by Monday EOD (PST). In the meantime, I uploaded a ...

messages.subject: Re: Integration kickoff: SDK operational handoff & runbook requests
messages.body:
Hi Sam —

Dropping in the technical guidance bits you asked for. High level recommendations first, then example snippets.

Recommended runtime knobs (opinionated):
- Connection timeouts: 3s connect, 60s read for standard generation calls.
- Streaming: use chunked streaming with heartbeat pings every 30s; reconnect with resumed offset if network interrupted.
- Retries: idempotent-only 429/5xx retries with exponential backoff (base 200ms, factor 2, max 10s), max 5 attempts.
- Jitter: add ±30% jitter to avoid synchronized retries during bursts.
- Circuit breaker: open after 20 consecutive 5xx or timeouts for 60s; then probe with 1 request per 5s.
- Token refresh: refresh tokens 2m before exp...

messages.subject: Re: Integration kickoff: SDK operational handoff & runbook requests
messages.body:
Thanks Monica — this is very helpful. A couple quick clarifying items: 

1) Token expiry: our platform currently issues long-lived tokens (24h). Can we keep those or do you require short-lived? If we must rotate tokens more frequently, we need to scope engineering work on our side.
2) Rate limits: can you confirm baseline concurrent requests per minute for our committed tier? We need a hard number for our throttler.
3) KV cache behavior: for streaming/prefix caching (we do conversation threading) — is the KV cache sticky to a session token or to a model shard? We worry about stale cache during canary.

Also appreciate the canary percentages — we’ll plan ingestion throttles accordingly.

—...

messages.subject: Re: Integration kickoff: SDK operational handoff & runbook requests
messages.body:
Hi Sam —

Short answers here and a few concrete items we’ll include in the runbook: 

1) Tokens: short-lived tokens are recommended but not mandatory. For your plan we can accept 24h tokens for the pilot; for production we recommend rotating to 1h tokens with a refresh endpoint. We’ll document refresh endpoints and provide sample code.

2) Rate limits: for your committed tier (DEAL-8342) baseline is 600 requests/minute (aggregate across your API keys) with burst allowance to 1200 rpm for 2 minutes. We’ll surface per-route quotas in the Console and provide a webhook for 429 alerts.

3) KV cache: cache is per-model-variant and sticky at the request-key + model-variant level. During canary, ...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: [INC-20260414] Observed latency/timeouts — verification requested
messages.body:
Hi all,

We completed the initial telemetry review for INC-20260414. Summary below:

- Confirmed elevated error rate originating from the inference pool serving RG-4982 between 09:03:18 UTC and 09:27:53 UTC (25m 35s).
- Root cause: a degraded KV cache broker caused a delayed cache eviction cascade, increasing per-request compute and triggering queuing on a subset of nodes.
- Partial mitigation: automated fallback routing triggered at ~09:07 UTC and progressively reduced the error surface by rerouting 60% of traffic to warm replicas. Complete normalization occurred at ~09:27:53 UTC.

Measurement notes (how we count impact minutes):
- Per our SLA, we count minutes where successful request r...

messages.body:

Thanks for the report and for your patience while our SREs investigated. Per Aditya's analysis, Redwood confirms an elevated error period on April 14 and a short window of full unavailability (09:03:18–09:07:05 UTC). The full timeline and metric slices are in the attached incident stub and the RCA draft: https://drive.redwood.example/r/INC-20260414-rca-draft (internal link).

Credit outcome:
- Per Section 4.1 of the Master Service Agreement (MSA) and our standard credit calculation, the measured full-unavailability window results in a small prorated service credit.
- We'll apply a prorated credit equal to 0.01% of Greenleaf's APRIL invoice (rounded to the near...

Vivek — looped in our engineering/product and legal buddies below. Quick technical notes for the draft exhibit and runbook:

- Evidence types: we should enumerate (a) encrypted snapshot of model weights/config (sealed, hash-signed), (b) runtime image (container manifest + image digest), (c) KV cache dump for on-prem incidents, (d) structured audit logs (JSON) with retention windows.
- Snapshot cadence: recommended default = quarterly sealed deposit. For regulated customers offer monthly rotation as an SLA add-on.

- Liability/Forensics carveout language located in Section 3 of the exhibit; we limited Redwood obligations to signed manifests and transport attestations.

- custody_runbook_draft.docx (edita...

messages.subject: Re: Evaluation assets + timeline request
messages.body:
Elena / Rachel —

Quick security/ops checklist for the MAP:
- Credentialing: we will issue a time-limited API key scoped to the evaluation project; key TTL = 30 days.
- Network: support both short-lived API keys and optional VPC peering. If you prefer peering, we need an initial CIDR and contact for SRE.
- Audit: token-level usage logs will be available for the evaluation period and retained per the MAP (we can export a CSV).
- Data handling: promptpack will be synthetically augmented; we will sign an attestation that no PII from Redwood customers is included.

If you share the SRE contact, we can schedule a short 30-min integration call tomorrow.

Karthik Iyer
Head of Platform Security, ...

messages.subject: Re: Evaluation assets + timeline request
messages.body:
Thanks, Rachel and Karthik — this looks good. A few procurement/legal asks before we can sign off on distribution of artifacts to our SRE/dev teams: 

1) Can Redwood confirm the exact revocation mechanism (API key revoke + confirmation email + capability to rotate any issued tokens)?
2) We need the MAP to include explicit acceptance criteria for the NLU intents (F1 thresholds) and a perf/latency target for tail p95 so our platform team can evaluate cost/throughput.
3) Please add a clause that derivative datasets created by BrightCart during evaluation will be used only for assessment and will be deleted at the end of the 30-day window; we will supply a deletion log back to Redwood.

I'll ...

messages.subject: Re: Evaluation assets + timeline request — acceptance gates and sample metrics
messages.body:
Elena —

Thanks for the feedback. I updated the MAP (see attached) to include:
- Acceptance criteria: pass rates and sample F1 thresholds for the NLU intents (we propose baseline F1 >= 0.78 for intent detection in the provided subset; can tune if you have target values).
- Latency target: p95 <= 420ms for the evaluation profile we included; we'll provide a small perf harness to reproduce the measurement.
- Revocation and audit: API key revoke is immediate and we will send a confirmation to the MAP contacts; token rotation is supported and documented.
- Derivative datasets: we accept a mutual deletion log exchange (BrightCart provides deletion CSV; Redwood acknowledges completion).

I've a...

title: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)
body:
Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwoodinference.com>: Team,

Thanks for walking through the audit export threat model yesterday. Capturing notes + required mitigations we need before Security can sign off for limited beta.

Key risks discussed:
1) Secret exposure (Splunk HEC token, Datadog API key, S3 assume-role creds) via logs, metrics labels, config endpoints
2) Data exfil/SSRF via destination configuration (customer-controlled URLs / hosts)
3) Cross-tenant leakag...

Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwoodinference.com>: Marcus — super helpful, thank you.

Proposed owners/dates:
- A (destination validation + egress hooks): Connor + my team. Target EOW for validation in control plane + exporter-side guard. We’ll implement “destination type”-based validation (Splunk/Datadog fixed domains; S3 is AWS SDK / no URL). For Splunk we’ll support allowlisted FQDNs only (no IP literal).
- B (secrets handling)...

normalized.subject: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)

cc: Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>, Hanae Suzuki <hanae.suzuki@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)
messages.body:
Team,

Thanks for walking through the audit export threat model yesterday. Capturing notes + required mitigations we need before Security can sign off for limited beta.

Key risks discussed:
1) Secret exposure (Splunk HEC token, Datadog API key, S3 assume-role creds) via logs, metrics labels, config endpoints
2) Data exfil/SSRF via destination configuration (customer-controlled URLs / hosts)
3) Cross-tenant leakage (exporter config sync, checkpoints, DLQ)
4) PII/sensitive data leakage in exported audit events (esp. user agent / IP + any freeform “details” fields)

Required mitigations (must-have for beta enablement):
A. Destination validation + egress controls
- Enforce allowlist patterns...

cc: Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>, Hanae Suzuki <hanae.suzuki@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)
messages.body:
Marcus — super helpful, thank you.

Proposed owners/dates:
- A (destination validation + egress hooks): Connor + my team. Target EOW for validation in control plane + exporter-side guard. We’ll implement “destination type”-based validation (Splunk/Datadog fixed domains; S3 is AWS SDK / no URL). For Splunk we’ll support allowlisted FQDNs only (no IP literal).
- B (secrets handling): Hanae, with Benji (not on thread) to wire secret refs end-to-end. Target early next week; we already have secret refs in control plane draft but need to ensure exporter never sees raw values except at send time.
- C (tenant isolation): my team; checkpoint + DLQ prefixes will include tenant_id + export_config_id...

cc: Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)
messages.body:
Adding a few specifics on secrets so we’re aligned:

- Storage: Control plane stores destination credentials encrypted w/ KMS and returns only a credential_ref to exporter via config sync.
- Retrieval: Exporter uses its service identity to call internal Secrets API “GetCredential(tenant_id, credential_ref)”. We should scope authz to tenant_id + export_config_id.
- Logging: we’ll add a redaction filter for headers/fields named token/api_key/secret/access_key and ensure adapter errors don’t include request headers.
- Rotation: we can support “create new credential + update export config to point to new ref”; exporter will pick up on next config refresh. Suggest default refresh interval 60s ...

cc: Hanae Suzuki <hanae.suzuki@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)
messages.body:
+1 to all.

PII/policy confirmations:
- IP address + user agent are permitted ONLY for auth/session/security events (login, token creation/revocation, permission changes, export config changes). Not permitted for inference request-level events.
- “details” field must remain structured + allowlisted (no freeform blobs). If any service emits a string payload into details, it should be dropped or replaced with “[REDACTED]”.
- Export boundary should enforce the audit schema v2 allowlist. If an event has unknown keys, exporter should drop those keys and increment a metric.

Also, please ensure we don’t export internal network identifiers (pod IPs, node names) in source.* fields.

-Kevin

cc: Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>, Hanae Suzuki <hanae.suzuki@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)
messages.body:
From SRE side, the “pause exports” / “kill switch” requirement is important and also security-adjacent (limits blast radius if a sink is misconfigured).

Requests:
- Expose an operator control to disable an export config quickly (control plane toggle that exporter honors within 1–2 min).
- Alerts should NOT include destination hostnames/tokens. For destination, include only destination_type + export_config_id.
- Add an explicit metric for validation failures (config rejected) vs delivery failures (adapter).

Once you have the first cut of destination validation, we can do a quick chaos test by forcing downstream 429s + verifying the exporter doesn’t “fail open” to alternate hosts.

-Sean

cc: Kevin Osei <kevin.osei@redwoodinference.com>, Hanae Suzuki <hanae.suzuki@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: Audit exporter threat model review - notes & required mitigations for beta sign-off (ENG-5189)
messages.body:
This all looks good.

A couple clarifications for “beta sign-off” so we don’t block on nice-to-haves:

Must-have for enabling the first beta tenants:
- Destination validation (including private-range/localhost/metadata IP protections) + exporter-side enforcement
- Secret refs end-to-end + redaction in logs/errors
- Per-tenant/per-config namespacing for checkpoints + DLQ
- Schema allowlist enforcement at export boundary (drop unknown keys)
- Kill switch / pause exports path documented in runbook

Can land after first beta enablement (but before expanding cohort):
- DNS rebinding continuous monitoring (as long as we have the exporter-side CIDR denylist and disallow IP literals)
- Additional...

High level: scanners will emit a scan event per badge with the following fields: badge_id, timestamp_utc, first_name, last_name, email (when captured), company, badge_type, opt_in_flag, form_id (if the attendee completed a QR form), and session_tags.

Questions we need answers to from your side to scope the HubSpot assimilation:
- Is the HubSpot contact create/update flow ok to map directly on email, or do you prefer dedupe by badge_id + email?
- Do you require any additio...

messages.subject: Re: Onsite badge scanner integration + HubSpot field question
messages.body:
Samir — thanks for this and the quick turnaround. A few answers from our side to start scoping: 

- HubSpot mapping: prefer primary dedupe on email where present, with fallback to badge_id for create/update when email is missing. We want to avoid creating duplicate contacts for the same person scanning multiple times.
- Consent: we'd like the raw opt_in_flag stored as a boolean custom property (redwood_event_opt_in), and additionally a consent_source property that stores "badge_scanner_v1" or "qr_form". That helps downstream retention/marketing rules.
- Dedupe/window: treat any scan within 12 hours at the same venue as duplicate for lead ingestion (but still store the raw event to our eve...

messages.subject: Re: Onsite badge scanner integration + HubSpot field question
messages.body:
Quick ops notes — tagging for inbound mapping and offline behavior. Copying Ben for infra constraints.

- HubSpot upsert: HubSpot allows upsert on any single property marked as unique via their API, but we typically recommend keeping email as primary and using badge_id as a secondary custom prop. The ingestion lambda can attempt upsert by email then badge_id.
- Deduping policy: 12 hours is fine. For big shows with many booths we should expose a dedupe window config per-venue; default 12h but allow shorter windows for booth-only captures.
- QR fallback: suggest EventFlow include a short form_id token and an encrypted payload for email capture; then the scanner can surface "complete form" l...

messages.subject: Re: Onsite badge scanner integration + HubSpot field question
messages.body:
Thanks Rachel / Marissa — useful guidance. Quick updates based on your asks:

- We'll emit scanner_id and venue_id in every event.
- We'll add consent_source and keep opt_in_flag as a boolean. We'll also include the privacy statement reference (EventFlow_privacy_statement.txt) in the event payload as privacy_ref.
- For emailless captures we'll emit form_id and a short qr_token that maps to an attendee record in EventFlow. The QR path will surface a small capture form; once they submit, we'll push the email back to your ingestion lambda with the original badge_id.

Attachment: EventFlow_privacy_statement.txt (text).

Operational constraints to note: our scanners batch events every 30s and ...

Jamie — quick note from the test-harness side.

- We're using the Redwood load harness v2 (k8s job) with the following knobs exposed: batch size (auto/1/4), kernel selection (fast/accuracy), kv-cache warm-up policy (true/false), and request shaping. We'll run both auto-batching and fixed-batch=1 to see latency/regression differences.

This looks good. We've uploaded the eval set to the Drive link you provided and added an IP allowlist for the Redwood test pool. Please use the fast-retail-test cluster ranges: 34.82.10.0/24 and 34.82.11.0/24.

I'll escalate the SLA/contract items to our legal/pricing teams to keep the signature target viable.

Best,
Laura Bennett
Head of Sales Operations, Redwood Inference

1) A ...

messages.subject: Production impact—queueing spike, request for short-term credit/behavior change
messages.body:
Hi Alex,

We saw a significant increase in tail latency and queuing on our reserved pool during yesterday's batch of customer requests (roughly 18:10–18:40 PDT). Several API calls retried which impacted UX for a portion of our customers.

We understand bursts happen, but our contract references a dedicated throughput target and queueing behavior that should minimize this. Could Redwood help with two things?

1) A short-term credit for the token overage incurred during the incident window (rough estimate attached).
2) A quick rundown of what caused the queuing behavior and whether a temporary priority carveout or headroom change is possible while we stabilize our request shaping.

Attached...

messages.subject: Re: Production impact—queueing spike, request for short-term credit/behavior change
messages.body:
Hi Samira—thanks for the note and for sending the logs. I'm sorry you hit that window; I know how disruptive retries are for end users.

Quick summary from our side after an initial look at the CSV: there was a concentrated burst from a handful of downstream clients that caused concurrent inflight tokens to exceed your soft headroom threshold on pool RW-Dedicated-14. That triggered the queueing guard which preserves latency SLOs for the pool but pushes lower-priority inflight into the backlog.

Immediate options we can offer:
- Temporary credit for the incremental tokens during the incident window (we can propose a credit amount once Ben confirms billing calculation).
- A time-limited pri...

messages.subject: Re: Production impact—queueing spike, request for short-term credit/behavior change
messages.body:
Alex / Lauren—I've run the numbers against LumenWorks' usage. If we base the credit on the incremental token count during the incident window, I'm comfortable offering a one-time credit equal to 80% of the incremental token spend for the affected window (this is discretionary but keeps precedent minimal).

Notes:
- Credit calculation uses the attached CSV and excludes any obvious client-side retry loops (we flagged a few anomalous patterns).
- For the temporary priority carveout: operationally we can enable a 48-hour soft-priority lane scoped to application key lw-main-01 with immediate effect. It will allow a higher inflight headroom allocation but does consume pooled capacity and could ...

Thanks Alex — got it. A few clarifications before the call:

> - Can you confirm the list of subprocessors? We need the full current inventory and a commitment on notice for new ones.

We require:
- A complete subprocessor inventory (vendor name, country, function). Please include whether each subprocessor processes EU data and where they are located.
- Retention: we marked a 3-year cap in Section 6; would Redwood accept 5 years for backups and logs but 1 year for derived analytics? If not we’ll need rationale.
- Transfers: our counsel is pushing for a specific clause referencing the EU Commission’s standard contractual clauses (modules + local addendum). If you have a standard Addendum, ...

messages.body:
Forwarding Immutecorp’s redline and initial questions — see inline below.

Key asks from customer: full subprocessor inventory, retention window flexibility (they propose 3 years vs our standard), and explicit SCC/Addendum reference. They CC’d external counsel (Nick Harris).

Can you: 
1) Provide a prioritized list of items we should concede vs push back on (privacy/counsel POV).
2) Draft a response redline (tracked changes) that we can send to Jane. Laura, if you can mark any commercial-risky liability language for me to flag to sales.
3) Confirm e-sign path: we prefer DocuSign for DPA + Annex. If we accept their changes, do we send one consolidated envelope?

Also linking HubSpot deal: ...

messages.body:
Thanks for the context, Alex. I reviewed Immutecorp’s redlines and drafted a counsel note (attached) with two recommended response paths and required evidence. High-level summary below — full comments are in privacy_counsel_comments.pdf.

Summary — primary negotiation points and recommended responses:

1) International transfers
- Recommend: Offer the EU SCCs + Redwood’s EU/UK Addendum (we should reference the latest Commission decision and include Module 2 where relevant). This is our low-friction path.
- If customer insists on a bespoke transfer clause, escalate to legal; it will add review time and risk.

2) Subprocessors
- Provide current subprocessor inventory (attached stub). We sho...

messages.subject: Proposed next steps + DocuSign envelope (DPA v2)
messages.body:
Hi Jane — thanks again for the quick turn. Per Monica’s counsel note, we propose the following plan to keep momentum:

1) We’ll route the EU SCCs + Redwood EU/UK Addendum as our default transfer mechanism (Monica attached the addendum draft to her comments).
2) We’ll accept the hybrid retention approach: 5 years for encrypted backups, 1 year for derived analytics, with definitions in the Annex.
3) Subprocessors: we’re attaching our current inventory. We’ll commit to 30 days’ notice for new subprocessors and a documented objection process; for any high-risk vendor we’ll share SOC 2 evidence.

I’ve prepared a response redline (Redwood_DPA_Response_v2.docx) and queued a DocuSign envelope to ...

Hi Sanjay —

Thanks again for taking time today. Quick recap from our discovery so we’re aligned before we draft the trial plan:

- Primary use case: triage-assist for the nurse intake queue (free-text patient notes -> structured triage suggestions).
- Key success metrics you mentioned: 1) 30% reduction in average triage time for intake notes, 2) 95% precision on high-priority flagging (safety-critical), 3) integration with Epic sandbox and response under 500ms median.
- Timeline target: 4-week POV starting ~Jul 6, with final readout in week 5.

Owners (proposed):
- Redwood: Rachel (AE) — overall coordination; Ben (Solutions) — infra + integration support; Marissa (CS) — onboarding and ev...

messages.subject: Re: Quick recap + proposed success criteria (POV)
messages.body:
Thanks Rachel — this is helpful. A few clarifications from our side:

- The 30% triage time reduction is an aspirational target; baseline is ~4.5 minutes per triage. If we can hit 20% that would still be a win.
- Safety is non-negotiable; the 95% precision should be for high-priority flags (~top 3% of cases). We need false negatives to be <1% for that category.
- Epic sandbox: Laura can confirm access later today. Note: our sandbox requires a static IP allowlist for API calls. Can Redwood accommodate that?

Sample data: I’ll pull ~200 de-identified triage notes and share a secure link by end of day Thursday.

One ask on evaluation: can Redwood produce per-run latency histograms (p50/p90/p...

messages.subject: Re: Quick recap + proposed success criteria (POV)
messages.body:
Appreciate the quick clarifications, Sanjay. Short answers inline below and next steps at the bottom.

- Baseline / targets: noted — we’ll mark 20% as stretch-minimum and keep 30% as aspirational. Will call out the "high-priority" bucket and require FN <1% there in the rubric.
- Static IP allowlist: yes — Redwood Dedicated can egress from fixed IPs; Ben, can you confirm which pool we would propose for Veridian?
- Latency & cost telemetry: yes — we’ll provide p50/p90/p99 latency histograms and estimated token cost per request for the POV. Marissa will define the eval runs and reporting cadence.

I’ll prepare a short infra checklist and drop it in the Drive tonight (infra-checklist.md).

—Ben

messages.body:
Alex — attaching our redlines to the Order Form and a few MSA sections. Key asks summarized here so we can move quickly:

- Output ownership: Acme requires explicit assignment of output/title to Acme for outputs generated from Acme-provided prompts and data.
- Training / model improvements: we need an opt-out for model training on our data, or explicit consent for retrain.
- Feedback / telemetry license: we want a narrow, non-exclusive feedback license limited to diagnostic/ops use (no training).

We have SOC2 evidence ready to share; legal@acme.com is on the thread for the redline. Can you confirm your position and timeline for a response? We’re trying to finalize procurement this month....

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Redlines attached: Order Form + MSA (IP & training clauses)
messages.body:
Alex / team — reviewed Acme’s markup. Below are legal’s recommendations and redline text snippets we should offer back. I’ve included reasoning so sales/procurement can set expectations with the customer.

Summary of position:
1) Output ownership: we can accept that Acme owns outputs that are produced solely from Acme-provided prompts and data ("Customer Content"). We should resist assignment of outputs derived from the model generally or from mixed inputs (model + customer) unless additional consideration is provided. Proposed clause: "Customer retains title to Outputs that are solely generated from Customer Content and prompts; Redwood retains title in Model Improvements and Outputs gen...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Redlines attached: Order Form + MSA (IP & training clauses) — ops feasibility notes
messages.body:
Quick ops notes / constraints:

- Hosted API: we can honor a training opt-out on a per-account basis by toggling telemetry capture and preventing data persistence into training pipelines. That requires a documented onboarding flow, ACLs, and a flag in account config. Cost: small infra & monitoring lift; no technical blocker.
- Dedicated pools / Private: easier to guarantee (customer-managed models or air-gapped). If Acme wants guaranteed non-use for training forever, only feasible with Private deployment or contractual controls + audits.
- Evidence required: SOC2 Type II latest report, security contact, and (for private) network architecture diagram.
- Timeline: ops and infra team needs 2...

messages.subject: Re: Redlines attached: Order Form + MSA (IP & training clauses) — proposed path forward
messages.body:
Sara / Acme legal — thanks for the redlines and quick turnaround. We’ve pulled together a recommended compromise across Legal and Ops (summary below) and attached a tracked-changes file for review (Proposed_Amendments_Acme.docx).

Proposal summary: 
- Outputs: Redwood will assign title to Outputs only when they are generated solely from Customer Content and prompts. Outputs that materially incorporate Model Improvements or non-customer training data remain Redwood’s. (See tracked language.)
- Training opt-out: Acme may elect an opt-out for use of Customer Content in training Model Improvements for an initial 12-month period post-go-live. For Hosted, opt-out activation requires a 2-week op...

Hi Olivia — thanks for the clear list. Pasted below is a concise breakdown; I attached a PDF with the same numbers and a sample SOW for an annual bundle (observability_pricing_v2.pdf and redwood-addons-sow.xlsx).

Summary list pricing (standard):
- Retention (storage): $0.12 per GB-month (30 days default).

messages.subject: Re: Observability add-ons — retention & tracing pricing questions
