Alex — thanks, that helps a lot. A couple of follow-ups from procurement and our platform lead:

1) Procurement asked whether you can do 20% off list (instead of 15%) for the $150k ARR commitment — they want the extra headroom to cover potential overages in the first quarter.
2) Platform lead asked if you can switch dashboard licensing to a mixed model: 15 seats + 10 dashboard bundles (rather than 25 seats).
3) For tracing: we mostly care about spans related to user-facing requests; can you include sampling guidance or recommend a cost-effective ingestion plan?

If you can provide a revised bundle price and a short note we can include in the PO describing sampling/ingestion best practice,...

- Terms: 12-month upfront payment, 2% overage buffer billed monthly at list if usage exceeds committed baseline.
- Reference clause: 6-month pilot case study allowed with mutual consent (we’d love to feature Acme). If you cannot allow case study, disc...

messages.subject: Data sovereignty commitments + subprocessor timeline
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for the deep dive yesterday. Per our procurement checklist and the regulator brief we have two items we need Redwood to commit to before we advance to our exec approval: 

1) Data residency commitment: we need a DPA addendum that (a) states production content for Acme Health customers originating in the EU will be stored and processed in EU-only infrastructure, and (b) allows for a transient failover to non-EU regions only for disaster/failover events with an explicit maximum window (we're proposing 72 hours) and required notification/recording in audit logs.

2) Subprocessor and KMS detail: a full roster of subprocessors with planned onboarding timeline (not just...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Data sovereignty commitments + subprocessor timeline
messages.body:
Luke —

Thanks for the clear list and the appendix. We can absolutely get you a redline and the roster. High-level answers up front so your counsel has context while we prepare the document: 

- DPA addendum: Redwood can offer a strengthened addendum that includes a contractual commitment to resident processing for EU-origin data under normal operations, and an explicit transient-failover clause limited to emergency/disaster scenarios. Our standard transient window proposal is 48 hours, but we can support 72 hours with additional monitoring and notification steps (we'd like to capture those in the addendum).

- Subprocessors: we'll provide the current roster plus a 6-month onboarding road...

messages.subject: Re: Data sovereignty commitments + subprocessor timeline
messages.body:
Luke / Rachel —

Thanks for the doc, Rachel. A few notes from Legal/Privacy Ops after a quick read of v1: 

- Failover window: we can accept 72 hours as a ceiling, provided we include (1) obligation to notify Acme within 2 hours of failover start, (2) post-incident report within 7 business days, and (3) an option for expedited remediation if Acme requests it (may have cost implications).

- Scope language: the addendum should be explicit about "EU-origin production content" and exclude aggregated telemetry used solely for platform reliability (we'll include an explicit carve-out for telemetry and metrics with examples).

- Subprocessor timeline & audit rights: we can provide a 6-month roa...

cc: Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.com>, ACME Security Team <security@acmehealth.com>
messages.subject: Re: Data sovereignty commitments + subprocessor timeline
messages.body:
Marissa / Rachel —

Appreciate the fast turnaround and the clarifications. The proposed notification cadence and the KMS wording tweak look reasonable to us. We'll review the roster and the updated v2 addendum when you send it. Two quick asks before tomorrow's call: 

1) Can you include an exhibit (technical appendix) that briefly explains how key-wrapped replication works in failover so our security team can verify there is no possibility of plaintext key export?

2) Please send a DocuSign-ready copy of the v2 addendum once final so our counsel can route it to Execs.

We're available for the 10:00-10:30 PT slot and will have one of our security architects on the line.

Thanks,
Luke Bryan...

Recap (what we heard)
- Primary objective: validate perceived latency delta vs Hyperscaler A/B under interactive load. Crestview cares most about p99 at high concurrency for the editor use case.
- Secondary: tokens/sec and cost-per-100k tokens comparison for our recommended config.
- Workloads: ~2.5–5s a...

- Priority metric is p99 under sustained concurrency (32 simulated users). p95 matters but p99 is the gating factor for the editor experience.
- Cost: approximate token-normalized cost (per 100k tokens) is helpful but not a blocker for the first run.
- We are OK with Tue morning PST (May 31) for a 2hr window.

I've attached...

messages.body:
Folks — looping you in to scope the runs and confirm deliverables. Highlights from customer: they want cold-start TTFT captured from container boot, and a concurrency ramp to max 64 simultaneous sessions (synthetic mix approximating 70% chat / 30% completions).

Jordan — can you confirm lab availability Mar 4–6 and if we should include a quantized model run vs FP16?
Dylan — can you outline the CSV schema you need for traceability (token timestamps, tail-latency markers, run-id, config)?
Laura — we’ll need slides + an executive one-pager for Mar 9 readout; can you reserve the 30-min slot on the customer calendar once we confirm results?

Thanks — I put the preliminary MAP in the attachment...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: MAP kickoff: benchmarking deliverables and timeline — ask: perf run plan and artifacts
messages.body:
All good on lab time — I've booked reserved nodes Mar 4 (smoke baseline) and Mar 5–6 (scaling experiments). Short plan:

- Mar 4: baseline runs (FP16, single instance) — measure p50/p95/p99, TTFT (container cold boot), and throughput at 1, 4, 8, 16 concurrent sessions.
- Mar 5: concurrency stress up to 64 sessions across multiple instances; enable batching heuristics and prefix caching where safe.
- Mar 6: quantized (int8) profile run for cost/latency comparison and a repeat of critical profiles.

CSV schema (Dylan please validate): run_id, timestamp_utc, request_id, session_id, model_variant, concurrency_level, token_index, token_latency_ms, cumulative_tokens, container_state(cold|warm),...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Run results posted: preliminary CSV + slides draft
messages.body:
I've uploaded preliminary outputs to S3 and attached a CSV extract (benchmark_results_20260308.csv) and a first-pass slide deck (slides_map_exec_readout_20260309.pdf). Summary highlights:

- Baseline FP16 single-instance: p50 28ms, p95 110ms, p99 240ms; TTFT cold (container boot + first token) = 1.6s median.
- Concurrency scaling: linear throughput up to 32 sessions; beyond 32 we see tail increases — p95 jumps ~2.2x at 64 sessions on current flavor.
- Quantized int8: ~18% lower median latency but p99 tail comparable; recommend int8 only if cost over performance tradeoff acceptable.

Notes for MAP: include both CSV and a small appendix describing how container pooling affects TTFT; I added...

cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.ai>, Dylan Brooks <dylan.brooks@redwood.ai>, Kevin Liu <kevin.liu@acme-payments.com>

Links and artifacts for the readout (sent ahead):
- Slides: https://drive.google.com/open?id=slides_map_exec_readout_20260309
- Raw CSV export: s3://redwood-bench/acme/benchmark_results_20260308.csv
- Live dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/bench/acme-payments?orgId=1

If results look good in the readout, we will transition to contract signature (DocuSign link sent) and schedule a production rollout pilot...

body:
Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>: Team —

Our current DocuSign enterprise agreement and Ironclad CLM renewal windows overlap in Q2. Legal has flagged both renewals as high priority; procurement has asked us to land a recommendation by Feb 5 so finance can provision.

Options on the table (high-level):
- Consolidate e-sign + CLM onto Ironclad (native signature + CLM): one vendor, potential SSO/SCIM consolidation, but heavier implementation and rewo...

Samira Khan <samira@ironclad.com>: Hi Karthik / team —

Thanks for the note. Quick wins from Ironclad's side:
- Ironclad Sign (our native signature) supports REST webhooks with at-least-once delivery and a delivery log we can provide for audit.
- We typically include a 2-week migration sprint + 3 enablement calls for renewals at enterprise scale; we can offset part of the renewal with a professional services credit if you sign a 2...

normalized.subject: Signature platform renewal — vendor consolidation vs. dual-stack proposal

messages.subject: Signature platform renewal — initial framing
messages.body:
Team —

Our current DocuSign enterprise agreement and Ironclad CLM renewal windows overlap in Q2. Legal has flagged both renewals as high priority; procurement has asked us to land a recommendation by Feb 5 so finance can provision.

Options on the table (high-level):
- Consolidate e-sign + CLM onto Ironclad (native signature + CLM): one vendor, potential SSO/SCIM consolidation, but heavier implementation and rework of existing templates.
- Keep DocuSign for e-sign, Ironclad for CLM (dual-stack): less migration cost, but extra monthly cost and duplicated integration maintenance.
- Migrate e-sign to DocuSign and decommission Ironclad attachments (unlikely given contract authoring features)...

messages.subject: Re: Signature platform renewal — initial framing
messages.body:
Hi Karthik / team —

Thanks for the note. Quick wins from Ironclad's side:
- Ironclad Sign (our native signature) supports REST webhooks with at-least-once delivery and a delivery log we can provide for audit.
- We typically include a 2-week migration sprint + 3 enablement calls for renewals at enterprise scale; we can offset part of the renewal with a professional services credit if you sign a 24-month term.
- Template migration: our import tool handles CSV + conditional blocks, but complex custom logic (JS-style templating) requires a migration sprint.

I'll attach a draft renewal term sheet and the migration checklist. Happy to do a 30-minute technical call next week with your infra te...

cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Markus Klein <markus_klein@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Signature platform renewal — initial framing
messages.body:
Samira — thanks for the draft. A few commercial asks from our side:
- We need a commitment for a maximum monthly rate for webhook retries (we bill customers per callback latency).
- Can you confirm SAML attributes and SCIM user schema (Okta mapping) are included at no extra cost for enterprise renewals?
- Implementation credit ask: can Ironclad provide a $25k PS credit if we commit to a 24-month term? That would make consolidation more compelling.

Also looping DocuSign to understand competitive positioning.

Thanks,
Marissa
Head of Legal Ops

---

messages.subject: Re: Signature platform renewal — initial framing
messages.body:
Marissa — appreciate the ping. A couple of notes from DocuSign:
- DocuSign Connect has guaranteed delivery SLA as part of enterprise (we provide message replay and an events archive).
- Scale: we handle high-volume signing flows; we can propose a dedicated ingest queue for Redwood to reduce latency spikes.
- We can offer a one-time PSA savings (TBD) and will surface a term sheet this week.

I'll bring an integration engineer next week to run a 60-minute walkthrough with Markus/Rachel re: our Connect endpoints and template translation options.

Regards,
Tom
Enterprise Sales, DocuSign
Attachment: docusign_term_sheet_2026-01-29.docx

---

cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Signature platform renewal — initial framing
messages.body:
Tom / Samira — thanks. A few technical constraints at our end to confirm before we run POCs:
1) Our ingestion pipeline requires idempotent callbacks with a monotonic sequence number. We need the vendor to preserve a per-document sequence id.
2) All webhook payloads must include the signed document URL with short-lived presigned links (we cannot allow permanent public URLs).
3) Template variables: our current contract-renderer expects a subset of the Ironclad templating (must support conditional blocks and multi-line clauses).

Can each vendor supply a short mapping doc that shows how their webhook/event model maps to our contract-store ingest fields? That will let us estimate the engineer...

messages.subject: Re: Signature platform renewal — procurement recommendation & next steps
messages.body:
All — after reviewing the term sheets and technical feedback, Legal Ops recommends a dual-track approach:

Recommendation (short):
- Renew DocuSign for 12 months (standard enterprise terms) to avoid interrupting customer-facing flows.
- Sign a 24-month Ironclad renewal with a conditional PS credit ($25k) and a 90-day pilot window for full template migration and decommission planning.

Rationale:
- Minimizes business risk by avoiding an immediate cutover.
- Provides runway to validate template fidelity and webhook reliability in production during the Ironclad pilot.
- Commercially, a 24-month commitment with a PS credit makes consolidation financially testable while keeping DocuSign as fal...

Jordan

messages.subject: Re: Recap & next steps from today — SOC 2, IR timelines, BCP evidence
messages.body:
Hi Dylan — thanks for the recap. Helpful. A few clarifications from our side before you send docs:

1) SOC 2: we need the full SOC 2 Type II report (PDF) with auditor name and period covered. Redacted items are fine for vendor review but we will request an unredacted copy under an NDA if the POC proceeds.
2) Incident notification SLA: Aurelia’s baseline is 48 hours for confirmed data breaches involving PII/PHI; for critical production outages that lead to customer impact we prefer notification within 24 hours. Can you confirm Redwood’s internal SLA and escalation contact?
3) BCP/DR evidence: for our procurement checklist we typically request RTO/RPO targets, last DR test date and summary,...

A) SOC 2: We have a SOC 2 Type II report for our Hosted API (period: Apr 2025 - Sept 2025). I can share a redacted copy under NDA; alternatively we can provide the SOC 2 bridge letter plus the auditor contact for verification.

B) KMS / encryption: For Hosted API we use AWS KMS (primary). We also support customer-managed keys via AWS KMS and have integrations for Google Cloud KMS for specific private deployments. We can support HSM-backed key storage via customer-managed configurations in our Private offering.

messages.subject: EverGuard request: runbook snapshot, telemetry sample, and tabletop availability
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

We are starting the SOC 2 scoping conversation with our audit team and they asked for a couple of artifacts from vendors as part of the onboarding packet. Can you share a redacted incident response runbook (IRP) snapshot, a small telemetry sample (sanitized), and your completed SIG/CAIQ responses where available?

Specifically, they asked for:
- A redacted IRP highlighting detection, escalation, and customer notification steps
- A 24–48 hour telemetry sample (sanitized/pseudonymized) showing traces and auth events
- SIG/CAIQ (or SOC 2 control narrative) exports if you have them

Also: would Redwood be available for a 60-minute tabletop walkthrough next week (Tue/Thu preferred)...

messages.subject: Re: EverGuard request: runbook snapshot, telemetry sample, and tabletop availability
messages.body:
Alex — thanks for the clear list. Looping in the security and telemetry leads so we can assemble redacted artifacts and confirm dates.

Marissa and Jonas — can you prepare a redacted IRP PDF (remove customer-specific identifiers and any IP-level forensic blobs) and a short control narrative for SOC 2? Karthik, can your team produce a sanitized telemetry export for the 2026-05-15 window (auth events + key traces only, no PII)?

We prefer to deliver a zipped package that includes: runbook_redacted_v3.pdf, telemetry_sample_20260515.zip (SHA256 listed), and a SIG/CAIQ response CSV. We'll provide a Google Drive link behind an NDA and can also send the redacted PDF directly.

For tabletop: Rach...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: EverGuard request: runbook snapshot, telemetry sample, and tabletop availability
messages.body:
On it — infra will generate the sanitized export. Plan:
- Export window: 2026-05-15 00:00–23:59 PT
- Fields retained: event_time, event_type, obfuscated_user_id, auth_result, service, region, request_id
- PII removed: user emails, IPs (will be hashed/trimmed), payloads excluded
- Delivery: telemetry_sample_20260515.zip (around 9 MB) + SHA256 value in a .sha256 file

I will also include a short README describing the redaction steps so auditors can follow the chain-of-custody. Target to upload to a gated Drive folder by Tue EOD (2026-05-19).

Karthik
Director, Production Security

[attachment: telemetry_export_plan.txt (TXT, 1.2 KB)]

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
messages.subject: Re: EverGuard request: runbook snapshot, telemetry sample, and tabletop availability
messages.body:
Alex — attaching the redacted runbook and an initial SIG/CAIQ export. Per Karthik's timeline, the sanitized telemetry package will be uploaded to the gated Drive folder (link below) by EOD today; I'll also place a .sha256 file in the folder.

Attachments included with this email:
- runbook_redacted_v3.pdf (IRP: detection, triage, escalation, external notification flow; customer identifiers removed)
- sig_caiq_responses_v1.csv (SIG/CAIQ mapping for controls requested)
- control-narrative-soc2.pdf (high-level SOC 2 control narrative)

Gated Drive folder (NDA required to access): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1A2b3C_everguard-share
DocuSign (for NDA access): envelope-0a1b2c3d4e5f

P...

messages.subject: Re: EverGuard request: runbook snapshot, telemetry sample, and tabletop availability
messages.body:
Rachel — this is great, thanks. We'll sign the NDA and grab the artifacts from the Drive link once DocuSign completes. Tue 2026-05-26 @ 11:00 PT works; attendees from our side will be myself, Priya Das (compliance), and Mark Donovan (audit lead).

One follow-up ask from audit: can you include a short redaction provenance note (who performed redactions and a checksum of the delivered artifacts) in the gated folder? Karthik's README should be perfect for that + the .sha256 file.

Appreciate the quick turnaround. See you on the 26th.

— Alex

title: POC orientation — missed session & proposed reschedule
body:
Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>: Hi Samir / Anya,

Looking forward to our POC orientation today at 10:00 AM PT. This will be a focused hands-on session: prompt review, routing configuration, and a short load test to validate concurrency assumptions (15–20 minutes).

Quick agenda:
- 10:00 — Introductions & scope (Alex)
- 10:10 — Prompt walkthrough + expected outputs (Samir/Anya share)
- 10:30 — Routing rules / fallback discussion (Diego)
- 10:45 —...

Samir Kaur <samir@acmehealth.com>: Hi Alex —

I’m really sorry, we ran over in a prior meeting and Anya had to step into an urgent clinic call. We weren’t able to join. My apologies for the short notice.

We definitely want the hands-on session — could we reopen for later this week? Preferably Wed/Thu afternoon PT. Also, we have a slightly different prompt set that exercises conditional branching (I’ll attach it).

Best,
Samir

messages.subject: Re: POC orientation — kickoff details and checklist
messages.body:
Thanks for the quick note, Samir — no worries.

We can absolutely reschedule. Proposed slots (all PT):
- Wed, Mar 24 — 1:30 PM / 2:30 PM
- Thu, Mar 25 — 3:00 PM / 4:00 PM

If any of those work, I’ll send an updated calendar invite and include a short pre-call checklist so the load test runs smoothly (we’ll need a dedicated test user account and a confirmation of expected peak RPS).

Also looping in Monica and Diego to highlight the routing and prompt-check items so they can prep.

Attachments: calendar-invite-ic.json (placeholder)

—Alex

messages.subject: Re: POC orientation — kickoff details and checklist
messages.body:
Hi all —

Thanks, Alex. Thu, Mar 25 at 3:00 PM PT works for us.

Regarding the load test: we want to validate a spike to 60 RPS for 5 minutes and then a steady-state at 20 RPS. We’ll provide a dedicated service account and test dataset by EOD Tue, Mar 23.

I’m attaching the prompt set we mentioned (cond-branch-prompts.txt). Can you confirm Redwood will exercise the conditional branching paths? We want the KV-cache behavior observed when context size exceeds 1,500 tokens.

Thanks — Anya

messages.subject: Re: POC orientation — kickoff details and checklist
messages.body:
Hi Anya / Samir —

Confirmed for Thu, Mar 25 3:00 PM PT. Quick notes on our end:
- We will exercise conditional branching and monitor KV cache hits/misses.
- For the 60 RPS spike, we’ll run a stepped ramp (10 -> 30 -> 60) and capture tail latency at 99th percentile.
- Please confirm the test user credentials and give us a sample of the longer context you mentioned (1,500+ tokens) so we can pre-warm caches.

I attached the loadtest-plan.xlsx with the ramp schedule and the metrics we will record.

Regards,
Diego

messages.subject: Re: POC orientation — kickoff details and checklist
messages.body:
Thanks for confirming, Anya. Two small asks before Thursday: 
1) Please upload the prompt set to the shared Drive link (see thread metadata) and flag any prompts that must not be persisted to cache.
2) If you have an expected token distribution (mean/95th), include that in the spreadsheet.

Alex will send the calendar invite with dial-in and the checklist tomorrow. Looking forward to getting this running.

Best,
Monica Patel
Sr. Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference
monica.patel@redwood.com

Hi Samira — great to hear and thanks for sending the intake form.

Short answers up front:
- Required paperwork: completed vendor intake, W-9 (U.S. vendors) or W-8 if non-US, and a copy of the signed SOW/ordering doc. For Redwood Private/Dedicated we also ask for a primary billing contact and remit address.

Alex —

Thanks for sending a preview of the order form. Before we finalize procurement, a couple asks from our side on the security exhibit and audit language:

- We require a SOC 2 Type II report (most recent 12-month period) and an accompanying control map that maps Redwood controls to SOC 2 criteria.
- Pen-test language: VineHealth needs an explicit window for on-demand pen tests with a 30-day courtesy notice for production scope; test plan to be shared in advance.
- Audit rights: our legal team prefers limited inspection only (no tenant-wide privileged access) and a designated evidence cadence (we typically ask for an evidence packet every quarter and ad-hoc logs for incidents).
- Evi...

messages.subject: Re: Draft security exhibit + SOC 2 attestation request
messages.body:
Elena — thanks for the clear list. Quick plan from our side:

1) I'll attach a redlined MSA focused on the security exhibit and order form language (see attached redlined_MSA_v2.docx).
2) Laura (legal) will review the audit-rights and limited inspection wording — we typically trade limited inspection + scoped sampling for SOC 2 evidence and a documented pen-test process.
3) Ben (AE) will coordinate procurement timing and the DocuSign flow.
4) Proposed evidence cadence: quarterly evidence packet (SOC 2, control matrix, system diagrams), ad-hoc incident logs within 5 business days for classified incidents, and an agreed pen-test notice window (we typically ask for 14 days).

We can be flexi...

messages.subject: Re: Draft security exhibit + SOC 2 attestation request
messages.body:
Folks —

I reviewed the attached redline and compiled a short legal note. High-level positions below (detailed redline inline in redlined_MSA_v2.docx):

- SOC 2: we can provide the most recent SOC 2 Type II report and a SOC control mapping. We will provide a compressed evidence package on a quarterly cadence; we cannot commit to continuous privileged access.
- Pen-test: Redwood accepts customer-requested pen-tests on a scoped, mutually-agreed basis. Our standard internal ask is a 14-day operational notice (to ensure scheduling and guardrails). If customer needs 30 days, recommend we accept a 30-day notice for production-only scoped tests but limit scope to agreed systems. We'll add langua...

messages.subject: Re: Draft security exhibit + SOC 2 attestation request
messages.body:
Thanks Alex + Laura —

A few clarifications from our SecurityOps / procurement side after reviewing your positions:

- The 30-day notice is not negotiable for production pen-tests at our compliance program (our board requires longer window). If Redwood can accept 30 days for prod scope, we can accept your limited inspection approach.
- On evidence cadence: quarterly compressed package is fine. For incidents, we need the ability to request logs and relevant exports within 7 business days for high-severity incidents (Severity 1/2).
- On indemnity: we will not require additional indemnity for customer-conducted pen-tests as long as the test plan is approved in advance and scope is limited; w...

Thanks again for your time this morning. Quick recap from our demo and an initial model shortlist with rationale so you have something concrete to share with the BrightLane team. I kept this focused on the three options we touched on during the call and mapped them to the tradeoffs you care about (cost, latency, quality / hallucination risk).

Executive summary (recommended order):
1) RW-Instruc...

Sofia Martinez <sofia.martinez@brightlane.ai>: Alex —

Thanks for the clear breakdown. A few quick clarifying questions before we finalize which path to run: 
1) On hallucination: can you point to 2–3 concrete example prompts from the prompt set where the 7B model fails but Llama-3-13B succeeds? That will help our product team judge the impact.
2) For the cost estimates, are those inclusive of Redwood service fees (or raw infere...

messages.subject: Post-demo: model fit options & tradeoffs (recommendations + next steps)
messages.body:
Hi Sofia / Evan —

Thanks again for your time this morning. Quick recap from our demo and an initial model shortlist with rationale so you have something concrete to share with the BrightLane team. I kept this focused on the three options we touched on during the call and mapped them to the tradeoffs you care about (cost, latency, quality / hallucination risk).

Executive summary (recommended order):
1) RW-Instruct-7B-quant (cost-first)
 - Why: ~good extraction and consistent structured outputs for the search/qa flows you described, lowest unit cost.
 - Benchmarks (internal subset): p95 latency ~120ms (single-request, 16-token output), hallucination rate ~6% (measured on our prompt set), ...

cc: Evan Cho <evan.cho@brightlane.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Post-demo: model fit options & tradeoffs (recommendations + next steps)
messages.body:
Alex —

Thanks for the clear breakdown. A few quick clarifying questions before we finalize which path to run: 
1) On hallucination: can you point to 2–3 concrete example prompts from the prompt set where the 7B model fails but Llama-3-13B succeeds? That will help our product team judge the impact.
2) For the cost estimates, are those inclusive of Redwood service fees (or raw inference cost only)? We need the final price we should expect on the invoice.
3) For the pilot fallback: what conditions trigger the automatic fallback exactly (latency threshold, error rate, or hallucination detection)?

If answers are quick, we'd prefer starting with the 7-day eval on a 2k random subset of our pro...

messages.subject: Re: Post-demo: model fit options & tradeoffs (recommendations + next steps)
messages.body:
Hi Sofia / Evan —

Quick answers + attachments (benchmark-summary-20260310.csv attached):

1) Example failure cases (from prompt-set-v1.txt):
 - prompt ID p_0421: multi-field extraction from a nested policy doc. RW-Instruct-7B-quant swapped two date fields; Llama-3-13B returns correct pairs 98% of the time.
 - prompt ID p_1134: multi-sentence aggregation where the model must reconcile conflicting facts across paragraphs; 7B hallucinated an unsupported claim in ~7% of samples, Llama-3-13B at 1.5%.

2) Pricing: the per-1k-token numbers Alex listed are raw inference cost estimates. Final Redwood invoice would add platform/serving fees — for this deal our proposal uses a blended multiplier of...

cc: Evan Cho <evan.cho@brightlane.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Post-demo: model fit options & tradeoffs (recommendations + next steps)
messages.body:
Sofia —

Perfect — thanks for the quick read. We'll proceed with the 7-day eval on the 2k sample as discussed. Next steps I'll execute once you confirm the evaluation cohort (intent filters or a sample list) and we get an e-sign on the minimal SOW (linked). Proposed timeline:
- Today (same day): sign SOW -> we provision endpoints (2–4 hours).
- Day 1–7: run eval (we’ll collect per-prompt diffs, cost projection, latency histograms).
- Day 8: review results (20–30 minute review); if Llama-3-13B materially improves quality for your critical intents, we recommend the A/B pilot path.

Action items for BrightLane:
- Reply to this thread with confirmation of the 2k-sample criteria (or attach CSV...

messages.body:
Alex — thanks for taking a look. Our legal team pushed a handful of redlines in the Order Form/Exhibit A that we think are necessary for StellarSys to move forward with productization. Two asks we want to confirm with Redwood: 

1) Output ownership / productization: The redline proposes that StellarSys receives an assignment of output rights (subject to certain limitations) once productization is enabled. We want to understand Redwood's willingness to assign outputs vs. grant an exclusive license and what operational conditions you need (billing tiers, additional indemnity, etc.).

2) Telemetry evidence: Our procurement team wants to know what constitutes acceptable \"telemetry evidence\"...

messages.subject: Re: Clarify redlines on productization and telemetry evidence
messages.body:
On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 9:13 AM Sandra Miller <sandra.miller@stellarsys.com> wrote:
> Alex — thanks for taking a look. Our legal team pushed a handful of redlines in the Order Form/Exhibit A that we think are necessary for StellarSys to move forward with productization.

Thanks, Sandra — appreciate the clarity and the quick turnaround. High level from our side: Redwood's standard is to provide customers a broad commercial license to use outputs for productization; we don't usually do an automatic assignment of title for generated outputs. That said, we do offer tailored commercial paths for customers that need stronger rights (assignment or exclusive productization rights) typically in ex...

messages.subject: Fwd: Clarify redlines on productization and telemetry evidence — internal sync
messages.body:
Team — forwarding StellarSys redline and my proposed two-option path. Summary below and quick asks: 

Summary of external ask:
- StellarSys redlines push for assignment of outputs and wants a clear definition of acceptable telemetry evidence to get productization rights.

My proposed path (see attached):
- Option A: Standard non-exclusive productization license (no assignment).
- Option B: Conditional expanded rights / assignment for defined product lines after StellarSys delivers telemetry/evidence + security attestation and pays an add-on fee.

Questions / asks for legal & procurement: 
1) Laura — can you draft language for Option B that: 
 - conditions expanded rights on delivery of sp...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Clarify redlines on productization and telemetry evidence — internal sync
messages.body:
Alex — thanks for the clear summary. Draft thoughts below and attached are two short snippets you can drop into the Order Form/Exhibit A.

Short answer on assignment vs license: Redwood's baseline remains a license. For customers that need stronger commercialization rights, we can offer a conditional grant of expanded rights (including a narrow assignment) that vests only after the evidence and attestation workflow completes and the parties execute a short addendum. We'll want to avoid a blanket assignment across all outputs — instead we should limit to named product lines or use-cases (language suggested below).

Suggested contract language (high level):
- \"Productization Rights Addendu...

body:
Sophie Lane <sophie.lane@novatech.com>: Hi Rachel,

Thanks for running the earlier pilot with our engineering team. We reviewed the implementation timeline internally and need to propose a small carve-out to the SOW tied to an expanded staging pool for Q3. High level:

- Add 40k staging tokens (equivalent to a short burst pool for migration testing) for a one-time implementation period.
- We request a one-time implementation fee to cover onboarding engi...

rachel.kim@redwood.com: Hi Sophie — thanks, received and looks straightforward.

Quick answers:
- We normally implement capacity increases via a Change Order to the Order Form (references Exhibit A), with an accompanying SOW amendment when deliverables or milestones change.
- Converting the fee into implementation credits is possible; it depends on whether you want the credits to be refundable/transferable.

Looping in Kar (sales) ...

messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks for running the earlier pilot with our engineering team. We reviewed the implementation timeline internally and need to propose a small carve-out to the SOW tied to an expanded staging pool for Q3. High level:

- Add 40k staging tokens (equivalent to a short burst pool for migration testing) for a one-time implementation period.
- We request a one-time implementation fee to cover onboarding engineering and white-glove migration assistance.
- Timeline: completion of migration milestones by end of Q3 (target Sept 30).

I attached a marked SOW and a draft order form with suggested line items. Can Redwood confirm whether this should be an amendment to the existing Order For...

messages.subject: Re: SOW carve-out and one-time implementation fee adjustment
messages.body:
Hi Sophie — thanks, received and looks straightforward.

Quick answers:
- We normally implement capacity increases via a Change Order to the Order Form (references Exhibit A), with an accompanying SOW amendment when deliverables or milestones change.
- Converting the fee into implementation credits is possible; it depends on whether you want the credits to be refundable/transferable.

Looping in Kar (sales) and Laura (procurement) to surface any commercial constraints. I also added the HubSpot deal link for tracking: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/HS-5591.

Next steps I propose:
1) Laura, can you confirm whether finance needs the fee to be invoiced upfront vs credit schedule?
2) Kar, confir...

messages.subject: Re: SOW carve-out and one-time implementation fee adjustment
messages.body:
Thanks Rachel. A few procurement points:

- Preference is to treat the implementation cost as an invoiceable one-time fee unless Novatech explicitly wants credits applied. Finance prefers clarity for Q2/Q3 forecasting.
- If we invoice, we should include a milestone schedule in the SOW and tie the final 20% of the fee to acceptance of the migration checklist to protect both sides.
- Please add a rollback / deprovisioning clause to the SOW amendment: in the rare event the expanded staging pool needs to be removed prior to cutover, define notice periods and data retention specifics.

Marissa — can you check whether the proposed redline (attached) needs an MSA-level carve-out (limitation of l...

messages.subject: Re: SOW carve-out and one-time implementation fee adjustment
messages.body:
Laura — noted. I reviewed Novatech_SOW_v2_redline.pdf and propose the following legal edits (high-level):

1) Change Order vs Amendment: Treat as a Change Order to the existing Order Form with a short amendment to the SOW (3–4 paragraphs) referencing the MSA (Section 2.3) to avoid creating a new SOW exhibit.
2) Rollback / Deprovisioning: Add a 30-day notice for deprovisioning, specify data purge timings, and require a joint acceptance checklist for final decommissioning.
3) Liability & Cap: For a one-time staging pool, keep liability within existing MSA cap. If Novatech insists on a separate fee with bespoke service-levels, we should include a one-off liability carve-out limited to the fe...

messages.subject: Re: SOW carve-out and one-time implementation fee adjustment
messages.body:
Marissa / team — thanks for the quick review. Novatech can accept the Change Order approach and the 30-day rollback notice. A couple of asks from our side:

- We can accept the liability cap remaining at the MSA level.
- Can Redwood reduce the one-time fee to $30,000 (Novatech will cover travel and any recurring staging usage beyond the carve-out).
- Please include a more granular milestone schedule in the amendment (we suggest week-by-week tasks for the four-week ramp).

We reviewed Marissa_SOW_comments.pdf; we’ll sign off on track if the above items are captured. I’ll initiate the DocuSign envelope for the Change Order once we get the clean text.

Best,
Julian Park
Contracts Manager
Nov...

messages.subject: Fwd: SOW carve-out and one-time implementation fee adjustment
messages.body:
Julian / Sophie — thanks for the confirmation.

We’ll proceed as follows:
1) Legal (Marissa) will produce a clean Change Order + SOW amendment reflecting: Change Order to Order Form, 30-day rollback clause, acceptance checklist, milestone split (40/40/20), and granular 4-week ramp schedule.
2) Commercial (Laura) will invoice $30,000 as a one-time fee per Novatech request; finance will mark the final tranche payable upon acceptance checklist.
3) Kar will update the HubSpot deal (HS-5591) and add the Change Order as an attachment.
4) I’ll send the DocuSign link as soon as the clean text is ready.

Attachments: OrderForm_2026-04-11.docx
Related signing link (to be sent once legal finalizes e...

messages.body:
Hi Sophie / John,

Thanks for taking this on. We have an in-person executive workshop for Acme on Feb 23 (full day) with a follow-up half‑day on Feb 24. Approx headcount: 35 in-room, ~8 senior execs joining remotely at times for panels. Key asks:

- Main room that seats 40 comfortably, with an adjoining breakout area for 3–4 concurrent small groups.
- AV: two large displays (4K preferred) facing the room, a center PTZ camera with good pan/zoom for speaker framing, and a second wide-angle room camera for gallery shots.
- Audio: ceiling or tabletop beamforming mic array (or equivalent) + mixer with echo cancellation for Zoom Rooms.
- Bring a dedicated Zoom Rooms license/hardware or a room P...

messages.subject: Re: Onsite workshop — room selection & AV runbook for Acme exec day
messages.body:
Hi Marissa / John,

Appreciate the detail — we can support this. Two quick options that match your list:

1) Room A — \"Boardroom + Breakout\" (ground floor)
 - Capacity: 40 seated theater / 28 at boardroom-style tables + adjacent breakout foyer for 3 small groups
 - AV: 2 x 85\" 4K displays, 1 x PTZ camera (USB 3.0 / HDMI out), ceiling mic array, Yamaha mixer with echo cancel, room PC pre-loaded with Zoom Rooms; 2x HDMI + 1x USB-C breakout box at lectern.
 - On-site AV tech: included 4 hours; additional hours $150/hr.
 - Internet: 1 Gbps dedicated VLAN; static IP available (we can allowlist Zoom IPs).

2) Room B — \"Small Auditorium\" (2nd floor)
 - Capacity: up to 120; has installed cam...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Fwd: Onsite workshop — room selection & AV runbook for Acme exec day
messages.body:
Team — forwarding Sophie’s options. Quick decisions needed so we can lock room and rehearsal time. Questions for you:

- Preference: Room A (boardroom + adjacent breakout foyer) vs Room B (auditorium)? I lean Room A for workshop vibe.
- Do we need full-day AV tech coverage (Sophie says 4 hours included; extra hours $150/hr)? I recommend booking full-day for peace of mind.
- Demos: Connor, you'll bring two demo laptops — confirm HDMI or USB-C? We'll also need a wired connection for any demo servers.
- Procurement: Rachel, can procurement process a PO if we confirm Room A? If so, Sophie needs billing contact and PO info.
- Any execs that must be onsite podium speakers vs remote? John, can y...

to: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Onsite workshop — room selection & AV runbook for Acme exec day
messages.body:
Marissa —

Short answers:
- I agree Room A (Boardroom + breakout foyer) is the sweet spot. Easier flow for breakout rotations.
- Yes to full-day AV tech. We should budget for 8 hours onsite.
- I will bring two laptops: one MacBook (USB-C) and one Windows laptop (HDMI). I’ll also bring a USB-C -> HDMI adapter and a spare HDMI cable.
- I’ll coordinate with IT for a static IP allowlist and send Sophie the device MACs if you want.

Connor

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Onsite workshop — room selection & AV runbook for Acme exec day

All —

We’re good to proceed with Room A. Confirming: 35 in-room + 8 remote (list below). I’ll open a PO with finance once Sophie sends a quote/invoice. Please direct invoices to procurement@redwood.com and reference PO number PO_4521 (I’ve attached a placeholder).

Remote execs:
- John Patel (Acme) — joins GUI panel 09:45–10:15 PST
- Maria Gomez (Acme) — keynote remotely 11:00–11:20 PST
- Two board members joining for the afternoon Q&A slots (times TBC)

We’ll require two demo laptops on a wired network; Connor has the adapters covered. Vivek — can you bring a spare hotspot (5G) as backup?

Thanks — R

[Attachment: PO_4521.pdf (PDF)]

cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>

title: Clarify billing units for split-context / stitched requests
body:
Liam O'Connor <liam.oconnor@fincloud.com>: Hi Alex,

Quick one from procurement as we parse your RFP: FinCloud has a workflow where we stitch multiple user turns and system prompts into a single long context, but often we also send short incremental requests that overlap with previously cached context (we do some client-side stitching and some server-side). Two specific questions: 

1) Do you bill per actual tokens processed on the backend or per request r...

Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>: Hi Liam — thanks for the clear examples and for the sheet. High level: we bill for the tokens our serving runtime actually decodes/encodes per request, but there are a few implementation details worth calling out so you can model costs precisely. Two quick notes up-front:

- We use a billing granularity we call a "billing chunk" (configurable per contract): requests are rounded up to the...

messages.subject: Question on how partial contexts and stitched requests are billed
messages.body:
Hi Alex,

Quick one from procurement as we parse your RFP: FinCloud has a workflow where we stitch multiple user turns and system prompts into a single long context, but often we also send short incremental requests that overlap with previously cached context (we do some client-side stitching and some server-side). Two specific questions: 

1) Do you bill per actual tokens processed on the backend or per request rounded up to some canonical chunk size? For example, if a request processes 350 tokens but your billing unit is a 128-token window, how is that arithmetic applied?

2) When we do incremental requests that reuse a cached prefix (e.g., we request for tokens 1001-1200 using an exist...

messages.subject: Re: Question on how partial contexts and stitched requests are billed
messages.body:
Hi Liam — thanks for the clear examples and for the sheet. High level: we bill for the tokens our serving runtime actually decodes/encodes per request, but there are a few implementation details worth calling out so you can model costs precisely. Two quick notes up-front:

- We use a billing granularity we call a "billing chunk" (configurable per contract): requests are rounded up to the nearest chunk for invoicing, not per raw token.
- Cache reuse (prefix KV reuse) avoids re-processing those tokens on the model runtime, so you are not charged twice for identical KV that never gets re-decoded — but the behavior depends on how the requests are routed and whether the cached state is conside...

messages.subject: Re: Question on how partial contexts and stitched requests are billed
messages.body:
Thanks Alex — that helps. A couple follow-ups from Priya (technical ops):

- We use a mix of client-side stitching (client keeps a sliding window) and occasional full rehydration of the full context to different regions. If cache persistence is required to avoid double charges, what are the operational constraints? (sticky routing, session affinity, TTL on KV, etc.)

- For modeling: can you give an explicit numeric example with a common chunk size (say 128 tokens) showing three scenarios: full single-shot request (1200 tokens), incremental delta-only (send 200 tokens with KV for previous 1000), and overlapping incremental (send 300 tokens where 100 overlap with previous request)? We want ...

messages.subject: Re: Question on how partial contexts and stitched requests are billed
messages.body:
Hi Liam — Neha (SE) here. Below are the concrete examples you requested; I kept the math simple and used a billing chunk of 128 tokens for illustration. Assume per-token price p, but we'll keep it in tokens billed for clarity.

Example A — single-shot 1200-token request:
- Raw tokens: 1200
- Rounded to 128-token chunks: ceil(1200 / 128) = ceil(9.375) = 10 chunks => billed tokens = 10 * 128 = 1280

Example B — delta-only request where client sends 200 tokens and the server reuses KV for previous 1000 (no re-decode of the prefix):
- Server processes only the delta: 200 tokens
- Rounded: ceil(200 / 128) = 2 chunks => billed tokens = 2 * 128 = 256

Example C — overlapping incremental request ...

messages.subject: Re: Question on how partial contexts and stitched requests are billed
messages.body:
Neha — that's exactly the clarity we needed, thanks. Two quick asks before we finalize the SOW: 

1) For committed volume pricing (we're targeting ~450M tokens/mo), would Redwood agree to a 64-token contract chunk and a billing uplift cap of 8%? That would make our forecasts conservative.

2) If we enable session affinity and your persistent KV store in our Dedicated deployment, can you commit in the SOW that delta requests that reuse cache will not be billed for the prefix tokens (i.e., you will only bill newly-decoded tokens)? We'd like that as a formal guarantee with a short list of operational caveats (TTL, failover rehydration).

If you can confirm both items, procurement will prepar...

messages.subject: Re: Question on how partial contexts and stitched requests are billed
messages.body:
Liam — appreciate the clear asks. We can commit to both items with the recommended caveats. Summary to include in the SOW: 

- Chunk size: 64-token billing chunk for committed volumes >= 400M tokens/mo.
- Billing uplift cap: 8% maximum rounding uplift per billing period (month) measured against actual decoded tokens for your account.
- Cache/delta guarantee: For Dedicated deployments with session affinity and Redwood persistent KV enabled, we will bill only for newly-decoded tokens when overlap is detected and the cache is successfully reused. Exceptions: failover rehydration, manual cache clears, or cross-region rehydration where cache is not present.

Next steps: I'll have our legalOps ...

messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for the partnership — your team has moved fast. PR at Splint is hoping to include a short customer quote and a one-page case study in our July materials. I attached a draft testimonial doc and a short one-pager. Would Redwood be ok with us publishing this?

We proposed a brief quote about reduced inference latency (~40%) and a short blurb on operational ease. Happy to adjust wording.

Attachments: Splint_Testimonial_Draft_v2.pdf (draft), Splint_OnePager_June.pdf

Would you prefer we route this through legal or marketing here? We can wait for your green light.

Best,
Alex Jordan
Head of Growth, Splint Analytics
alex.jordan@splint.co

---
Please do not publish witho...

to: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Fwd: Request: can we publish Splint case study + quote? (sensitivity check)
messages.body:
Forwarding — PR at Splint sent a draft testimonial. Quick background: Splint experienced a user-impacting incident on 2026-06-10 that led to a temporary downgrade and some churn noise in week 24. CS is engaged. Before we give permission to publish anything that frames the relationship as an unproblematic success, I want input from CS/legal/ops.

Key questions:
- Is the incident material to any of the proposed claims (latency/uptime)?
- Any legal / data-retention red flags with the proposed quote?
- Is a redacted/feature-specific quote acceptable to marketing if we need to avoid broader claims?

Please reply with a one-line recommendation. PR needs an answer by EOD today (they want to roll...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: can we publish Splint case study + quote? (sensitivity check)
messages.body:
Short answer: hold.

Context: Splint had a production incident on 2026-06-10 caused by a misconfiguration during a load spike. It affected some tenants for ~45 minutes; we restored service and implemented mitigations. Post-incident sentiment in the account is fragile — the primary contact is evaluating fit and we saw a downgrade in committed capacity earlier this week.

Recommendation: Pause public testimonial until CS completes the post-incident wrap (scheduled next Tue 2026-06-23) and we confirm there are no ongoing remediation items. If marketing needs something now, offer a narrowly-scoped, factual statement (\"reduced inference latency on X workflow\") without claims about uptime or ...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: can we publish Splint case study + quote? (sensitivity check)
messages.body:
Adding ops note: the 6/10 incident root cause was an autoscaling edge case combined with a new batching configuration. Engineering rolled a fix and we added a guardrail; no customer data was exposed.

Legal flagged two items in the attached incident summary: the draft quote references \"achieved 40% lower inference latency leading to 3x throughput\" — we should avoid unverified multiplicative business outcomes in public materials until we confirm the measurement windows and baselines. Also confirm that Splint is okay with naming specific percentile improvements in a public asset.

If marketing wants a publishable line today: recommend a factual, narrow tag like \"reduced model latency on ...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Request: can we publish Splint case study + quote? (sensitivity check)
messages.body:
Thanks — agree with the hold. PR timeline is tight (they asked for final copy by end of day), but I won't push something that could backfire. Two workable options for Splint:

1) Narrow factual quote today (ops-approved language + no %s). Marketing can insert a 1-sentence feature-focused quote and label it "customer statement".
2) Hold public release until CS completes postmortem and we secure an updated sign-off (target 2026-06-24).

If they accept option 1, we'll ask them to sign a short release that limits claims. If they insist on the original language, we should decline until after the postmortem.

Marissa Cole
Director, Marketing, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Request: can we publish Splint case study + quote?
messages.body:
Hi Alex / Taylor — thanks for the draft and for checking with us. We appreciate the partnership and are excited to see Splint featured, but we need to hold on publishing the current draft.

Why: Splint experienced a production incident on 2026-06-10 which is still in active post-incident review. To avoid any perception mismatch, we can't approve broad performance or business-impact claims right now (e.g., percentage improvements or statements implying uninterrupted production availability).

What we can offer immediately: a short, factual quote limited to a specific feature or workflow (for example: "Redwood reduced inference latency on our invoice-processing model, improving developer ve...

- Deliverables: (1) bankwest_bcp_control_matrix.xlsx (control-by-question mapping); (2) SOC2_Type2_Redwood_2025.pdf (redacted where needed); (3) rto_rpo_extraction_summary.csv (per-deployment, per-region RTO/RPO); (4) short FAQ about evidence access and on-prem requirements.

title: Convert intro demo into architecture sprint — reschedule request
body:
Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwoodinference.com>: Hi Alex / Priya —

Thanks again for the interest in Redwood. I scheduled a short intro demo next week so we can walk through the platform and share high-level fit:

 • When: Thu, Jun 18, 10:00–10:30 AM PT
 • Format: 30-min overview + Q&A
 • Objective: confirm primary integration patterns and next steps (POC / scoping)

Attached are the intro slides we generally use (Intro_Slides.pdf). If you already have specific ...

Our lead architect and platform engineer want to understand LLM serving options, VPC/private deployments, and the dedicated capacity tradeoffs.

- Ben: draft a pricing workbook with slabs and a pilot credit proposal (they prefer credits on first invoice). Include a 3-month pilot concession and a 12-month commit path.
- Rachel: quick confirm on tracing billing model (we typically bill ingest + query egress per million spans; clarify sample rates and any egress credits). Also please pull the SOC 2 pointer for them.

I'll own external comms and send the proposal once we have the workbook + SOW. Keep it tight — procurement is strict on timing.

Thanks,
Mateo
Product Partnerships, Redwood Inference

messages.subject: Re: FW: Questions on retention/tracing/dashboard pricing + pilot credits
messages.body:
Team — attached is a first-pass workbook (pricing-model-internal.xlsx). Key commercial suggestions: 

- Retention: slab tiers by GB/month — 0-5TB @ $120/GB-month (pilot list), 5-50TB @ $85/GB-month, >50TB custom. For pilot we can apply a 40% effective pilot discount (implemented as a one-time credit equal to 40% of expected first-invoice add-on charges) — aligns with earlier concessions for similar accounts.
- Tracing: bill ingest (spans) at $0.015/1k spans + egress/query at $0.05/1k queries. Offer 100k tracing credits for pilot (covering expected sampling during POC).
- Dashboards: seat-based $120/seat/mo + $0.01/query-unit. For pilot: 3 seats included, plus 500k query-units credit.
- Co...

messages.subject: Observability add-ons — pilot proposal and SOW
messages.body:
Hi Sara / Procurement team,

Thanks for the clear ask. Attached is our workbook (AH-pricing-proposal.xlsx) and a draft SOW (SOW-AtlasHealth-v1.pdf) for review. Highlights / proposed structure: 

1) Retention (logs/metrics/traces stored): tiered GB/month model with simple slabs. For the pilot we propose a one-time credit equal to 30% of anticipated add-on charges (applied to the first invoice). If AtlasHealth moves to a 12-month commit after pilot we would transition to the committed slab pricing (an additional 10% off list prices).

2) Tracing: ingest billed per 1k spans; egress/query billed per 1k queries. For the pilot we will include 50k spans credit and 200k query-unit credit (to cove...

messages.subject: Re: Observability add-ons — pilot proposal and SOW
messages.body:
Hi Mateo / Sara,

Thanks for the materials — workbook looks tidy and SOW is a good start. A few asks from finance/procurement before we can sign: 

1) Discount: finance is requesting a 30% concession on add-on charges for the pilot (applied to invoices for the first 90 days). They prefer a % discount vs a one-time credit for accounting reasons. Can you accommodate 30% instead of 30% equivalent credit?

2) Credits timing: finance wants any credits/discounts to be applied across the first two invoices (roughly first 60 days) rather than a single invoice.

3) Seats: can the 3 pilot seats be converted to a 6-month reduced seat-rate if we extend to proof-level usage? They prefer stepped down s...

messages.subject: Re: Observability add-ons — pilot proposal and SOW (revised term sheet)
messages.body:
Hi procurement team / Sara — appreciate the clarity and timeline. We can accommodate most of your asks with a small commercial tradeoff: 

- Pilot discount: we can convert the pilot concession to a 28% invoice discount applied across the first two invoices (approx first 60 days). We cannot go to 30% without a 24-month commit given our current pricing guardrails; 28% is the max we can approve for a 90-day/60-day two-invoice spread without a longer-term lock.
- Seat conversion: we will convert the 3 pilot seats to a 6-month reduced seat-rate at 15% off list if you elect to continue after pilot (applies months 1–6 post-pilot).
- Tracing credits: we'll increase tracing/queries credits to cove...

messages.body:
Hi Elena / Markus —

Thanks for the call yesterday. Attached is our working redline to the Order Form DPA (Novapraxis_OrderForm_DPA_Redline_v1.docx). High-level asks from our side:

- Confirm whether you want to incorporate the SCCs as an annex to the DPA (our default suggestion) or reference the EU Commission decision directly in the Order Form and keep a short DPA.
- We proposed a narrow transfer addendum (Section 6) to capture non-EU subprocessors and cross-border routing exceptions — please flag any operational constraints.
- On roles: we suggested a controller/processor mapping table (pg. 3) and a simple delegation clause for subprocessors that requires prior notice for high-risk sub...

messages.subject: Re: Finalize DPA language for Order Form (SCCs + transfer addendum)
messages.body:
Alex —

Thanks, received. We reviewed the redline quickly and have three immediate flags:

1) Subprocessor notice: we need a shorter notice window for onboarding (proposing 10 business days instead of 30) — our procurement team requires faster confirmation cycles.
2) Encryption/Key management: can you confirm which KMS integrations Redwood supports for customer-managed keys? We need this detail before agreeing to the proposed language in Section 5.
3) Liability carveouts: our counsel is asking to cap processor liability for indirect damages in the DPA — can you advise whether that is a redline we should expect pushback on?

I’m copying Markus (ops) to confirm if any of the transfer addend...

messages.subject: Re: Finalize DPA language for Order Form (SCCs + transfer addendum)
messages.body:
Elena —

Thanks for the early read and flags. Quick responses inline: 

- Subprocessor notice: we can accept 10 business days for non-critical subprocessors. For high-risk subprocessors (model hosting, telemetry, or customer KMS hosting) we request 30 days + right to pause onboarding. If this is acceptable, we will add a short definitional table to avoid ambiguity.

- KMS / CMK: Redwood supports AWS KMS customer-managed keys and a bring-your-own-key flow for GCP (Cloud KMS) in our Private deployment. I’ll add an explicit implementation note to the DPA (new Section 5.2) and a cross-reference to the security whitepaper.

- Liability carveouts: cap on indirect damages — we are generally cons...

messages.subject: Fwd: Finalize DPA language for Order Form (SCCs + transfer addendum) — counsel notes
messages.body:
Alex / Karthik —

Quick note from external counsel (Graystone): I reviewed your redline + the proposed annex. Short summary before the call: 

1) SCCs: embedding the Commission SCCs as an annex is the cleanest path. Add a short mechanism for updating to future SCC versions by mutual written agreement. Avoid automatic amendment language.

2) Transfer addendum: limit routing exceptions to named non-EU subprocessors and require documented justification per transfer. That will preserve NovaPraxis’s audit posture.

3) Subprocessor notice: 10 business days is reasonable if the onboarding checklist includes security attestations; keep 30 days for anything that involves MI/aggregate telemetry or ...

- SOC2_TypeII_Redacted_2025.pdf: Type II period 2025-01-01 to 2025-12-31. Scope includes Redwood hosted inference service, API endpoints, key management processes, and backup/restore. We redacted customer-identifying artifacts and some network diagrams for public sharing.

- Limited liability carveout specifically for marketplace listing metadata (we kept the standard security obligations).
- Clarified data retention and deletion timelines for trial accounts created via marketplace.
- Added reference to the SOC 2 attestation provided (see attached).

Request: please confirm whether the redline is acceptable or provide a short list of required edits. If it looks good, we can route the final DPA through DocuSign — link below.

1) Can you confirm the crypto/encryption controls for data at rest (KMS usage, CMKs vs AWS-managed keys)? A short control narrative is fine.

Quick summary: Sunrise Prep (K-12 district) surfaced a request for a FERPA addendum and wants to confirm our subprocessor commitments for student data. Separately, Northshore Bank (pilot) raised GLBA questions during procurement and asked if we can map data flows and provide a GLBA clause set. Both asks impact our DPA templates and subprocessor disclosure cadence.

Immediate asks:
- Stephanie: can Legal reu...

Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com>: Thanks for the summary. High level: we can reuse large parts of the HIPAA/BAA framework (data classification, access controls, encryption-in-transit/at-rest) but FERPA has student-education-record-specific definitions and parental rights language we must incorporate. GLBA needs explicit consumer financial data definitions and a different data-share consent model.

messages.subject: SunPrep: FERPA & GLBA addenda request — next steps
messages.body:
Team,

Quick summary: Sunrise Prep (K-12 district) surfaced a request for a FERPA addendum and wants to confirm our subprocessor commitments for student data. Separately, Northshore Bank (pilot) raised GLBA questions during procurement and asked if we can map data flows and provide a GLBA clause set. Both asks impact our DPA templates and subprocessor disclosure cadence.

Immediate asks:
- Stephanie: can Legal reuse the HIPAA/BAA playbook as a template or do we need new language for FERPA/GLBA?
- Ben: can you produce a short subprocessor export (last 90 days) plus the proposed residency/transfer guarantees we can offer for US K-12?
- Grace: timeline on SOC/Audit artifacts we can share (re...

messages.subject: Re: SunPrep: FERPA & GLBA addenda request — next steps
messages.body:
Thanks for the summary. High level: we can reuse large parts of the HIPAA/BAA framework (data classification, access controls, encryption-in-transit/at-rest) but FERPA has student-education-record-specific definitions and parental rights language we must incorporate. GLBA needs explicit consumer financial data definitions and a different data-share consent model.

Action items I propose:
1) I will draft two short addendum templates (FERPA addendum, GLBA addendum) and annotate where language differs from our standard DPA. Target: Tue 4/14 EOD.
2) Ben, please attach the subprocessor export and the residency guarantees (e.g., US-only cache TTL, cancelable export) — we need to confirm whether...

messages.subject: Re: FERPA & GLBA addenda implementation pathways
messages.body:
Alex — thanks for the quick heads up. A few clarifications from our end before you draft:

- FERPA scope: we only need the addendum to cover PII in education records for K-12 students when Redwood processes student-submitted content and grading metadata.
- Subprocessors: we must publish a list and get 30-day notice for additions. We understand sensitive infra vendors may be redacted but need a contact for escalation.
- Data residency: Sunrise requires that student content for our district be stored within US regions; ephemeral caching in other regions is permitted for inference if TTL < 1 hour.

Attachments: Our procurement team attached a short checklist (FERPA_QA_checklist.pdf). Can you...

messages.subject: Re: SunPrep: FERPA & GLBA addenda implementation pathways
messages.body:
Alex / Steph — attaching the quick export: GLBA_party_matrix.xlsx shows subprocessors with processing roles and whether they see plaintext. Summary: no logging provider receives unencrypted student content; logs are pseudonymized and access-controlled. We do use a third-party telemetry pipeline (TelemetryCo) that receives metadata (error traces, token counts) but no student PII, and it's configurable to drop payloads for specific customers.

I also confirmed our storage for K-12 customers can be pinned to US regions; inference edges may run in multi-region pools but we can add TTL constraints (suggest 30m default for student content).

Attachments: GLBA_party_matrix.xlsx

—Ben
Ben Carter
...

cc: Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie_nguyen@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Maya Chen <maya.chen@colelaw.com>
messages.subject: Proposed next steps and timeline (Sunrise Prep FERPA addendum)
messages.body:
Alicia — thanks for the clarifications and for the checklist. Quick update and next steps: 

1) Feasibility: Redwood can support the FERPA scope you described. We can commit to US-only storage for student content and configurable inference TTLs. We will provide a subprocessor list and 30-day notice for changes; for sensitive subprocessors we will provide a redacted entry plus a named escalation contact.

2) Attachments & artifacts: Stephanie (Legal) will draft a FERPA addendum and annotate points that differ from our standard DPA. Ben attached an operations matrix confirming no subprocessor ingests unencrypted student content (see GLBA_party_matrix.xlsx). Grace will provide a redacted SOC...

- For your account we aggregate p95 a...

messages.subject: Challenge with p95 measurement buckets and monthly SLO for hosted API
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for the demo last week — the team liked Redwood's latency profiles and the cost controls. One operational concern came up during our SRE review: the way p95 is being measured across short rolling buckets exposes us to monthly credit risk because a handful of isolation events in an unlucky hour can push the monthly p95 over the commitment.

Specifically: FinNova's traffic is spiky (batch imports + interactive widgets). We saw three minutes last week where a regional infra issue inflated the p95 for that 1-hour bucket and caused our monitoring to flag a breach even though the rest of the month looked fine. For a contracted monthly p95 that matters.

Can Redwood prop...

messages.subject: Re: Challenge with p95 measurement buckets and monthly SLO for hosted API
messages.body:
Hi Jake — appreciate the clear reproduction and the sample report.

Short answer: yes, we can do a pragmatic, time-boxed bridging plan while you finish the caching rollout. I sketched two complementary approaches below and attached a draft SLO addendum and a diagram of the measurement buckets for reference.

Option A — Measurement-bucket consolidation (preferred):
- For your account we aggregate p95 across rolling 6-hour windows (instead of strict 1-hour buckets) for the purpose of monthly SLO computation.
- This reduces sensitivity to short, concentrated blips while preserving visibility into tail behavior.
- Retroactive application: we can start the consolidated measurement at the begin...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: RE: Proposal: measurement-bucket consolidation + temporary bridging plan for p50/p95 hosted API SLOs
messages.body:
Rachel / Karthik — quick internal on the options. Quoting Rachel's summary below for context.

On Option A (6-hour consolidated measurement):
- Legal: easier to operationalize; minimal redlines. Need a clear definition of how rolling windows align with billing cycles (we recommend "align to UTC day with 6-hour rolling aggregation for billing computation").
- Finance: OK with retroactive start at next billing period; ensure we cap retroactive liability.

On Option B (60-day bridging):
- Legal: accept but want explicit criteria for denial (e.g., deliberate customer-induced spikes) and an audit trail obligation.
- Product/Telemetry: provide the link to the internal SLO visualization (we shou...

messages.subject: Re: Proposal: measurement-bucket consolidation + temporary bridging plan for p50/p95 hosted API SLOs
messages.body:
Rachel / Monica — thanks for the fast turnaround. After an internal readout we prefer Option A (measurement-bucket consolidation to a 6-hour rolling aggregation) to reduce noise in monthly billing and avoid administrative exceptions. Option B is fine as a fallback but not necessary if Option A is applied at the next billing cycle.

Please proceed with the addendum for Option A and share the DocuSign link. We'll expect the consolidated measurement to begin on 2026-04-01 and would like the scoped telemetry view you mentioned so our SREs can validate once the new window is live.

Also request a joint 30-day check-in with Redwood CS/AE to review observed tail behavior and confirm whether a pe...

title: Post-call synthesis: PayFlux pilot economics, levers & decision plan
body:
jonas_weber <jonas@redwood.ai>: Great speaking with you both today — thanks for the time. This thread is a concise recap so we have alignment on pain, success criteria, and next steps for a small pilot focused on payments fraud detection UX. High level takeaways: 

- Pain: high friction from false positives in fraud flow; manual review cost ~ $120k/year + latency hurting conversion.
- Goal: reduce manual reviews by 40% within pilot scope while k...

Marta Ruiz <marta@payflux.io>: Jonas — thanks for the clear summary and the workbook. Quick answers:

messages.subject: Post-call synthesis: PayFlux pilot economics, levers & decision plan
messages.body:
Great speaking with you both today — thanks for the time. This thread is a concise recap so we have alignment on pain, success criteria, and next steps for a small pilot focused on payments fraud detection UX. High level takeaways: 

- Pain: high friction from false positives in fraud flow; manual review cost ~ $120k/year + latency hurting conversion.
- Goal: reduce manual reviews by 40% within pilot scope while keeping false positives within the current tolerance (+/- 1%).
- Budget guardrails: procurement indicated a target ceiling of ~$30k for the 8-week pilot (inclusive of inference).

Cost levers we discussed (initial sizing in attached spreadsheet):
- Token economics: average 450 tok...

messages.body:
Jonas — thanks for the clear summary and the workbook. Quick answers: 

- Traffic: initial sample is closer to 18k evaluations/day (we exclude low-value batch rechecks).

Can you include a conservative scenario that assumes no batching (real-time flow only) and an optimistic scenario with 2x batching for background checks? We need both to show up in our internal budget ask.

Also: curious on latency tradeoff if we enable prefix caching — would that materially impact real-time scoring (target <250ms p95)?

messages.subject: Fwd: Post-call synthesis: PayFlux pilot economics, levers & decision plan
messages.body:
Looping in a short pricing thought for the two-scenario ask.

Jonas / Alex — for the conservative (real-time only) path, lead with a fixed pilot cap (e.g., $28k) that covers up to X tokens and a clear overage rate. For the optimistic (batching) path, offer a bi-modal price: a lower per-token rate for batched traffic + a small standing monthly connector fee. This lets procurement justify the pilot as cost-saving if batching works.

Also suggest including an optional evaluation add-on: 1k sample calls for $1,500 (covers instrumentation + a short report). Helps get a concrete number into their budget.

— Monica
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
monica@redwood.ai

messages.subject: RE: Post-call synthesis: PayFlux pilot economics, levers & decision plan
messages.body:
Marta — quick technical notes to address your latency + caching question. Short answer: prefix/KV caching typically helps warm-starts and repeated-context flows; it rarely increases p95 for cold real-time calls if we keep the model pinned to low-latency variant.

Notes: 
- Real-time scoring (<250ms p95) is achievable with a 13B int8 variant and small batch sizes (1-4).
- Batching (2-8) for background checks can increase throughput 1.8-2.5x depending on request variance; intrinsic latency for batched windows increases but asynchronous flows tolerate it.
- Estimated latency impact for prefix cache hits: often reduces time-to-first-token by ~10-25ms on warm paths; misses behave like normal c...

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday. Procurement asked us to pull a baseline SLA we can take to legal. Two quick asks we want to see in a formal template:

- Uptime: 99.99% monthly
- Remedies: monthly service credit formula (tiered by monthly availability shortfall) and a cap at no less than 30% of monthly fees

We also want clarity around maintenance carve-outs, rolling window calculations, and...

rachel.kim@redwood.com: Hi Tom —

Appreciate the clarity. We can do a draft redline. Quick context up front: our standard hosted SLA is 99.95% with a tiered monthly credit schedule and a cap that usually sits at 30% of monthly fees. For pilots we have flexibility on carve-outs and the operational window, but 99.99% is a materially tighter commitment that typically requires dedicated capacity or higher reservation levels.

messages.subject: Fwd: SLAs for pilot — internal intake (Neptune)
messages.body:
Rachel / Karthik —

Quick internal take before I draft redline:

- 99.99% is high for a multi-tenant hosted offering; we should offer a path: 99.95% standard, 99.99% if Customer takes Dedicated or committed baseline (monthly commit).
- Remedies: propose a simple three-tier monthly credit schedule (below 99.95 -> 10%, below 99.8 -> 20%, below 99.0 -> 50%) with an overall cap at 50% for extreme cases. For commercial deals we usually cap at 30% — but for negotiated exceptions we can be explicit about carve-outs and escalation triggers.
- Rolling window: use calendar month with clearly defined maintenance windows and planned maintenance carve-outs (>=72 hours notice for events >2 hours).
- Ex...

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: SLAs for pilot — internal intake (Neptune)
messages.body:
Quick ops note:

- 99.99% sustained across a calendar month for a general hosted pool with bursty LLM workloads is risky without reserved capacity. Intermittent model loading, large-batch inference spikes, and upstream network outages are common causes.
- If Neptune wants 99.99 we should require either Dedicated or a defined reservation commitment (example: 2x expected qps, or a committed spend threshold).
- Maintenance carve-outs: we can accept planned maintenance as carve-outs with 72 hours' notice. Emergency maintenance limited to critical security patches with post-mortem and no credits.

I can include a short operational annex that defines measurement points (Health checks, synthetic...

messages.subject: Proposed redline: uptime & monthly remedy schedule (draft)
messages.body:
Tom —

Please find a first-pass redline attached. Highlights and proposed changes:

- Baseline availability: 99.95% (calendar month) for hosted service.
- Option: 99.99% availability if Customer elects Dedicated capacity or signs a minimum commitment (recommendation: $75k/mo committed spend or equivalent capacity).
- Monthly remedy (simple tiers):
 - Availability >= 99.95%: no credit
 - Availability >= 99.80% and < 99.95%: 10% service credit for that month
 - Availability >= 99.00% and < 99.80%: 25% service credit for that month
 - Availability < 99.00%: 50% service credit (and eligibility for termination for cause after two consecutive months)
- Cap: credits for any month will not exceed...

messages.subject: Re: Proposed redline: uptime & monthly remedy schedule (draft)
messages.body:
Rachel —

Thanks for the fast turn. The redline looks closer to what we can take to legal. A few follow-ups before we sync:

1) Can you confirm the measurement source for availability (is it Redwood monitoring or third-party synthetic checks)? We prefer a joint measurement or a neutral endpoint.
2) On the remedy cap: Neptune's procurement will push for 30% cap rather than 50% for commercial fairness — would Redwood be willing to make 30% the negotiated cap if we sign a 12-month reserved commit?
3) Exit remedy: the two-month termination right is acceptable but procurement wants a 30-day cure period before termination is effective.

We are open to Dedicated if the delta on committed spend v...
