messages.subject: AWS Private Offer: term extension & burst capacity for Acme Logistics
messages.body:
Hi Lara,

Following up on our renewal planning call — thanks again for the time. Per Acme's growth forecast, you mentioned wanting to lock in a longer term and add burst capacity for peak months. To make procurement simpler on your side we can: 

- Extend the Private Offer term from 12 to 24 months
- Increase committed capacity from 40 to 80 concurrent inference units (2x)
- Add a capped burst pool of +30% available for peak months billed separately

I've attached a draft price matrix with the commit/ burst breakdown. The private offer structure will live in AWS Marketplace (we'll push a revised Private Offer ID once terms are final).

Ask: can you confirm who on Acme will be the PO signa...

messages.subject: Re: AWS Private Offer: term extension & burst capacity for Acme Logistics
messages.body:
Neha —

Thanks, the structure looks promising. A couple quick items from procurement: 

- We need pricing tied to an AWS Private Offer ID and an updated SOW for our PO review.
- Signatory will be Pete Smith (VP Ops) — CC'd.
- Preferred payment terms: Net 60. Is that manageable with the 24-month term?

I'll loop Legal once we have the updated offer + SOW so they can sanity-check standard clauses. Can you send the updated price matrix with line items for commit vs burst and an expected invoice cadence?

—Lara
Lara Chen
Procurement Manager | Acme Logistics
lara.chen@acme-logistics.com

messages.subject: Re: AWS Private Offer pricing + approval for Acme (RDW-7421)
messages.body:
Quick sync — I reviewed the proposed 24-month extension + 2x commit increase. High level:

- Margin check: acceptable if we keep effective commit unit price within the range in RDW-Acme-PrivateOffer-v3.xlsx.
- Discount: we can offer a 5% term discount for 24-months (applied to commit units), plus a separate burst rate.
- Finance flagged invoice cadence: net-45 preferred, but procurement requested Net-60; okay to approve Net-60 for this strategic customer.

Next steps for you:
1) Update the pricing sheet with the 5% term discount and explicit invoice cadence lines.
2) Draft a short SOW (2-pg) that references the Private Offer ID and deployment milestones so Legal has something concrete.

I...

messages.subject: Updated: Private Offer ID + pricing (RDW-7421)
messages.body:
Hi Lara / Pete —

Thanks for the quick turnaround. Per Rishi's note, attached is an updated price matrix with the 5% term discount applied to the 24-month commit rows and a distinct burst rate for the capped pool. Key callouts:

- Commit: 80 units (2x previous) at discounted commit unit price.
- Burst: up to +30% of commit (metered monthly) at burst rate.
- Term: 24 months.
- Invoice cadence: Net 60 (per Acme's request).
- AWS Private Offer ID (provisional): AWS-PO-0a1b2c (we will confirm the final offer ID after AWS publishes the amended offer).

Attached: RDW-Acme-PrivateOffer-v3.xlsx, SOW-Acme-2026.pdf (2-page SOW with milestones and acceptance criteria).

Can you confirm: 
- PO signat...

messages.subject: Re: Updated: Private Offer ID + pricing (RDW-7421)
messages.body:
Neha/Lara —

Legal reviewed the SOW and pricing. Two quick asks before we sign: 

1) Data retention & deletion clause: we need explicit language for logs and telemetry retention (ACME policy is 24 months) and a mechanism for deletion on contract termination.
2) Termination for convenience: can we include a 90-day termination for convenience (pro-rated refunds for any prepayments)?

Also confirm invoice cadence (Net 60) and whether Redwood will accept PO referencing the AWS Private Offer ID in lieu of a separate master services agreement (our procurement prefers minimal paperwork).

If you can provide a tracked-change SOW with the retention language and the termination clause language, we ...

messages.subject: Final: DocuSign & AWS Private Offer live (RDW-7421)
messages.body:
Hi all —

Thanks for the reviews and the quick legal feedback. We've incorporated Acme's two items:

- Data retention: SOW section 3.2 updated to state telemetry/logs retention of 24 months and a deletion procedure on termination.
- Termination for convenience: Added a 90-day termination for convenience clause with pro-rated refund language for any prepayment (SOW section 5.1).

Next steps: 
1) DocuSign envelope created: https://docs.docusign.com/envelope/ABC1234 (envelope includes the SOW and the Private Offer cover sheet).
2) AWS Private Offer has been pushed (final offer id: AWS-PO-0a1b2c) — your procurement can accept on the AWS Marketplace console which will link to the DocuSign enve...

messages.subject: Order Form redlines — SLA uptime & credits
messages.body:
Rafael — thanks again for the call this morning. Attaching GridPay's initial redline of the order form focused on SLA and maintenance language. High-level asks:

- Move uptime target from 99.9% to 99.99% for production (customer-facing) traffic.
- Remove/raise the credit cap on cumulative monthly credits (we're pushing for uncapped credits beyond a threshold).
- Shorten P1 response time from 1 hour to 30 minutes and require a 2-hour mitigation/update cadence until resolution.
- Limit scheduled maintenance to a single weekly 2-hour window per region and require 72-hour advance notice; exclude emergency maintenance only with explicit consent.

Also attaching a short excerpt of our runbook s...

messages.subject: Re: Order Form redlines — SLA uptime & credits
messages.body:
Emily — appreciate the clear asks and the runbook excerpt. Looping in Lauren (legal), Rachel (ops), and Aditya (SRE/measurements) to coordinate. Quick points from my side:

- Uptime target: 99.99% is a material change with significant capacity and cost implications. We typically offer a 99.9% production SLA with a tiered credit schedule. We can propose a narrower maintenance exclusion window and a higher credit tier at 99.95% for customers committing to reserved capacity.

- Credits cap: we can increase the monthly cap and add an escrow-style carveout for sustained outages, but uncapped credits are very challenging without an alternative commercial commitment.

- Response times: happy to ...

messages.subject: RE: Order Form redlines — SLA uptime & credits (legal redlines attached)
messages.body:
Thanks, Rafael and Emily. I've reviewed GridPay's redlines and drafted legal track changes with alternative language that balances GridPay's ask and Redwood's operational constraints. Summary of proposed legal positions: 

1) Uptime measurement and metric: define "Availability" as inbound request success rate measured at the per-tenant load-balancer egress with a 5xx/timeout threshold; specify 30-day rolling measurement window. This avoids noisy minute-by-minute fluctuations.

2) Credit structure: introduce a two-tier credit model: a higher uncapped multiplier for incidents meeting the "Sustained Partial Outage" definition (>= 6 hours continuous impact affecting >= 20% requests), and a ca...

messages.subject: Re: Order Form redlines — SLA uptime & credits
messages.body:
Lauren / Rafael — thanks for the quick turnaround and the clearer legal language. The two-tier credit structure is promising; GridPay can live with a "Sustained Partial Outage" clause if the thresholds are explicit and the measurement window is per-tenant. A couple of clarifying asks before we sign: 

- Emergency maintenance: please add an explicit escalation path and a weekend-oncall notification requirement for emergencies that could cause customer impact. We need a contact and a phone escalation in addition to email.
- Rollback guarantees: we would like a back-out plan to be documented in the runbook and a commitment to attempt rollback when a change increases errors by >5% within the ...

title: Re: Pricing mechanics for private deployment — node commit / platform-fee offsets
body:
Tom Hargrove <tom.hargrove@medisys.com>: Rachel, thanks again for the demo last week. Procurement asked me to get a clear write-up on how Redwood handles the interplay between node-based commit, platform fee amortization, and support tiers for on-prem/VPC deployments. A few specific asks: 

- If we commit to 12 nodes for 12 months, how do you apply the platform fee? Is it amortized, billed upfront, or can we offset it against node commits?
- Are operator...

Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>: Tom — thanks for the clear list, and appreciate the SOW. High level answers up front, then I'll follow with a worked example and a proposal for options you can present to procurement.

Short answers:
- Platform fee: normally invoiced as an annual platform fee (can be annualized or invoiced upfront). We frequently amortize it across node commits if the customer signs a multi-year reserved capaci...

normalized.subject: Re: Pricing mechanics for private deployment — node commit / platform-fee offsets

messages.subject: Private deployment pricing mechanics — need clarity on offsets
messages.body:
Rachel, thanks again for the demo last week. Procurement asked me to get a clear write-up on how Redwood handles the interplay between node-based commit, platform fee amortization, and support tiers for on-prem/VPC deployments. A few specific asks: 

- If we commit to 12 nodes for 12 months, how do you apply the platform fee? Is it amortized, billed upfront, or can we offset it against node commits?
- Are operator licenses (per-seat) treated separately or included in node pricing?
- What tradeoffs exist if we want a lower platform fee in exchange for a longer commit or higher oncall retainer?

I've attached our draft SOW (Medisys_SOW_v1.pdf) and a simple node-count forecast. Can you advis...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment pricing mechanics — need clarity on offsets
messages.body:
Tom — thanks for the clear list, and appreciate the SOW. High level answers up front, then I'll follow with a worked example and a proposal for options you can present to procurement.

Short answers:
- Platform fee: normally invoiced as an annual platform fee (can be annualized or invoiced upfront). We frequently amortize it across node commits if the customer signs a multi-year reserved capacity agreement — that yields a lower monthly effective platform charge.
- Operator licenses: sold as an add-on (operator-seat or cluster license). For larger node commits we can bundle a number of operator seats into the node SKU.
- Tradeoffs: lower platform fee can be achieved via longer commits, hig...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment pricing mechanics — need clarity on offsets
messages.body:
Rachel / Alex — preliminary numbers below; assume list prices for this exercise (we can apply negotiated discounts later).

Assumptions:
- Node list: $10k / node / month (licensed throughput-based SKU)
- Platform fee: $120k / year (standard for this tier)
- Operator license: $12k / seat / year
- Implementation credit (one-time): $40k

Scenario: 12 nodes committed, month 1-6 stable at 12, month 7-18 linear ramp to 36 nodes. 18-month term.

Simple approach: amortize platform fee pro-rata across committed node-months. Committed node-months = 12 nodes * 18 months = 216 node-months. Platform fee annual = $120k -> for 18 months = $180k. Per-node-month platform allocation = $180k / 216 = $833.33...

messages.subject: Re: Private deployment pricing mechanics — need clarity on offsets
messages.body:
Quick operator-seat guidance for Medisys: for up to 24 nodes we typically include 2 operator seats; for 25-48 nodes we include 4 seats. Additional seats priced at $9k/seat/year on negotiated deals. If they want the operator license bundled fully into the node SKU as a discount, we can decrease node list by 4% and include 3 seats for the 12-to-36-node ramp scenario.

Also flag: if they require air-gapped/on-prem packaging there is usually a packaging surcharge (one-time) — we should confirm their topology vs pure VPC.

Alex
Sales Engineering

cc: Lisa Moreno <lisa.moreno@medisys.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, procurement@medisys.com
messages.subject: Re: Pricing mechanics for private deployment — sample commercial options
messages.body:
Tom / Lisa — sorry for the delay; attached is a short two-option commercial summary and the node-rate worksheet (private-deploy-ratecard.xlsx). Highlights: 

Option A — 18-month committed path (amortized platform fee)
- Commit: 12 nodes, 18 months
- Platform fee: $180k for 18 months (amortized across 216 node-months) -> ~$833/node-month
- Node price (list): $10k -> effective node-month: $10,833
- Operator seats: 3 included (as Alex suggested, bundled); additional seats $9k/yr
- Implementation credit: $40k applied to year-1 platform fee (reduces platform to $140k)

Option B — 24-month anchor (better economics)
- Commit: 12 nodes, 24 months with ramp to 36 at month 12
- Platform fee: $216k ...

messages.subject: Re: Pricing mechanics for private deployment — sample commercial options
messages.body:
Rachel — this is very helpful, thanks. Procurement prefers an invoice schedule (quarterly) rather than full prepay. Medisys confirmed VPC-only (no airgap) so no packaging surcharge. Two quick asks before we share internally: 

1) Can you show Option A with platform fee reduced by the $40k implementation credit (explicit line items) and the quarterly invoice cadence?
2) For Option B, can you show the math if we instead commit to 36 months (what's the platform fee discount and effective per-node-month)?

If you can send those two small tables and a short paragraph describing the oncall retainer scope for the swap option, we'll put this on the procurement agenda next week.

I'll review the w...

body:
Diego Fernandez <diego@redwood.ai>: Hi Emma / Liam —

Thanks again for the call this week. Per our discussion, we'd like to run a 60-minute live enablement session focused on the Redwood Console + API quickstart and a short hands-on lab where we walk through tokenization, embedding calls, and a simple generation flow. Proposed slots (PT):

- Tue May 18 — 10:00–11:00
- Wed May 19 — 14:00–15:00
- Thu May 20 — 09:00–10:00

Please let me know which work...

Emma Brooks <emma@acmehealth.com>: Hi Diego —

Thanks for the options. Unfortunately our engineering team has a sprint deadline the week of the 18th and won't be available until the following week. Would you be able to move to the week of May 22?

Preferably the morning PT hours. Also we want the session to be API-first (we'll spin up code snippets rather than rely on the Console). Would it be possible for you to include a brief checkl...

messages.subject: Live API enablement - hands-on lab
messages.body:
Hi Emma / Liam —

Thanks again for the call this week. Per our discussion, we'd like to run a 60-minute live enablement session focused on the Redwood Console + API quickstart and a short hands-on lab where we walk through tokenization, embedding calls, and a simple generation flow. Proposed slots (PT):

- Tue May 18 — 10:00–11:00
- Wed May 19 — 14:00–15:00
- Thu May 20 — 09:00–10:00

Please let me know which works best and whether your team prefers a production-focused walkthrough or a sandbox-oriented lab (we can provide temporary API keys). I'll loop in our solutions engineer to lead the lab portion.

Attaching a short agenda for context.

Thanks,
Diego Fernandez
Account Executive | Re...

messages.subject: Re: Live API enablement - hands-on lab
messages.body:
Hi Diego —

Thanks for the options. Unfortunately our engineering team has a sprint deadline the week of the 18th and won't be available until the following week. Would you be able to move to the week of May 22?

Preferably the morning PT hours. Also we want the session to be API-first (we'll spin up code snippets rather than rely on the Console). Would it be possible for you to include a brief checklist of prerequisites (local env, Python version, curl examples)?

Best,
Emma Brooks
Director, Platform Integrations
Acme Health

--
Emma Brooks
Acme Health

messages.subject: Re: Live API enablement - hands-on lab
messages.body:
On Mon, May 18, 2026 at 8:22 AM Emma Brooks <emma@acmehealth.com> wrote:
> Thanks for the options. Unfortunately our engineering team has a sprint deadline the week of the 18th and won't be available until the following week. Would you be able to move to the week of May 22?
> Preferably the morning PT hours. Also we want the session to be API-first (we'll spin up code snippets rather than rely on the Console). Would it be possible for you to include a brief checklist of prerequisites (local env, Python version, curl examples)?

Hi Emma — absolutely. Would Fri May 22 at 10:00 AM PT work for your group? Marissa will run the hands-on lab — she can cover the Python quickstart and a short cURL...

messages.subject: Fwd: Live API enablement - hands-on lab (please review)
messages.body:
Marissa / Rachel —

Forwarding Acme's request. They've asked for an API-first, morning PT slot in the week of May 22 and specifically called out the need for Python + cURL snippets and a prereq checklist. Can you confirm availability to lead the live lab on Fri May 22 at 10:00 AM PT? If Marissa's out, Rachel can cover backup.

Also: please review the attached agenda and snippets and flag any changes by EOD Wed so I can send the final pre-read. I've linked the shared Drive folder here: https://drive.redwood.com/shared/acme-live-lab

Diego
--
Diego Fernandez
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

messages.subject: Re: Live API enablement - hands-on lab

Fri May 22 at 10:00 AM PT works for me. I'll lead the live lab and focus on the Python quickstart + cURL examples you requested. Attached is a short prereq checklist (Python 3.9+, pip, venv recommended) and a link to a small GitHub repo with runnable snippets.

Zoom link & calendar invite: https://zoom.us/j/1234567890 (invite sent separately)

- Install Python 3.9+ and create a venv
- pip install redwood-sdk (or run from snippets folder)
- Have curl available for the cURL examples
- Request temporary sandbox API key from Diego if you want to follow along in a live sandbox

See you F...

messages.subject: Quick: how do tokens map to billing across endpoints?
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks for taking a look at this. We're evaluating Redwood's hosted API for a pilot and need a clear mapping from tokens to billing units across the different endpoints. Our procurement and finance team are getting tripped up by the endpoint names — Chat Completions vs Responses vs the older /v1/completions — and want a short, concrete example we can put in the SOW.

Two asks:
- Can you confirm how tokens are counted for Chat Completions vs Responses vs legacy endpoints (are input + output both billed, how are system messages treated, are there different denominators/units)?
- Can you show a simple cost example (using your hosted baseline rates) for a 3,000-token interaction w...

messages.subject: Re: Quick: how do tokens map to billing across endpoints?
messages.body:
Hi Samir —

Thanks for the clear asks. Short answer up front, then a worked example you can paste into your SOW. I'm attaching our current hosted rate card (PDF) as requested.

High level:
- Tokens: For all text-generation endpoints we bill on a tokens-used basis that includes both the prompt (input) and the model output (assistant response). System messages or special metadata that are sent as part of the request are counted as input tokens.
- Endpoint differences: The main difference between Chat Completions and the newer Responses endpoint is interface and capability (structured outputs, function calling), not a fundamentally different token meter — both count input + output tokens. Ho...

messages.subject: Re: Quick: how do tokens map to billing across endpoints?
messages.body:
Nice summary — thanks. Two quick internal asks:
1) Samir's asking about a pilot discount. Do we have flexibility on the hosted rates for a 3–6 month pilot (projected ~20M tokens/month)?
2) If Acme is likely to convert to Dedicated in 6–12 months, can we present a blended commitment option (hosted pilot -> Dedicated reserved pricing) as part of the commercial proposal?

If you can confirm the pilot discount band and whether we can promise a migration path with a transitional pricing credit, I'll take this to solutions and finance.

Marissa
Director, Sales Operations
Redwood Inference

messages.subject: Re: Quick: how do tokens map to billing across endpoints?
messages.body:
Marissa / Sean — quick summary so we have a single line to present:
- Pilot discount: offer a one-time pilot rate of 10% off the hosted listed price for up to 6 months for pilots <= 30M tokens/month. This is an internal standard pilot concession (no long-term SLA change).
- Conversion/migration path: if customer commits to Dedicated within 12 months, we can provide a transitional credit equal to 25% of pilot spend to be applied toward the first 3 months of the Dedicated commitment, subject to legal review and a signed MOU.
- Next steps: Rachel will (a) confirm expected monthly token volumes with Acme, (b) share a short commercial note with the pilot rate and credit language for legal/fina...

messages.subject: Re: Quick: how do tokens map to billing across endpoints?
messages.body:
Thanks Rachel — got the rate card and the worked examples, very helpful. The pilot discount and the migration credit sound reasonable; procurement wants a short 30-minute call to confirm the terms and ask a few contract questions (SOC 2 evidence, PII retention controls).

Are you available tomorrow at 10am PT? If not, suggest two other times and we'll loop in legal.

Thanks — Samir

Thanks again for the conversation yesterday. Attached is an initial proposal for a deal-linked GPU pool that maps to the usage profile you shared: a medium-throughput inference service with occasional high-burst periods for batch reprocessing. The attached PDF lays out three footprint options, a recommended minimum-guarantee (min GPUs) per lane, and a staged ramp schedule aimed to balance cost pr...

Tom Everett <tom.everett@celestis.io>: Marissa —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the options. A few immediate procurement/finance questions before we pick a lane: 
1) Do you have a defined minimum MRC for the Compact lane or is the charge purely usage-based above the min GPU guarantee?
2) If we hit the burst capacity frequently (>=20% of days in a month), what are the change control/commit rules for moving lanes? Any cooling-o...

messages.body:
Hi Tom / Priya —

Thanks again for the conversation yesterday. Attached is an initial proposal for a deal-linked GPU pool that maps to the usage profile you shared: a medium-throughput inference service with occasional high-burst periods for batch reprocessing. The attached PDF lays out three footprint options, a recommended minimum-guarantee (min GPUs) per lane, and a staged ramp schedule aimed to balance cost predictability and headroom.

High-level ask: could you confirm which of the three footprints (Compact / Standard / Expanded) best aligns with your Q3 roadmap? Once you pick a lane we can finalize the ramp dates and propose a small SLA uplift for mid-month heavy batching windows.

...

messages.subject: Re: Proposal: tailored GPU pool options for Celestis (initial draft)
messages.body:
Marissa —

Appreciate the quick turnaround and the options. A few immediate procurement/finance questions before we pick a lane: 
1) Do you have a defined minimum MRC for the Compact lane or is the charge purely usage-based above the min GPU guarantee?
2) If we hit the burst capacity frequently (>=20% of days in a month), what are the change control/commit rules for moving lanes? Any cooling-off or prorate windows?
3) We need an estimate for one-time onboarding credits or near-term warm-start credits (our finance team asked specifically).

Technical aside: our infra team will want the exact min GPU mapping to instance types for planning network/router quotas (they need vCPU/GPU ratios). C...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Tom Everett <tom.everett@celestis.io>, Priya Shah <priya.shah@celestis.io>
messages.subject: Fwd: Proposal: tailored GPU pool options for Celestis (initial draft)
messages.body:
Karthik —

Forwarding Tom's questions (below). We need a crisp, decision-ready response that covers: min MRC / billing behavior for Compact lane, change-control rules if they sustain burst usage, onboarding credits, and an instance-level mapping you can commit to for the SOW. Marissa has the commercial lead; please cc her on technical specifics.

Target: reply to Tom with numbers and a proposed ramp timeline by end of day tomorrow so we can get Procurement moving. If any of this requires escalation to Eng Leadership for capacity guarantees, flag it.

Thanks —
Rachel
Rachel Kim
Senior AE, Redwood Inference

Thanks for the clear list. Below are concise answers and a recommended ramp + billing model that balances predictability and flexibility. I also attached an internal cost delta matrix to guide the SLA uplift conversation (SLA_uplift_matrix.xlsx).

- Min monthly recurring charge (MRC) for Compact is tied to the 16-min GPU guarantee. We propose a flat MRC that covers 16 GPUs at baseline utilization (price detailed in the PDF). Usage above the 16-GPU guarantee is billed as tokenized usage with a tiered burst rate; the first 100GPU-hrs above baseline / month are billed at a discounted burst rate, thereafter at standard on-demand. 1) Please remove the specific latency micro-benchmark on page 3 (\"p50 = 18ms\") — that metric varies by region and we don’t want per-region breakdowns in a public case study.
2) Change the cost-savings phrasing to ...

Ideal timeline: if we can get final feedback by EOD Thu 2/15 we can schedule design and publish the week of 2/22. If that’s to...

messages.subject: Re: Draft case study: Olive Health — quote and fact-check
messages.body:
Marcus —

Thanks for the draft. Overall looks good, but our comms team requested a few changes (attached doc has inline redlines). Top-line notes here so they’re easy to act on: 

1) Please remove the specific latency micro-benchmark on page 3 (\"p50 = 18ms\") — that metric varies by region and we don’t want per-region breakdowns in a public case study.
2) Change the cost-savings phrasing to be \"up to ~48%\" instead of a definitive number; legal prefers a softer claim.
3) Our preferred public quote is slightly different — see redline on the first page.

We can approve with those edits. Also attaching a Word doc with tracked changes for convenience. If you integrate and send back the upda...

to: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Fwd: Draft case study: Olive Health — quote and fact-check
messages.body:
Folks — forwarding Elena’s redlines (attached). Quick notes / asks: 

- Monica: can you propose the customer-facing language for the \"cost-savings\" line and the alternate quote Elena suggested? I want the marketing team to have something polished to drop into the doc.
- Marissa: can you confirm whether removing the p50 latency callout has design or storytelling implications? If we remove that callout, what would you like to replace it with (e.g., a general \"latency improved\" sentence + relative performance chart)?
- Rachel: legal flagged the \"definitive\" cost savings number; can you confirm if \"up to ~48%\" is acceptable and whether we need a release form before publication?

Targe...

messages.subject: Re: Draft case study: Olive Health — quote and fact-check
messages.body: [See attached redlines]

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Draft case study: Olive Health — quote and fact-check
messages.body:
Thanks for the ping — reviewing the redlines now. High-level suggestions below: 

- Cost-savings line: use \"up to ~48% reduction in per-token inference cost (workload dependent)\". That keeps the number but adds context.
- Latency: replace the p50 callout with \"consistent sub-100ms p50 on typical production workloads\" — that avoids region-specific numbers while remaining concrete.
- Quote: Elena's suggested text is good; propose a slightly shorter variant if space is tight: 
 \"Redwood Inference helped us scale inference without sacrificing latency or quality.\"

I’ve pasted the proposed snippets into the attached doc (comments). If Marketing and Legal are happy, I’m comfortable moving...

to: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Draft case study: Olive Health — quote and fact-check
messages.body:
All — legal has reviewed the proposed language. Summary: 

- \"Up to ~48%\" is acceptable provided we include the parenthetical \"workload dependent\" and we keep a saved calculation methodology internally.
- Removing the exact p50 figure is fine; the suggested \"sub-100ms p50\" is reasonable as long as we don’t present it as guaranteed SLA.
- We do need a signed release before publishing. I’m attaching our standard short release for customer OKs — if Olive can sign that (or provide their standard equivalent), we’re good.

I made two tiny edits to the quote for clarity (tracked in the PDF). Approve on my side once we get their signed release.

Rachel Kim
Senior Counsel | Redwood Inference...

cc: Daniel Park <dpark@olivehealth.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>

messages.body:
Elena — thank you for the quick turnaround and for the redlines. Attached is the integrated final PDF: 

- We changed the cost line to: \"Up to ~48% reduction in per-token inference cost (workload dependent)\"
- Replaced the p50 micro-benchmark with: \"consistent sub-100ms p50 on typical production workloads\"
- Updated the front-page quote to the slightly shortened variant Monica suggested. Rachel has given legal approval on the edits and attached a short release form.

Next step: could you confirm by reply that the wording looks correct to you and either: 
1) attach a signed release (signed_release_olive.pdf) in reply, or
2) confirm we can proceed and we’ll send a DocuSign link for your...

messages.subject: Nimble AI redlines - MSA (Liability / OSS)
messages.body:
Hi Marissa,

Thanks for the demo last week. Attached are Nimble's redlines to your standard MSA. The key open items for us are:
- Limitation of liability: a mutual cap tied to fees for the prior 12 months plus carve-outs for willful misconduct and IP infringement.
- Consequential damages: we still want a narrow exception for data exfiltration losses tied to breach of security obligations.
- OSS/model licensing: we need an explicit indemnity for claims arising from licensed open-source components and model licensing constraints; pass-through to sub-suppliers is acceptable but must be uncapped for IP claims.

I've attached the redlined PDF (redlined_msa_20250910.pdf). Please confirm your le...

messages.subject: Re: Nimble AI redlines - MSA (Liability / OSS)
messages.body:
Hi Sofia — thanks for sending this over and for the clear summary. Looping in Dev (legal) and Rachel (finance) — see inline below for next steps: 

- Dev: can you review Nimble's request on OSS indemnity and the pass-through/uncapped element for IP?
- Rachel: please let us know the commercial cap range that sales can accept for an enterprise of this size.

I'm available for a quick call tomorrow afternoon if that helps. Our hope is to keep the general cap structure (fees-based) but agree limited carve-outs for IP and security where appropriate.

Marissa Cole
Enterprise Accounts, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com
+1 (415) 555-0198

> On Wed, Sep 10, 2025 at 09:22 AM Sofia...

messages.subject: Re: Nimble AI redlines - MSA (Liability / OSS)
messages.body:
Team / Sofia — reviewed the redlines and drafted alternative language attached (vendor_os_clause_draft.docx). High-level positions below: 

1) Overall cap: we can accept a mutual cap tied to aggregate fees paid in the prior 12 months, with a separate carve-out for willful misconduct and gross negligence.

2) Consequential damages: Redwood's standard position is to exclude consequential damages broadly; however, we can accept a narrow exception for direct losses from proven data breach if caused by Redwood's failure to adhere to security obligations, subject to a sub-cap (e.g., 2x annual fees).

3) OSS / model licensing: we are uncomfortable with uncapped pass-through indemnity. Proposed c...

messages.subject: Re: Nimble AI redlines - MSA (Liability / OSS)
messages.body:
Dev / Marissa — thanks for the quick review. From Nimble's perspective: 

- The concern with your proposed limitation is that our use of open models and third-party OSS in the pipeline exposes us to copyright and license-compliance claims that historically require pass-through relief. Several recent incidents in our industry forced customers to seek indemnity and direct recovery from vendors.

- We are not asking for an uncapped open-ended guarantee for all upstream code — rather, for an exception to the cap specific to third-party IP infringement claims that arise directly from licensed OSS distributed as part of the model package Redwood provides. That exception should not be limited by...

messages.subject: Re: Nimble AI redlines - MSA (Liability / OSS)
messages.body:
Sofia — thanks, constructive framing. Proposal from Redwood side to avoid escalation: 

1) Liability cap: mutual cap equal to total fees paid in prior 12 months. Carve-outs: (a) willful misconduct; (b) proven IP infringement claims arising from code or model artifacts provided directly by Redwood.

2) OSS / model IP carve-out (proposed redline snippet):
"Notwithstanding the foregoing, Vendor's liability for third-party claims of intellectual property infringement arising from software or model artifacts that Vendor (a) packaged, distributed, or delivered to Customer as part of the Service, and (b) represented as being under a license that permits such use, shall not be subject to the liab...

messages.subject: Re: Nimble AI redlines - MSA (Liability / OSS)
messages.body:
Marissa / Dev — this looks good and aligns with Nimble's risk profile. We have two minor edits: 
1) Make the carve-out expressly limited to "third-party claims attributable to Vendor's distribution of a specific model or package to Customer" (to avoid broad downstream claims);
2) Clarify that "reasonable defense costs" include external counsel fees with prior approval.

Assuming those edits are acceptable, please send the updated MSA and the Order Form (we'd like the cap reflected in the Order Form value). Attached the latest order form for your reference (orderform_v3.xlsx). Once we have the updated documents, we'll route internally for signatures and push for DocuSign.

Sofia Martinez
H...

- Consequential damages: we still want a narrow exception for data exfiltration losses tied to breach of security obligations.
- OSS/model licensing: we...

Alex: send the approved template + TrustRadius link back to Priya and cc me so I can keep track of the promo. I unblocked the voucher delivery — payment/ops will handle the e-gift via the email she provides.

Ben

messages.body:
Team — looping you in. Customer: Acme Logistics (DL-9342). They report duplicated invoice lines (INV-8221, INV-8222) totaling ~$2.4k.

Actions requested:
1) Dev — can you confirm root cause and produce a token-level audit for the window 09:00–09:30 PST? We need a CSV with request_id, timestamp, token_count_raw, token_count_reconciled.
2) Rachel — please prepare a credit estimate and an authorization PDF we can send to procurement once audit confirms duplicates.
3) Jonas — FYI for escalation/exec awareness; I'll keep you posted.

Thanks — need ETA on the audit and credit timeline so I can give the customer an answer.

—Marissa
Account Exec

messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Billing anomaly — duplicate token counts during Feb 12 outage
messages.body:
Marissa, Rachel, Jonas —

Root cause summary (TL;DR): a retry-suppression race during our rollback path caused a small subset of requests to be enqueued twice when the upstream LB timed out. That resulted in duplicate generation calls and duplicate token accounting for ~0.27% of requests in the 09:02–09:18 PST window.

What we did and next steps:
- Completed token-level reconciliation for Acme's tenant (attached). The CSV lists 1,324 requests in the window; 27 were duplicate-accounted. Reconciled token deltas: -12,430 tokens total.
- Preventive fix: adjusted retry-suppression gating and added a per-request idempotency check in the router (deploy completed 12:47 PST).
- Recompute plan: bil...

messages.subject: Re: Billing anomaly — duplicate token counts during Feb 12 outage
messages.body:
Hi Samira —

Thanks again for your patience. We completed the audit and confirmed duplicate accounting on 27 requests affecting INV-8221 and INV-8222. Summary below: 

- Duplicate tokens identified: 12,430 tokens (net)
- Credit amount: $2,432.00 (applied as a one-time account credit to Acme Logistics; will appear on your next invoice)
- Files provided: reconciled_usage_20260212.csv, token_audit_20260212.csv (attached), redwood-refund-authorization.pdf

What we changed: a fix to retry-suppression logic was deployed at 12:47 PST to prevent re-enqueueing in the rollback path. We also added an idempotency check that compares request IDs at the router layer.

Next steps for you:
- Rachel (cc'd...

We're available to support the scope you described and will draft an updated SOW and a pricing matrix that includes a 30-day trial month and a 12-month renewal option with volume breakpoints. A couple quick clarifying questions before we draft:
1) For PII redaction, do you need AnnotateNow to perform automated redaction + human verification, or will Redwood supply pre-redacted datasets?
2) For the 48 hour SLA on urgent tickets, what % of overall volume do you expect to be marked urgent? (helps size a priority pool)
3) Will you want the ability to route tasks to a small dedicated team vs. a shared pool (this affects per-label pri...

messages.subject: Re: Renewal + pricing request for annotation support
messages.body:
Thanks Lisa — appreciate the quick turnaround on the pricing matrix. A few product/ops notes from our side to make the SOW actionable:

- Data handling: Redwood will deliver datasets with sensitive fields flagged. We require:
 * Triage pipeline where flagged PII is auto-redacted before human labeling unless the task explicitly requires human review.
 * Delete raw user inputs 30 days after label completion unless flagged for audit.
- QA: We need a documented adjudication workflow (two labelers + tie-breaker) and a monthly QA report with error breakdown by label type. Target pass rate >=98% for pairwise and structured labels.
- Turnaround: For production batches (non-urgent) we plan weekly ...

Attached is AnnotateNow's proposed SOW and contract for a 12-month engagement with a 30-day trial month. Key commercial points:
- Monthly committed labels: 100k (with standard overage pricing)
- Volume discounts: 5% at 200k+, 10% at 500k+
- Dedicated mini-team option: +25% on per-label price (includes named POA and a monthly sync)
- Data retention default: 90 days unless the \"30-day ephemeral\" clause is checked — we can add language for the 30-day delete in the SOW.
- SLA: standard turnaround windows included; urgent SLA is available as an addendum (we priced the 48-hour urgent pool as a 10% capacity reservation).

messages.subject: Re: Subprocessor roster and SOC 2 evidence request
messages.body:
Alex — quick clarification before you send the package: 

1) Can you confirm whether any subprocessor performs fulltext indexing or long-term analytics on customer-provided content (not just transient caching)? If yes, which ones and where do they store replicas?
2) Does Redwood employ any third-party telemetry vendors that receive request/response payloads (even for monitoring) in unredacted form?

These two items are blocking technical sign-off. If you can answer briefly in-line we can triage immediately and not wait for the full packet.

Thanks,
Sanjay

--
Quoted earlier: 
> Also call out any subprocessors that perform customer data indexing, persistent storage of unencrypted PII/PHI, ...

messages.subject: Re: Subprocessor roster and SOC 2 evidence request
messages.body:
Hi Sanjay — thanks for the follow-ups, and apologies for the slight delay. Sending the package requested below and answering the two clarifying points inline. Files are attached.

Answers to clarifying questions:
1) Fulltext indexing / long-term analytics: None of our listed subprocessors perform long-term fulltext indexing of unredacted customer content by default. We use transient indexing for internal cache/latency features, but persistent indexes that include customer content are only created if explicitly enabled by the customer. For MedCore's default configuration we will not enable persistent indexing.

2) Telemetry vendors and unredacted payloads: Redwood sends aggregated telemetr...

title: Re: PioneerHealth migration economics & shelf-credit structuring
body:

messages.subject: Pioneer migration economics — shelf credit ask
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks for taking a look at this. We're running a competitive review internally and have a near-term window to kick off a migration if we can close economics and an implementation assurance package. Two asks from procurement: 1) can Redwood match the per-token economics we were offered by FastCloud for inference (they're offering an effective 25% unit discount on the models we're using), and 2) can you provide a one-time "shelf" credit to offset migration and egress risk (our finance team wants a buffer for potential performance delta during the first 60 days).

I've attached our current_contract_summary.pdf so you have the baseline. High level usage is ~60M generated tokens/m...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Pioneer migration economics — shelf credit ask
messages.body:
Ava — thanks for the clear framing and the contract summary. Looping in Marissa (AM), Connor (solutions), and Jordan (pricing) to run the numbers and propose a concrete structure.

Quick clarifying questions while they model:
- Can you confirm the % of traffic that is latency-sensitive (low latency SLA) vs batch?
- Do you have a preferred go-live window? Procurement mentioned "near-term" — are we looking at July start or later?

We'll produce an initial econ model (attachment) with two alternatives: (A) volume-based permanent discount that matches FastCloud's net effective unit price if you commit to a 12-month reserved baseline, and (B) a smaller per-unit discount + one-time shelf credit...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
messages.subject: RE: Pioneer migration economics — shelf credit ask
messages.body:
Team — ran two pricing constructs against Ava's baseline (see attached option_matrix.csv):

Option 1 (Commit + Match):
- 12-month reserved baseline = 50M tokens/month (covers base usage)
- Effective unit price matched to FastCloud at volume tiers (requires 12-month commit)
- No shelf credit; instead we front-load an onboarding credit worth 10% of one month baseline on first invoice to offset ramping ops costs

Option 2 (Smaller Commit + Shelf Credit):
- 6-month reserved baseline = 40M tokens/month + burst capacity
- 10% per-token discount (not full match) + one-time shelf credit equal to estimated 60-day delta (~$45k) applied as invoice credit

Operational constraints / notes:
- Shelf cre...

messages.subject: Re: Pioneer migration economics — shelf credit ask
messages.body:
Thanks Marissa — that helps. A few quick clarifications from procurement and engineering:

- Procurement prefers Option 2 if the shelf credit is explicit and uncapped against implementation overruns (they want it structured as a $ credit applied within 90 days).
- Engineering wants a 4-week migration window (two weeks prep + two weeks cutover) given data validation and low-latency testing. That will increase uplift costs slightly.
- We're okay with a 6-month baseline commitment if Redwood will increase the shelf credit by 15% to account for the longer migration window.

Also, here's our HubSpot record HS-87654321 for reference. Tom from FastCloud is copied because procurement wants the ex...

cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor.obrien@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Pioneer migration economics — shelf credit ask
messages.body:
Ava / team — following up from the call (thanks all) with a recommended construct we can scope into the SOW:

Final recommended construct (per call):
- Baseline: 6-month reserved baseline at 40M tokens/month.
- Per-token pricing: 10% off list for the baseline tier; burst pricing at standard Redwood burst rates.
- Shelf credit: one-time $51,750 invoice credit (represents 75% of the 60-day delta estimate to account for the 4-week migration window) payable on first invoice after go-live. Credit is non-recurring, included in SOW, and expires 90 days post go-live if unused.
- Migration window: 4 weeks (2w prep + 2w cutover). Redwood will provide an onboarding engineer and a runbook; travel not...

body:
Alex Martinez <alex@redwood.ai>: Hi Priya — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and two asks so we can put together an accurate cost estimate: 

Summary from our side:
- BlueWave plans to embed summarization + context-aware search across ~3 product flows.
- Expected peak QPS per flow: 20, 10, and 5 respectively.
- Estimated prompt size (average user input + instructions + context) you mentioned: ~1,800 characters typical, up to 6,000 f...

Priya Shah <priya@bluewave.ai>: Thanks Alex — good follow-up. A few quick clarifications from our side: 

1) "Requests" we quoted were end-user actions (an action can be multiple API calls). For example, our summarize flow does: 1) extract, 2) summarize, 3) re-rank — so that’s up to 3 API calls per user action in current design.
2) We currently measure input size in characters, not tokens. We don't have a formal char->token conversion in ...

messages.subject: Quick recap + pricing metric Qs (tokens vs chars vs requests)
messages.body:
Hi Priya — thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and two asks so we can put together an accurate cost estimate: 

Summary from our side:
- BlueWave plans to embed summarization + context-aware search across ~3 product flows.
- Expected peak QPS per flow: 20, 10, and 5 respectively.
- Estimated prompt size (average user input + instructions + context) you mentioned: ~1,800 characters typical, up to 6,000 for heavy contexts.
- Monthly active users projected for pilot: 5k–15k.

Asks we ran through on the call and need clarified to finalize pricing: 
1) Are your volume estimates for "requests" actually API calls (i.e., HTTP requests) or end-user actions (one action can spawn multiple...

messages.subject: Re: Quick recap + pricing metric Qs (tokens vs chars vs requests)
messages.body:
Thanks Alex — good follow-up. A few quick clarifications from our side: 

1) "Requests" we quoted were end-user actions (an action can be multiple API calls). For example, our summarize flow does: 1) extract, 2) summarize, 3) re-rank — so that’s up to 3 API calls per user action in current design.
2) We currently measure input size in characters, not tokens. We don't have a formal char->token conversion in our analytics.
3) Caching: the search flow should be very cacheable (many repeated queries), summarization less so. We expect 40–60% cache hit for search; <10% for ad-hoc summarization.

Budget: for the pilot, leadership gave us a target of ~$250–400k ARR spend equivalent (they want to ...

messages.subject: Re: Quick recap + pricing metric Qs (tokens vs chars vs requests)
messages.body:
Priya — perfect, that clears up a lot. I've turned the inputs you sent into token estimates (see inline). Two things I want to be explicit about because they commonly cause confusion: 

1) Tokens vs characters vs requests:
- Our billing is by billed tokens per model invocation. Characters are only useful as a heuristic; you should convert samples to tokens to get real estimates. For English, ~4 chars/token is a rough ballpark, but prompts with lots of code/emoji/foreign text differ.
- "Requests" = HTTP calls. Each request can consume many tokens in prompt + completion + KV cache fill. We recommend counting projected API calls per user action and multiplying by average tokens per call.

2)...

messages.subject: Re: Quick recap + pricing metric Qs (tokens vs chars vs requests)
messages.body:
Priya — quick intro from me, I'll run the tech session if you'd like. A couple of practical items that engineering teams often miss when converting characters to billed tokens: 

1) Tokenization variance: run a 1k-sample through the exact tokenizer used by the serving model (I'll share a small script) — this avoids systematic under/over-estimates.
2) Prefix vs suffix caching: store large static context in KV cache (we bill KV fills once), and send only the variable suffix. That can reduce billed tokens per request by 30–70% depending on context size.
3) Batching latency vs cost tradeoff: we can configure continuous batching with latency SLOs; I can simulate the expected latency impact for...

Hi Rachel —

Thanks for the quick sync earlier. Our CIO (Amy Levin) and VP of Infrastructure (Samir Patel) would like a short, targeted demo focusing on cross-region failover and cost controls. Can we lock a 30-minute slot with both execs present next week? Prefer mornings PST. If a full 30 is hard, 20 minutes focused on the high-level ask + 10 for Q&A would work.

Any chance you can confirm availability and who from Redwood will be the exec-level owner?

Best,
Oliver
Director of Platform Partnerships
Stellarnet

messages.body:
Oliver —

Thanks — we can absolutely do a compact window. Proposed options (PST):
- Tue 05/25 10:30–11:00
- Wed 05/26 09:30–10:00
- Fri 05/28 11:00–11:30

Ben and Marissa, can you confirm Redwood coverage for the 20/30 minute format and who will join from our side? Ideally an engineering sponsor + product brief owner. I can hold the calendar once we confirm final slot.

Director, Sales Engineering | Redwood Inference

Ben

Customer: Acme Analytics (deal AC-2024-1789)
Attachments from customer: screenshot_429.png

> Hi Marissa,
> 
> We're seeing a spike of 429s hitting our production integration for /v1/generate starting ~09:05 PT (request examples attached). Our app began logging rate-limited responses and the error rate jumped to ~18% for 20...

to: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Stephanie Nguyen <stephanie.nguyen@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Unexpected 429s from morning run
messages.body:
Checked infra and rate-limiter configs. Root cause summary: per-tenant quota mapping for Acme (org: acme-123) was accidentally mapped to a shared config profile due to a recent rollout of routing policy v3.0. The shared profile had a lower per-second token rate (intended for sandbox/test tenants). When a second large customer hit steady traffic at 09:03 PT, the shared bucket hit capacity and started returning 429s for others mapped to that bucket (including Acme).

Mitigation performed (09:12–09:20 PT):
- Temporarily increased per-tenant token rate for acme-123 to restore capacity (rolled at 09:12).
- Reverted the erroneous mapping in the routing policy and re-deployed v3.0 with the corre...

messages.subject: Post-incident update — 429s earlier today (Acme Analytics)
messages.body:
Hi Julia — following up with a short technical summary and remediation plan. Thank you for the quick report and the request IDs — they helped us trace the timeline.

What happened (TL;DR): A routing-policy rollout accidentally mapped Acme's tenant id to a shared test profile with a lower per-second quota. This caused temporary cross-tenant exhaustion when another customer produced a burst. We identified the misconfiguration, increased Acme's effective token rate, and corrected the policy. Normal service resumed by ~09:23 PT.

What we did (timeline):
- 09:03 PT: Surge from another tenant observed; 429s start for the affected bucket.
- 09:12 PT: SRE applied a temporary per-tenant increase f...

messages.subject: Re: Post-incident update — 429s earlier today (Acme Analytics)
messages.body:
Thanks Marissa — that explanation makes sense and we can confirm our ingestion job recovered after 09:25 PT. The attached remediation guidance looks helpful.

A couple quick asks:
1) Can you share the postmortem PDF internally (we need that for our compliance folder)?
2) We'd like to request the one-time credit and the exact billing window you will apply it to.

If you can send the postmortem link and an estimated credit amount we can close out our internal incident ticket. We're available for a 30 minute call tomorrow at 11:00 PT if Rohan can join.

Regards,
Julia

messages.subject: Re: Post-incident update — 429s earlier today (Acme Analytics)
messages.body:
Julia — glad to hear your job recovered. Quick updates:
- Postmortem PDF: https://redwood.drive/postmortem/acme-429-20260308 (public share link attached).
- Credit: We will apply a one-time credit equivalent to the overage tokens billed during 09:00–09:30 PT on 2026-03-08; estimate ~ $1,200 — final amount will be confirmed by Billing and I’ll follow up by EOD.
- Call: 11:00 PT tomorrow works for Rohan and team. I’ll send calendar invite.

Closing note: We’ve scheduled a change to the rollout automation to add tenant-mapping validation and a canary test. I’ll keep you posted once the fix is live.

— Marissa

messages.subject: Re: Quick check — LiftOps usage & optional walkthrough
messages.body:
Hey Alex,

Thanks for the heads up — yes, we did push a new pipeline last week and saw the burst. Helpful to know you noticed. A quick walkthrough would be useful. Two quick asks up front:

- Can you show typical cold-start numbers for a 7B model (p99 tail)?
- Any notes on cost per 1M tokens with batching and KV caching enabled?

I’m copying our CTO, Miguel Santos (miguel@liftops.com), who should join if the session gets technical. We have availability next Tue/Wed afternoon PT.

Best,
Catalina Vega
Head of Product | LiftOps Inc.
Catalina@liftops.com

---
Attachments: (none)

messages.subject: Re: Quick check — LiftOps usage & optional walkthrough
messages.body:
Thanks, Catalina — great questions. Briefly:

- Cold-start: for a 7B-class open model we typically see p95 in the 120–230ms range on warm pools; cold (first token after idle) depends on pool size and batching but we can show concrete numbers during the demo.
- Cost: batching + prefix/KV cache routinely reduces per-token costs by 30–50% for interactive workloads vs naive single-request runs. I’ll bring a short slide with example math tailored to your 500k burst profile.

Proposed slots (PT):
- Tue 2025-05-13 2:00–2:20pm
- Tue 2025-05-13 3:30–3:50pm
- Wed 2025-05-14 1:00–1:20pm

Choose one here and it will send an invite: https://calendly.com/redwood-alex/20min

I attached a one-page agenda...

- a short cost calc for 1M tokens with and without KV caching/batching

1) PO issuer vs PO requester: our Coupa export includes both "PO Issuer" (finance person who signs off) and "Requester" (the business owner). For EDI/PO XML mapping, which field does Redwood need to match to generate a valid PO acknowledgement?

2) Line-level required fields: we have GL code, cost center, and insurance-category on the line items. Can Redwood accept optional line-level metadata, or does the order form/PO need to contain a minimal canonical set (e.g., sku, qty, unit_price, ship_to)?

3) Signature / acceptance flow: our procur...

messages.subject: Re: Questions on PO issuer/line-level fields + signature routing for Coupa enrollment
messages.body:
Hi Alex — thanks for the clear summary and attachment. Quick answers and next steps:

- PO issuer vs requester: for our automated PO-ack path the canonical field is the PO Issuer (the finance/accounting field). If "PO Issuer" is present in the XML we'll map that to our payment/AR metadata; the requester can map to the internal owner field.

- Line-level fields: we accept optional line-level metadata, but our orderform/fulfillment contract requires SKU, quantity, and unit_price as the minimum. Additional fields like GL code and cost center are supported as metadata but require mapping to your Coupa XML schema.

- Signature/acceptance flow: Redwood's standard is: signed MSA (DocuSign) + sys...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Questions on PO issuer/line-level fields + signature routing for Coupa enrollment
messages.body:
Rachel / team — quick read of the redline attached by Alex and guidance below.

Summary: the NovaMed redline is mostly standard, but two items need attention if we want to rely on "MSA + system PO" acceptance:

- Acceptance language: the MSA redline replaces our standard "signed order form" language with a requirement that each PO be a "signed instrument." That would technically require a signature on the PO in addition to the MSA. We can accept a one-sentence carveout that treats a system-generated PO in a recognized procurement system (Coupa/Ariba) as an "order" for purposes of acceptance provided the MSA is fully executed and the PO references the executed MSA and purchase number.

- S...

messages.body:
Thanks — that helps. A couple follow-ups:

• We can add the suggested acceptance sentence into our Coupa order template so POs reference the executed MSA automatically. That solves the PO-signature worry.

• Attestation: procurement is fine issuing a one-paragraph attestation email confirming that the PO Issuer role carries signature authority for order acceptance. I'll drop that note into procuretech-ops and have it routed to your legal contact.

• Attachments: attached is the NovaMed_PO_Field_Matrix.xlsx and a sample Coupa XML export. We also created a DocuSign envelope for the MSA; link below (DocuSign_NovaMed_MSA_link.txt contains the public URL).

• PO-ack SLA: our standard is 24 hou...

Alex — great, thanks for the matrix and the attestation path. Quick playbook and owners: 

1) Legal (Marissa): incorporate the one-line acceptance clause into the MSA redline and confirm whether an email attestation is sufficient. Target: EOD Thu 14 Aug.
2) Onboarding (Jonas): validate the Coupa XML mapping for PO Issuer + GL/cost center and return a mapping doc and sample transformed payload for Redwood devs. Target: Fri 15 Aug.
3) Rachel (me): I'll coordinate DocuSign completion and confirm the HubSpot deal update once MSA is executed.

SLA: Jonas — can we support automated PO acknowledgement within 24 business hours for the initial integration? If not, let's draft a short order-form SL...

title: Liability cap proposal — anchoring vs annual smoothing
body:
Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.inference>: Patricia —

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our discussion, I wanted to put two concrete approaches on the table for the MSA / Order Form cap so your procurement and legal teams can react quickly. Attached is our current redline with inline notes (RWI_MSA_redline_20260509.docx).

Two options we can pursue:

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.inference>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.inference>
messages.subject: RE: Liability cap proposal — anchoring vs annual smoothing
messages.body:
Team —

I reviewed ACME's annotated comments. Short framing from legal: we should aim for a commercially acceptable compromise that preserves our operational exposure management while meeting ACME procurement constraints. My recommended counter:

- Core: Annual reset cap at 1.0x annual fees (aligns to ACME policy).
- Tradeoff: Keep a small set of exclusions but tighten the language and add a specific carve-in for SLO-credit resolution.

Specifically:
1) Cap definition
 - "Cap" means the greater of: (a) total fees paid by Customer during the preceding 12 months, and (b) $100,000, per-12-month computation; resets each anniversary. (This preserves a floor for low-spend pilots.)
2) Multiplier...

cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.inference>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.inference>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.inference>
messages.subject: RE: Liability cap proposal — anchoring vs annual smoothing
messages.body:
Patricia —

Thanks for the quick turnaround and for sharing ACME's comments. Our legal team dug in and we think there's a pragmatic path that should satisfy procurement while protecting both sides. High-level counterproposal (attached as a short clause we can drop into the MSA):

- Cap: Annual reset cap equal to Fees paid by Customer during the preceding 12 months, subject to a floor of $150,000 (i.e., the greater of 12-month fees and $150k).
- No multiplier applied (1.0x).
- Exclusions from the cap limited to: (i) Redwood's indemnity obligations for third-party IP infringement; (ii) damages resulting from willful misconduct or gross negligence; (iii) legal obligations that cannot be limi...

messages.subject: Re: Liability cap proposal — anchoring vs annual smoothing
messages.body:
Rachel / Karthik —

Appreciate the compromise language and the floor explanation. Procurement and legal have reviewed: the annual reset + 1.0x approach with a floor is acceptable in principle, but they want the floor lowered to $100k for pilot accounts and a clause that the floor steps up to an enterprise-tier floor once annualized spend exceeds $2M (i.e., different floors by tier).

Legal liked the tightened exclusions and the SLO-first escalation. Please incorporate the tiered floor language and send a final redline for DocuSign. Also send the updated Order Form showing the SLO-credit table and the PO template so procurement can route a PO quickly.

If you can return the final redline b...

Jordan (ex-GrowthAI, previously Senior ML Engineer) finished on-site last week. He asked for clarity on level and comp before we send an official offer. Recruiter/KEP indicated a target base of $220k and 20% target bonus. Internal comps spreadsheet attached (20260218_offer_comps.xlsx).

Key points:
- Strong system design + shipping roadmap for inference optimizations.
- Has IC leadership and led cross...

markus_klein@redwood.ai: Lauren —

Short answer: lean L6. Long answer: Jordan demonstrated cross-cutting impact in the on-site — the production-readiness approach, ownership of latency regressions at GrowthAI, and the architectural proposal for persistent KV caching are exactly the problems we need at Staff level on the Inference Runtime team. He hasn't managed direct reports, but Staff is commonly used for high-impact ICs here.

- Strong stakeholder management with infra and platform teams.

Given the scope and the autonomy he demonstrated, I prefer L6. He will be the technical lead on the prefix caching initiative and will influence runtime kernel selection. No direct reports required for success.

Highlights: 12-month term starting 2026-01-01, same usage bands as current year, list price $0.012 per enriched record, 5% annual escalation for multi-year options.

We can offer a 10% partner discount if you commit to an annual prepay. The DocuSign is ready if the terms look good.

Quick housekeeping: we’ve included an export clause and will retain only hashed identifiers for troubleshooting by default. If you have specific data residency or deletion requirements, let us know and I’ll pull in our...

cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Proposed 2026 renewal for ClearLens Enrichment - Redwood Inference
messages.body:
Sara — thanks for the proposal and the heads-up. A few clarifying questions before we loop legal/procurement: 

- Usage tiers: what are the overage rates and are they billable monthly or quarterly?
- Data retention/export: can ClearLens agree to a 30-day deletion window on PII-derived attributes upon request (we hash identifiers in transit but need deletion guarantees for regulated customers)?
- SLA: current SLA is fine but we need a 99.9% uptime commitment for our dedicated routes (we sometimes serve low-latency enrichment in production).

I’ve copied Laura and Rachel for budget/PO coordination and Monica for contract redlines. If you can share your standard redlineable SLA and a DocuSig...

messages.subject: Re: Proposed 2026 renewal for ClearLens Enrichment - Redwood Inference
messages.body:
Looping in a quick note: our expected budget for third-party enrichment next year is flat to slightly up (+8%) but we’d prefer to lock in price if possible. If ClearLens can move to a committed usage discount (12% off list for a 12-month commit) that keeps this within current spend and avoids heavy quarterly spikes.

Also asking for a PO process timeline — Rachel, can you confirm typical procurement lead time?

-Laura

messages.subject: Re: Proposed 2026 renewal for ClearLens Enrichment - Redwood Inference
messages.body:
Karthik / Laura / team — thanks for the quick review. We can do the following adjustments to get this over the line: 

- Move to a committed 12-month prepay at 12% off list (matches your ask).
- Overage: $0.009 per extra record billed monthly.
- SLA: we can update to 99.9% for dedicated routes and include a credits table.
- Data deletion: we’ll accept a 30-day deletion window for derived attributes if the request comes from the account owner and is accompanied by the account ID.

I’ve attached an updated SLA redline from our contracts team and seeded the DocuSign envelope: https://docusign.com/clearlens/abc123. Let me know if legal needs different language and we’ll get a revised doc out ...

cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Proposed 2026 renewal for ClearLens Enrichment - Redwood Inference
messages.body:
Sara / ClearLens contracts team — thanks for the updated SLA. A few redlines from legal: 

1) Limit the export of hashed identifiers to troubleshooting only; require an access justification log for any human review.
2) Add a Customer Data Deletion clause that includes a certification step once deletion is completed.
3) Clarify indemnity carve-outs for data breaches caused by ClearLens negligence (standard market language fine).

I attached our tracked changes (ClearLens_SLA_Redline_20251110.docx) with suggested language. If you accept these redlines I can route approval internally. We also need the DocuSign to include a signature block for Redwood’s Head of Procurement (Laura) and CTO (Ma...

cc: Laura Bennett <laura_bennett@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel_kim@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
messages.subject: Re: Proposed 2026 renewal for ClearLens Enrichment - Redwood Inference
messages.body:
Sara — appreciate the fast turnaround from you and the contracts team. We’re aligned on the commercial terms (12% off committed prepay; $0.009 overage; 12-month term). Monica’s redlines are acceptable to us with one small tweak to the customer-deletion certification window (we asked for certification within 10 business days of deletion). If ClearLens can accept that minor change, we’ll sign the DocuSign and raise PO-REDWOOD-4578 against the envelope.

Rachel — please prepare the PO and confirm expected payment terms (Net 30 preferred). Laura / Marissa — if you’re good on budget and final signatories, I’ll execute on the DocuSign once we see the revised certification language.

Thanks all ...

messages.subject: Dedicated capacity — hybrid commit question
messages.body:
Hi Rachel,

Thanks again for the demo yesterday. A couple of follow-ups from our infra and procurement teams: we like the idea of reserving capacity per-region but we have seasonal spikes where APAC and EU will need to burst into US pools for 2–6 hour windows.

Questions: would Redwood support a hybrid commit where we have reserved minimum pools in EU/APAC and a shared US pool we can burst into with pre-negotiated credits? Also: what are the throughput guarantees by pool, and can we get headroom credits for DR drills?

Procurement will also need a rough TCV and an outline of how credits/overage are billed so we can model cost for our Q4 ramp. Attached is a sample traffic profile.

Can you...

messages.subject: Re: Dedicated capacity — hybrid commit question
messages.body:
Hi Sanjay — great working through this with you.

Short answers up front: yes, we can structure a hybrid commit that combines per-region reserved pools (EU/APAC minimums) with a cross-region shared bursting pool in US for short windows.

High-level mechanics we'll propose:
- Reserved minimums per region: guaranteed GPU quantity and baseline throughput SLA (tokens/sec aggregate per pool).
- Cross-region bursting: pre-authorized credit bucket that can be consumed when traffic exceeds regional reserved capacity; billing against committed credits first, then overage rate.
- DR drills / headroom credits: we can include an annual allotment of DR credits for failover testing (non-production dril...

to: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Fwd: Acme hybrid commit — quick internal alignment (sizing + discount asks)
messages.body:
Team — forwarding Acme's profile and initial ask. Key asks: separate minimum pools in EU/APAC; shared US bursting pool; headroom credits for DR drills; they want TCV for Q4 ramp.

Notes / proposed approach:
1) Sizing: based on their attached CSV, I estimate ~60 steady GPUs across EU/APAC combined for baseline (split 35 EU / 25 APAC), with a 15–20 GPU shared US burst pool for 3–6 hr windows at 95th p95 traffic. Ben — can you validate if continuous batching + kv-cache will change GPU count assumptions?
2) Billing mechanics: propose a two-layer commit: 1) regional reserved (monthly commit, smaller discount tier) + 2) cross-region bursting credits purchased up-front at a higher per-credit dis...

to: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.ai>
messages.subject: Re: Fwd: Acme hybrid commit — quick internal alignment (sizing + discount asks)
messages.body:
Rachel / team — ran a quick pass on the traffic CSV and assumptions. Quick findings:

- With continuous batching and prefix caching we can compress their 95th-percentile peak profile by ~18–22% token-wise, which reduces required GPUs in the shared burst pool from 15–20 to ~12–16 depending on model selection and sequence-length distribution.
- If they opt for a single-architecture quantized variant for burst traffic (faster kernels), we can reduce burst GPU count further ~2 GPUs but that requires model onboarding + verification (2–3 week pipeline).
- Active-active replication across regions will incur a replication premium: approx 6% on top of committed pool fees (covers cross-region state...

Hi Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week — team was impressed. We want to move to a 2-day POC kickoff and hands-on session the week of Mar 23. Main goals: review prompt sets, validate routing behavior with our test traffic, and run some short load checks on representative flows.

Two logistics questions up front:
- We prefer onsite for the first day (deep pairing) and virtual for day two (wrap-up + follow-ups). Can Redwood support that hybrid format?
- If onsite, we need visitor access instructions and any security forms for laptops.

Proposed attendees from our side: Tim (platform lead), myself (PM), and our facilities coordinator (looped on this email).

Can you propose a detail...

cc: Tim Smith <tim.smith@stellarpayments.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel@redwood.ai>, Facilities <facilities@stellarpayments.com>
messages.subject: Re: POC kickoff — hands-on workshop options and logistics
messages.body:
Hi Julia — great to hear, and yes we can support a hybrid format. High level we typically bring: 1x Solutions Engineer for pairing, 1x Infra lead for load tests (remote), and a kickoff deck + detailed agenda.

Quick proposal:
Day 1 (onsite, 9:00–17:00): kickoff, prompt review workshop, routing deep-dive, small-scale live run-throughs.
Day 2 (virtual, 10:00–14:00): load tests, results review, next-step planning.

normalized.subject: MSA: Term, Auto-Renew & TfC - redline coordination

messages.subject: MSA - Term & Renewal Questions
messages.body:
Alex —

Thanks for sending the draft MSA. Procurement asked me to flag three items that are blocking our legal review: 1) the auto-renew clause (Section 2.3) — we prefer opt-in renewals or a 60-day written notice rather than automatic renewal; 2) termination-for-convenience (Section 8) — can you confirm the wind-down obligations and whether there will be any refunds for pre-paid committed capacity; and 3) notice windows for material changes — we need at least 45 days.

I attached a redline (ACME_MSA_redlines_v2.docx) with our suggested edits and comments. Happy to jump on a short call if that helps.

Best,
Vivian Lee
Senior Contract Manager
ACME Corp

Attachment: ACME_MSA_redlines_v2.docx...

messages.subject: Re: MSA - Term & Renewal Questions
messages.body:
Vivian —

Thanks — got the redline and appreciate the clear bullets. Looping in Kimberly (legal) and Marissa (sales ops) to triage. High-level thoughts from our side:

- Auto-renew: We typically support auto-renew with a notice window; open to shortening to 60 days for mid-market accounts.
- TfC (Termination for Convenience): We can offer a wind-down period and prorated refunds for unused pre-paid credits for pilots but for reserved capacity there are constraints we need to surface.
- Material change notice: We aim for 30-60 day windows depending on region and product tier.

I'll let Kimberly propose specific language. In the meantime I attached a brief summary doc (Term_Window_Summary.pd...

messages.subject: Re: MSA - Term & Renewal Questions
messages.body:
Alex / team —

Reviewing ACME's redline. Below are proposed positions and draft language we should offer back. I'm keeping it pragmatic — preserve Redwood's ability to operate while addressing ACME's concerns.

1) Auto-Renew (Section 2.3)
- Position: Keep auto-renew but shorten the notice window to 60 days for enterprise deals; for pilots less than $50k TCV, allow opt-in renewals.
- Draft: "This Agreement shall automatically renew for successive one-year terms unless either party provides written notice of non-renewal at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current term. Notwithstanding the foregoing, pilot agreements (defined as commitments with total consideration less tha...

messages.subject: Proposed compromise: notice windows & TfC language
messages.body:
Vivian —

Thanks for your patience while we reviewed ACME's redlines. Per Kim's suggestions above, Redwood is prepared to propose the following compromise: 

- Auto-renew: Auto-renew remains in the MSA with a 60-day cancellation notice for enterprise deals; pilots under $50k will require mutual renewal.
- Notice for Material Changes: We agree to a 45-day notice for specified "Material Changes" with a narrow definition included in Exhibit A.
- Termination for Convenience: 60-day wind-down; pro rata refund for unused prepaid credits for pilots/self-serve; termination fee for dedicated/reserved capacity as specified in the Order Form.

I've attached our marked counter with the exact redline ...

Thanks again for the call this morning. Per our conversation, I wanted to put a lightweight proposal on timeline and roles for incident notices that involve subprocessors and cross-border handover. This is intended to make sure your legal and forensics teams have a practical playbook we can operate against (not contractual language yet).

High-level proposal (attached: redwood_dpa_incident_clause_propo...

Marta Ruiz <marta@healthcorps.io>: Thanks Ava — this is helpful and matches the tone we want. A few clarifying Qs from our side before we loop in internal counsel:

1) For the 6-hour triage notice: do you mean 'detection' by your monitoring, or 'confirmation' after you validate exposure? Our legal team generally expects a notification window that starts on confirmation.
2) For subprocessor handback: what level of PII mapping will you prov...
